[ANSWER]: Unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA) seeks to transfer knowledge from a labeled source domain to an unlabeled target domain by learning representations that are robust to distribution shift. Early theoretical work showed that domain‑invariant features can make a source‑trained classifier applicable to the target domain, and a large body of methods operationalizes this idea either by minimizing statistical divergences (e.g., MMD‑based approaches) or by employing domain‑adversarial training. The latter has become the dominant paradigm because adversarial objectives can directly reduce the discrepancy between source and target feature distributions. Our method builds on this successful framework: we retain the adversarial discriminator‑generator interaction but augment it with a task‑oriented mechanism that explicitly directs the alignment toward features beneficial for classification, thereby enhancing the effectiveness of any existing adversarial UDA backbone without increasing inference cost.

A second line of research refines alignment by exploiting class‑level information or by selecting particular regions or objects that are presumed to be transferable. Conditional domain adversarial networks condition the discriminator on predicted class scores, while multi‑discriminator designs such as MADA perform class‑wise alignment. More recent works select local regions based on discriminator uncertainty or focus on object centerness to mitigate background noise. Although these strategies move beyond naïve global alignment, they still treat the alignment objective as separate from the classification goal and lack a principled way to decide which sub‑features should be aligned. In contrast, our approach is the first to decompose each source feature into a task‑discriminative component and a task‑irrelevant component using meta‑knowledge derived from the classification task itself, and to align target features only with the discriminative component. This explicit decomposition provides a clear, interpretable criterion for feature selection that is directly tied to improving classification performance.

A third strand of literature addresses the tension between alignment and classification, noting that parallel optimization can cause the two objectives to conflict and lead to loss of discriminative information. Some works introduce restoration modules or employ meta‑learning to reconcile the gradients of alignment and classification, while others decouple features into domain‑invariant and domain‑specific parts for tasks such as person re‑identification. These methods either mitigate the conflict post‑hoc or operate at the domain level rather than the task level. Our contribution differs fundamentally: we propose a task‑oriented alignment that proactively guides the alignment process using classification‑driven sub‑features, thereby preventing the incorporation of task‑irrelevant information from the outset. By integrating this decomposition into the adversarial training loop, we achieve more coherent optimization of alignment and classification, leading to state‑of‑the‑art performance across multiple benchmark settings.

[ANSWER]: Traditional graph tasks such as link prediction [1], knowledge base completion [2], and basic one‑hop query answering are essentially variants of a single‑variable inference problem: determining whether a triple (h, r, t) exists in a knowledge graph (KG) or whether an entity t satisfies a query of the form (h, r, ?). These tasks assume that only one element of the triple is missing.  

Knowledge graph reasoning extends this setting to queries that may involve multiple missing edges or nodes in large, incomplete KGs. Such queries can be expressed as path queries, conjunctive queries, disjunctive queries, or combinations thereof, and require inference over multiple hops and logical operators. Formal treatments of KG reasoning define a KG as a set of entities 𝔈, a set of relations ℛ, and a set of triples 𝔗 ⊆ 𝔈 × ℛ × 𝔈, with reasoning tasks aiming to predict triples absent from the original graph 𝔗 [3].  

Embedding‑based approaches have been proposed to answer multi‑hop queries on KGs. Methods such as those in [4]–[7] learn vector representations that support traversal and composition over several hops, enabling the answering of conjunctive and path‑based queries. Related work on question answering over KGs also leverages embeddings to map natural‑language questions to logical forms that can be executed on the graph [8, 9].  

Beyond embeddings, a variety of techniques address KG reasoning. Attention mechanisms have been employed to weight relevant parts of the graph during inference [11]. Reinforcement‑learning frameworks treat query answering as a sequential decision process, training agents to navigate the graph toward answers; notable examples include the approaches in [10, 12] and the DIVA model [13]. Neural logic networks integrate logical operators directly into neural architectures, learning to approximate logical inference through differentiable modules [14, 15].  

Collectively, these lines of work explore different aspects of KG reasoning, ranging from representation learning and neural operator design to search‑based and logic‑guided inference strategies.

[ANSWER]: Video captioning has progressed from early approaches that simply aggregated frame‑level features by mean pooling and applied image‑captioning pipelines to more sophisticated models that incorporate recurrent neural networks and attention mechanisms. While mean‑pooling works for short clips, it degrades on longer, untrimmed videos, prompting the adoption of RNN‑based encoders such as S2VT, which offer greater flexibility and efficiency. To capture richer temporal dynamics, subsequent work extended single‑sentence generation to multi‑sentence or paragraph‑level descriptions, enabling the narration of longer events. Some recent methods have explored weakly supervised learning to produce diverse captions linked to different spatial regions, emphasizing the need for models that can associate linguistic output with distinct visual components.

Dense event captioning (DEC) builds on the “detect‑and‑describe” paradigm, requiring both temporal localization of events and generation of corresponding captions. Early DEC systems employed proposal generators like DAP for event detection and enriched the captioning stage with context‑aware S2VT models. Other approaches have leveraged grouping strategies based on video‑highlight detectors and attribute‑augmented LSTMs to improve both proposal quality and linguistic expressiveness. More recent studies have investigated joint optimization of proposals and sentences, using generated captions to refine event proposals or replacing DAP with bidirectional segment‑selection techniques and bidirectional attention mechanisms. These works typically rely on fully supervised training with explicit temporal segment annotations and often use one‑to‑many mapping detectors to retrieve multiple events simultaneously.

Sentence (or moment) localization in videos focuses on aligning a natural‑language query with the correct temporal segment. Early efforts were limited to constrained domains such as kitchen environments, but deep‑learning‑driven models have broadened applicability to open‑world videos. A common two‑stage “scan‑and‑localize” framework first enumerates candidate clips and then matches them to the query; examples include Moment Context Networks that score clip‑sentence pairs and Cross‑modal Temporal Regression Localizers that reduce computation by using coarsely sampled clips. An alternative direction directly regresses temporal boundaries from fused video and sentence representations, bypassing explicit proposal generation. These methods generally assume access to paired video‑sentence data with known temporal extents during training.

In contrast to prior fully supervised DEC and moment‑localization approaches, this work introduces weakly supervised dense event captioning, which eliminates the need for temporal segment annotations during training. By assuming a one‑to‑one correspondence between captions and event segments, the problem is decomposed into dual tasks of caption generation and sentence localization. A cyclic training scheme leverages reconstruction loss to iteratively refine both modules, enabling the model to learn segment–caption alignments without ground‑truth boundaries. At inference time, candidate segments are evaluated by the caption generator and mapped back to the temporal domain, producing coherent event detections and descriptions. The contributions are threefold: (1) formulation of a weakly supervised dense event captioning task that substantially reduces annotation cost and facilitates exploitation of large‑scale web videos; (2) development of an efficient cycle‑based framework that exploits the presumed one‑to‑one mapping to jointly train captioning and localization components; and (3) empirical validation on the ActivityNet Captions benchmark, demonstrating competitive dense captioning performance and accurate sentence localization despite the absence of explicit temporal supervision. This positions the proposed approach as a bridge between fully supervised dense captioning and weakly supervised video‑language grounding, advancing the ability to learn rich video narratives from minimally annotated data.

[ANSWER]: In several machine learning settings, negative samples are produced from a statistical generative model. [1] generate negative data using GANs for semi‑supervised learning and novelty detection. [2] propose a theoretical framework that relies on an oracle classifying a sample as valid or not, without providing a practical implementation. [3] use adversarial training to generate hard negatives that fool the discriminator for NLP tasks. [4] employ a GAN to learn the negative‑data distribution with the aim of classifying positive‑unlabeled data.  

In contrastive unsupervised learning, common negative examples are assumed to be semantically farther from the positive samples. Word2Vec [5] treats negative samples as coming from a different context, while CPC‑based methods [6] such as momentum contrast [7] use data augmentations from different images as negatives. Negative data augmentation (NDA) has been introduced as a way to obtain negative samples by transforming existing samples, thereby incorporating useful inductive biases that may be difficult to capture otherwise [8].

[ANSWER]: Traditional graph‑related tasks such as link prediction, knowledge‑base completion, or single‑hop query answering can be formalised as a simple existence test for a triple (h, r, t) or a query with a single missing variable. While these problems have driven much of the early KG literature, they do not capture the compositional complexity of multi‑hop reasoning, where a query may involve several unobserved edges, conjunctions, disjunctions, or negations over large, incomplete graphs. Our work moves beyond these basic settings by targeting the full spectrum of First‑Order Logic (FOL) queries, thereby addressing a substantially harder reasoning problem that requires modelling sets of entities and logical operators in a unified embedding space.  

Embedding‑based approaches for multi‑hop KG reasoning, notably Graph Query Embeddings (GQE) [4], Query2Box (Q2B) [5, 6], and BetaE [7], have demonstrated that representing queries and entities as vectors enables efficient answer retrieval via nearest‑neighbor search. However, these methods are limited either to existential‑positive query fragments (GQE, Q2B) or to a restricted set of logical operators (BetaE handles negation but relies on handcrafted operator designs). In contrast, our models embed both queries and entities as single‑point vectors and learn **all** logical operators—including projection, conjunction, disjunction, and negation—through flexible neural‑network modules. This design not only broadens the class of admissible queries to the entire FOL language but also yields a more compact representation that improves scalability and training efficiency.  

Beyond embedding techniques, several families of KG reasoning methods have explored alternative mechanisms such as attention‑based aggregators [11], reinforcement‑learning agents [10, 12, 13], and neural‑logic networks [14, 15]. While these approaches offer valuable insights, they typically involve complex search procedures, require extensive supervision, or impose architectural constraints that limit operator generality. By contrast, our neural‑network‑driven operator framework provides a **single, end‑to‑end trainable architecture** that can be instantiated for any logical connective without redesigning the underlying model. Empirically, this simplicity translates into a consistent 5 %–10 % relative gain over the strongest embedding baselines (GQE, Q2B, BetaE) and a 30 %–40 % improvement over the original single‑point embedding method, confirming that our contribution lies both in methodological versatility and in state‑of‑the‑art performance.  

Finally, the ability to process **negative queries** has remained a central open challenge in KG reasoning. BetaE was the first to introduce a learned negation operator, yet its formulation is tightly coupled to a specific box‑embedding geometry. Our approach leverages the expressive power of neural networks to construct a negation module that operates directly on the same single‑point vector space used for all other operators. This unified treatment not only simplifies the overall system but also achieves superior empirical results on benchmarks containing negated queries, thereby establishing a new standard for handling full FOL reasoning on knowledge graphs.

[ANSWER]: CNNs have long served as the default backbone for visual recognition, with architectures such as VGG, ResNet, and DenseNet driving advances in both image and video tasks. More recently, Vision Transformers (ViT) and their video‑oriented extensions have demonstrated that self‑attention can surpass convolutional designs when sufficient data are available. While these transformer‑based models achieve strong performance, they typically rely on massive image‑level pre‑training and are trained on a single video dataset. In contrast, our approach leverages the expressive power of transformers but introduces two novel regularizers—informative loss and projection loss—that enable a single model to learn robust, dataset‑agnostic representations without requiring external large‑scale image pre‑training. This positions our work as the first to incorporate principled representation regularization into multi‑dataset video action learning.  

Action‑recognition research has progressed hand‑in‑hand with the growth of large‑scale video benchmarks such as Kinetics‑400/700, Moments‑in‑Time, ActivityNet, and Something‑Something‑v2. State‑of‑the‑art methods (e.g., SlowFast, MViT) generally improve accuracy by scaling model capacity or by pre‑training on massive image corpora, yet they remain tied to a single dataset and inherit its specific biases. Our method departs from this single‑dataset paradigm: by jointly training on five diverse action collections and explicitly modeling inter‑class relations across datasets through the projection loss, we obtain a unified model that consistently outperforms these specialized approaches while incurring negligible extra computation.  

Multi‑dataset co‑training has been explored primarily in the image domain for detection and segmentation, and more recently for video by combining several action datasets, often together with additional image data, multimodal signals, or multi‑stage training pipelines (e.g., OmniSource, VATT, CoVeR, PolyViT). These strategies improve data diversity but increase system complexity and depend on resources that are not universally accessible. Our contribution is a streamlined multi‑dataset training framework that requires no auxiliary image collections, no extra modalities, and no dataset‑specific sampling schedules. The learned weighting of loss terms eliminates manual hyper‑parameter tuning, offering a simple yet effective alternative to the elaborate co‑training pipelines of prior work.  

Video domain generalization seeks models that transfer to unseen target domains, typically by learning domain‑invariant features or by augmenting training with synthetic shifts. Although related, VDG tackles a different problem: achieving strong performance on novel, out‑of‑distribution videos. Our objective is to train a single model that simultaneously excels on multiple known action datasets, thereby addressing parameter inefficiency and dataset bias without the need for separate models per domain. By focusing on joint optimization across related tasks and introducing the informative and projection losses, we provide a more parameter‑efficient solution that complements, rather than replaces, existing VDG techniques.

[ANSWER]: Sequence‑to‑sequence problems naturally give rise to symmetric tasks in opposite directions, a source‑to‑target mapping and a target‑to‑source mapping. Several studies capture this bidirectionality as a constraint to improve sequence‑to‑sequence tasks such as machine translation. Dual learning leverages reinforcement learning to achieve interaction between two separate directional translation models, while partially model‑level dual learning shares components of similar underlying functionality for forward and reverse tasks. A variational probabilistic framework has been proposed to model the two directional translation models with language models. These approaches typically set up two separate simplex models to consider task bidirectionality.

Other work unifies two directional tasks in a multilingual fashion by sharing the same computational process of a single simplex model, which requires the model capacity to be split between encoding and decoding multiple languages. 

Non‑autoregressive translation (NAT) models have attracted significant research interest due to the inefficiency of traditional autoregressive seq2seq models. Fully NAT models generate sequences in parallel in a single shot but often sacrifice performance. Semi‑autoregressive models improve performance by performing iterative refinement of translations based on previous predictions.

Reversible neural networks have been proposed for various purposes and based on different architectures. Reversible networks help model flexible probability distributions with tractable likelihoods by defining a mapping between a simple known density and a more complex target density. Reversibility also enables memory‑efficient algorithms. The reversible residual network (RevNet) modifies the residual network for image classification, allowing activations at any layer to be recovered from the following layer, which reduces memory consumption during back‑propagation. Extensions of RevNet have been applied to recurrent neural networks and Transformers for natural language processing. Reversible generative adversarial networks have been introduced for image‑to‑image translation, sharing the intuition of using reversible mappings in vision tasks.

[ANSWER]: The literature on the combinatorial geometry of ReLU networks has highlighted the relevance of activation regions and tropical geometry, but it has not linked these ideas to the data‑driven zonotope structure that underlies the global optimization problem. Prior work such as [1] studies the partition of input space induced by a fixed network and derives a tropical representation of activation regions. While we can recover a zonotope description for a single ReLU unit by swapping the roles of weights and data in their framework, our contribution is to show that, when the training examples are treated as generators, the resulting zonotope captures the entire set of feasible activation patterns for a shallow network. This perspective is new and enables us to analyze the combinatorial search over vertices directly, something that was not addressed in the earlier tropical‑geometric analyses.

A line of research has used Hausdorff distances between zonotopes to bound approximation errors of shallow ReLU networks [2] and to guide optimization procedures [3]. These works treat zonotopes as objects associated with the network’s parameters, whereas our approach treats the zonotope generated by the training data as a fixed polyhedral object whose vertices correspond bijectively to activation‑pattern subproblems. By establishing this one‑to‑one correspondence, we provide a concrete bridge between geometric distances and the combinatorial complexity of the underlying optimization, which was missing from the earlier distance‑based analyses.

The NP‑hardness of training shallow ReLU networks was first proved in [4] and subsequently strengthened in [5,6], establishing that even approximate solutions cannot be avoided in the worst case. These results, however, rely on constructions that do not respect the generic position of real‑world data. Building on the NP‑hardness reductions of [4,5], we prove that the loss surface can exhibit discontinuous jumps under arbitrarily small perturbations of a dataset in general position, thereby exposing a limitation of the existing hardness proofs. Moreover, we modify the reduction to operate on data in general position, showing that the NP‑hardness claim remains valid under realistic assumptions—a refinement that directly addresses the gap left by the earlier works.

Overparameterization results have demonstrated that gradient descent can find global minima in polynomial time when the network is sufficiently wide. The pioneering analysis in [7] introduced a width bound of  (where  is the number of hidden units), which was subsequently improved by [8,9] and most recently tightened to  in [10]. These works interpret overparameterization as a way to “smooth out’’ the nonconvex landscape, but they do not explicitly relate the phenomenon to a combinatorial search over activation patterns. Our work provides this missing link by showing that overparameterization makes a randomly chosen zonotope vertex likely to be close to a good solution, and we complement the existing bounds with a novel polynomial‑time algorithm that selects a vertex guaranteeing the global optimum using only about twice the minimal number of hidden units. This algorithm bridges the gap between purely statistical overparameterization guarantees and explicit combinatorial constructions.

Convex‑regularized formulations for global ReLU optimization have been proposed in [11,12], where a single convex program simultaneously handles all activation patterns of one unit, and multiple units are incorporated by summation. While these approaches avoid exponential dependence on the number of units, they retain exponential dependence on the data dimension, limiting scalability. Our zonotope framework circumvents this limitation by encoding the exponential set of activation patterns as vertices of a low‑dimensional polytope, enabling us to design algorithms whose complexity depends only on the number of vertices explored rather than on the ambient data dimension. Consequently, we achieve a more favorable trade‑off between expressive power and computational cost.

Finally, heuristic searches over activation patterns have been explored in [13], which prioritize examples with large positive labels when constructing the active set for a single unit. This heuristic operates without a principled geometric foundation. In contrast, we introduce a greedy local‑search algorithm that moves along edges of the data‑generated zonotope, systematically exploring neighboring activation patterns. Empirically we demonstrate that this method consistently outperforms standard gradient descent on under‑parameterized synthetic and simplified real‑world tasks, providing both a theoretical justification (via the zonotope edge structure) and practical performance gains over earlier heuristics.

[ANSWER]: Recent theoretical work has sought to delineate the expressive power and limitations of self‑attention architectures.  Hard‑attention Transformers, even when equipped with positional encodings, have been shown to be unable to model the Dyck language with unbounded nesting depth, while soft‑attention models that satisfy a bounded Lipschitz condition cannot achieve perfect cross‑entropy on the same language.  Variants that incorporate positional masking but omit explicit positional encodings are capable of solving certain regular languages yet still fail on more complex hierarchical patterns.  Despite these negative results, empirical studies report that Transformers can learn to approximate hierarchical dependencies from finite data and often outperform LSTM baselines on syntactic benchmarks, highlighting a tension between worst‑case theoretical limitations and observed practical performance.

A parallel strand of research has examined recurrent neural networks and their ability to process hierarchical structures.  With infinite‑precision arithmetic or the addition of external memory modules, RNNs and LSTMs can recognise context‑free languages such as the classic Dyck language.  In the absence of such assumptions, however, formal analyses demonstrate that standard recurrent models cannot handle unbounded nesting, a finding corroborated by experimental work showing poor generalisation of RNN‑based systems on deeply nested or long‑range dependency tasks.  Extensions that augment recurrent units with explicit memory buffers have been proposed to overcome these deficits, underscoring the importance of auxiliary storage for hierarchical computation in sequential models.

The broader NLP community has debated whether the non‑recurrent nature of self‑attention inherently restricts its capacity to capture hierarchical structure.  Consequently, several proposals have enriched Transformers with recurrence‑like mechanisms, aiming to combine the parallelism of attention with the depth‑sensitive processing of recurrent models.  At the same time, analyses of pretrained Transformers reveal that they encode rich syntactic and word‑order information and achieve state‑of‑the‑art results on dependency parsing and related syntactic tasks.  Nevertheless, on linguistically motivated evaluations such as English subject‑verb agreement, recurrent architectures have sometimes been reported to surpass attention‑only models, suggesting that the trade‑offs between distributed and recurrent processing remain an open question.

In this work we bridge the theoretical‑empirical gap by proving that self‑attention networks can exactly recognise and generate the bounded‑depth Dyck language $\mathsf{Dyck}_{k,D}$, which captures the finite hierarchical depth observed in natural language.  We construct a hard‑attention Transformer with $D\!+\!1$ layers whose per‑token, per‑layer memory grows only as $O(\log k)$, and a soft‑attention Transformer with two layers that attains the same $O(\log k)$ memory bound while using a simple depth‑counting mechanism.  Our analysis identifies precise requirements on positional encodings: they must provide $\Theta(\log n)$ bits for an input of length $n$ and support efficient position comparison.  Empirically, we show that monotonic scalar positional encodings satisfy these conditions and enable near‑perfect generalisation to inputs far longer than those seen during training, whereas standard sinusoidal or learned encodings degrade rapidly.  Further experiments confirm that the proposed attention‑based models require substantially less memory than comparable LSTMs to achieve high accuracy on hierarchical tasks, thereby reconciling earlier impossibility results with the practical success of Transformers on natural‑language syntax.  Together, these contributions clarify the circumstances under which self‑attention can faithfully model bounded hierarchical structure, elucidate the role of positional information, and quantify the memory advantages of attention over recurrence.

[ANSWER]: Fair resource allocation via utility maximization has a long tradition in economics, mechanism design, and network management.  Classical works such as Palomar & Fischer (2006) and Eryilmaz & Srikant (2007) formulate fairness as the maximization of a concave utility function, but they assume a discrete‑time setting in which each action consumes a unit of time and the statistics of rewards are either known a priori or can be estimated offline.  Consequently, these models ignore the random, action‑dependent completion times that arise in many real‑world platforms (e.g., contractual hiring or cloud‑computing).  By contrast, our paper introduces a continuous‑time utility‑maximization framework that explicitly models stochastic completion times, incorporates deadline constraints, and operates without prior knowledge of reward or time distributions.  This positions our work as the first to unify fairness criteria (proportional, max‑min, reward‑maximizing) with time‑efficient decision making in a setting where both rewards and durations must be learned online.

The only existing continuous‑time treatment of fair allocation is Neely (2012), which develops an offline stochastic‑control solution under the assumption that first‑order statistics of the underlying processes are known.  While Neely’s approach provides valuable insights into the structure of optimal policies, it does not address the algorithmic challenges of learning these statistics or of delivering finite‑time performance guarantees.  Building on Neely’s formulation, we derive a tractable approximation to the optimal offline policy using Lagrange duality and renewal theory, and we prove that this approximation is asymptotically optimal.  Moreover, we extend the offline results to the online setting by designing a dual‑ascent algorithm that achieves a $\tilde O(B^{-1/2})$ regret bound, thereby advancing the state of the art from purely offline analysis to provably efficient online learning.

Lyapunov‑drift and drift‑plus‑penalty methods have become standard tools for dynamic optimization in queueing and wireless networks (e.g., Neely 2006, Eryilmaz & Srikant 2009).  Existing applications typically assume that random quantities (such as channel states or service times) are observed before the control decision, or that their first‑ and second‑order moments are known.  These “opportunistic” assumptions are violated in our target applications, where the controller must decide on a worker and a deadline before the actual completion time is realized, and where the statistical model must be learned.  Our work is the first to integrate Lyapunov‑drift analysis with online learning of unknown completion‑time and reward distributions, while simultaneously enforcing fairness through a general utility function and improving time‑efficiency via controlled deadlines.

The bandits‑with‑knapsacks (BwK) literature addresses online learning under resource constraints, focusing on maximizing expected total reward while consuming a stochastic budget (Badanidiyuru & Kleinberg 2018; Tran‑Thanh et al. 2012).  Extensions that incorporate continuous‑time dynamics (Cayci et al. 2019) or concave rewards with convex constraints (Agrawal & De Rotto 2014) still prioritize reward maximization and either ignore fairness considerations or lack explicit deadline mechanisms.  In contrast, our framework augments the BwK setting with a fairness‑oriented utility function, action‑dependent random completion times, and hard deadlines that guarantee time‑efficient budget usage.  The resulting algorithm retains low computational complexity while delivering regret guarantees that are comparable to, and in some regimes superior to, those of existing BwK approaches.

Finally, recent works on online convex optimization with constraints (e.g., Zhou & Jiang 2020) achieve vanishing constraint violation by solving linear programs at each round, but they do not enforce strict time budgets, nor do they model the interplay between random service durations and fairness.  By contrast, our contribution lies in a unified treatment of (i) continuous‑time stochastic dynamics, (ii) fairness‑driven utility maximization, (iii) deadline‑based time‑efficiency, and (iv) online learning with provable $\tilde O(B^{-1/2})$ regret.  This combination of features fills a gap in the literature and provides a versatile methodological foundation for a broad class of fairness‑sensitive sequential decision problems.

[ANSWER]: Image-to-image translation is frequently cast as a per‑pixel deterministic regression problem between two image domains [1, 2, 3]. Paired approaches employ conditional adversarial networks to learn the mapping directly [4], whereas unpaired methods introduce additional constraints on the joint distribution of images from separate domains to enable training without explicit correspondences [5–9]. CycleGAN [5] and UNIT [6] learn bidirectional mappings between domains, while several recent works focus on unidirectional mappings [7–9].

The quantification of uncertainty in predictions generated by unpaired image‑to‑image translation models has received limited attention. In medical imaging, where image‑to‑image translation is applied to tasks such as modality synthesis and image enhancement [10–15], reliable confidence estimates are particularly valuable, especially when certain imaging modalities are missing [16, 17].

Uncertainty estimation for deep models is commonly categorized into epistemic uncertainty, which reflects limited knowledge about model parameters due to finite training data, and aleatoric uncertainty, which captures inherent noise in the observations [18, 19]. Bayesian deep learning techniques have been employed to estimate various forms of uncertainty in image‑to‑image translation and related regression tasks [19–23]. Providing uncertainty estimates can facilitate downstream decision‑making and allow clinicians to review model outputs in high‑stakes applications [24–34].

Many existing approaches model per‑pixel heteroscedasticity using a Gaussian distribution for regression problems [19]. However, Gaussian assumptions may be suboptimal when data contain outliers that follow heavy‑tailed distributions [35, 36]. Alternative probabilistic models, such as the generalized Gaussian distribution, can represent a broader family of distributions—including Gaussian, Laplace, and heavier‑tailed variants—thereby offering greater flexibility for capturing diverse noise characteristics.

[ANSWER]: Decomposing visual content into semantically meaningful parts for analysis, synthesis, and editing has been explored through layered representations. Early work decomposed videos into image layers undergoing temporally‑varying spatial warps, grouping content by motion similarity and demonstrating applications to compression. Flexible Sprites introduced full‑canvas semi‑transparent layers with translation‑only motion, solving for layers and motion parameters via expectation‑maximization and applying the technique to video editing. Subsequent extensions expanded the motion model while retaining the translation constraint. Localized approaches limited motion to neighborhoods around anchors, enabling tractable inference with many sprites and factorizing spatially and temporally recurring local patterns for downstream editing. Other methods decomposed videos of humans into independent layers to enable retiming of individual actions, though motion and appearance remained entangled. Generative adversarial networks have incorporated layered representations as inductive biases, often restricting layers to solid colors. Automatic image decompositions into “soft layers” based on texture, color, or semantic features have supported image editing, while deep image priors and differentiable compositing have been employed to separate images into layer pairs and discover patterns, respectively.

Interpretability in neural synthesis has been addressed by constraining generators to domain‑specific, differentiable functions for gradient‑based optimization. Some approaches limit generators to a small set of parametric photographic edits, others make established imaging operators differentiable to preserve efficiency relative to costly neural networks. Ray‑marching priors and rendering models have been used to train multilayer perceptrons that encode radiance fields for novel view synthesis. Neural textures replace RGB textures on 3D meshes with high‑dimensional trainable features, enabling view‑consistent editing through projection and decoding. Spatial transformers integrated into generators provide interpretable, explicit geometric transformations. These works aim to balance fidelity, control, and interpretability in generative models.

Object‑centric representations have been pursued through unsupervised compositional methods. Slot Attention extracts object‑centric representations from videos, while other approaches such as SPACE seek meaningful decompositions into object layers using unsupervised learning, though they may embed recurring patterns in background layers without explicit control. MONet decomposes images into multiple object regions via an attention mechanism. Recent studies employ predetermined parametric primitives as building blocks for image representations, facilitating structured decomposition.

In the domain of video games, models like GameGAN synthesize new frames conditioned on controller inputs by splitting rendering into static and dynamic components, yet they render full frames without factorizing individual parts, resulting in entangled appearance and dynamics. 

Video compression techniques have long leveraged appearance consistency and motion compensation. Deep learning‑based compression methods have further advanced the field. Classical approaches such as DjVu separate scanned documents into a background layer compressed with wavelets and a foreground text layer, exploiting glyph recurrence for compression. Digipaper extends this idea with three layers, supporting colored text. Image epitomes summarize and compress an image’s shape and appearance into a miniature texture, reconstructing the original via smooth mappings. These layered and dictionary‑based strategies inspire modern attempts to model videos as compositions of moving elements, aiming to reduce redundancy and improve editing control.

[ANSWER]: Conventional deep learning approaches to action recognition can be divided into two main streams: deep sequential models and deep convolutional networks. Early work extended recurrent architectures to the three‑dimensional spatio‑temporal domain, enabling direct modeling of motion dynamics. Parallel to this line, two‑stream convolutional networks introduced separate branches for RGB frames and optical flow, explicitly capturing appearance and motion cues. Subsequent methods such as C3D and I3D generalized 2‑D convolutions to 3‑D kernels, providing a natural framework for spatio‑temporal feature learning, albeit at the cost of substantially higher computational demand and a need for larger training datasets. To mitigate these issues, factorized designs like P3D and S3D decomposed 3‑D convolutions into spatial 2‑D and temporal 1‑D components, reducing complexity while preserving performance. Architectures such as SlowFast and X3D explored trade‑offs among spatial resolution, temporal frame rate, and channel width to achieve efficient video recognition. The introduction of non‑local operations added the capability to capture long‑range dependencies across space and time. More recently, pure‑Transformer models have been applied to spatio‑temporal learning, leveraging self‑attention to model extended temporal relationships and further improving recognition accuracy.

In the broader vision community, Transformers have achieved notable success following their dominance in natural language processing. Vision Transformers (ViT) demonstrated that large‑scale pre‑training can yield state‑of‑the‑art results for image classification, despite the lack of built‑in inductive biases present in convolutional networks. Variants such as CvT incorporated convolutions to enhance efficiency, while DeiT and MViT employed knowledge distillation and multi‑scale designs to address training challenges. Extensions like PVT and segmentation‑oriented Transformers expanded the paradigm to dense prediction tasks, including object detection and semantic segmentation. Directly applying Transformers to video spatio‑temporal representation, however, intensifies training difficulty and necessitates careful architectural design.

Transformer‑based action recognition has attracted increasing attention, with several works focusing on efficiency and scalability. ViViT and TimeSformer investigated factorization strategies along spatial and temporal dimensions to reduce computational load. MViT introduced a multi‑scale Transformer that balances resolution and channel capacity, constructing hierarchical representations that progress from fine‑grained dense features to coarser, abstract ones. VATT explored unsupervised, multi‑modality self‑supervised learning using a pure‑Transformer backbone. Collectively, these studies illustrate the evolving landscape of Transformer architectures for video understanding, highlighting advances in factorization, multi‑scale modeling, and self‑supervised training.

[ANSWER]: Recent advances in neural network architectures have markedly improved performance on a variety of natural‑language processing tasks. Models based on recurrent, convolutional, and transformer layers have been applied to reading‑comprehension benchmarks, achieving state‑of‑the‑art results by learning contextual representations of long passages [1]. Similar architectures have been extended to dialogue modeling and generation, where they capture turn‑taking dynamics and produce coherent responses across multiple conversational turns [2, 3, 4]. A substantial body of work has focused on attention mechanisms that allow models to weigh salient words or sentences when producing predictions, and on memory‑augmented networks that store and retrieve external knowledge to enrich representations [5, 6, 1].

The integration of personality information into conversational agents has attracted growing interest, particularly for enhancing the naturalness and diversity of chit‑chat interactions. Early approaches employed rule‑based or statistical methods to inject predefined persona traits, while more recent neural systems condition response generation on learned persona embeddings. By embedding personality cues, these models reduce repetitive utterances and enable agents to adapt their style to different user profiles, improving user engagement in open‑domain dialogue settings [7, 8, 3, 9].

In the domain of literary and cinematic analysis, graphical and probabilistic models have been proposed to infer character personas from narrative texts. Such approaches often rely on structured inputs, including plot summaries, character interaction networks, or annotated scripts, to discover latent persona clusters that align with manually defined archetypes. While effective for discovering high‑level character roles, these methods typically do not exploit dialogue excerpts directly, limiting their applicability to tasks that require fine‑grained conversational cues [10, 11, 12].

The present work introduces a supervised character‑trope classification framework that learns persona embeddings from short dialogue snippets extracted from movie subtitles. By assembling a dataset of IMDB quotes aligned with the 72 tropes defined in the CMU Movie Summary Corpus, the study evaluates neural models that incorporate multi‑level attention over words and over sets of utterances. Experiments demonstrate that this hierarchical attention substantially outperforms baseline classifiers. Moreover, the incorporation of a Knowledge‑Store memory initialized with textual descriptions of each trope further enhances performance, suggesting a flexible mechanism for leveraging prior semantic knowledge. These contributions extend prior research on attention‑based NLP models, personality‑aware dialogue systems, and narrative persona inference by directly linking conversational language to interpretable persona categories and by providing a transferable approach for downstream applications such as character similarity search and movie clustering.

[ANSWER]: Quantized neural networks have demonstrated that high‑precision parameters are not strictly required for strong performance in deep models. Early work reduced memory usage and computational complexity by employing low‑bitwidth weights and activations, showing substantial savings while maintaining accuracy on benchmarks such as CIFAR‑10 and SVHN. Subsequent extensions generalized these schemes to include low‑bitwidth gradients, enabling training of convolutional networks with quantized parameters. By applying quantization after training or using the straight‑through estimator, these approaches avoided the difficulties associated with non‑differentiable optimization. However, experiments on large‑scale classification tasks such as ImageNet revealed a rapid degradation in performance when weights and activations were quantized below four bits. Further refinements addressed the gradient‑mismatch problem in low‑bitwidth models, yet a gap remained between highly quantized networks and their full‑precision counterparts.

Binarized neural networks explore the extreme case of quantization by restricting weights and activations to binary values. Historically, binary activations were introduced as unit step functions, motivated by biologically inspired spiking behavior that enables event‑based computation and energy‑efficient operation. More recent approaches have formalized binarized neural networks with binary weights and activations at inference time, and have extended binarization to intermediate representations within convolutional architectures. These methods drastically lower memory requirements and replace most arithmetic operations with bitwise logic, offering the potential for significant gains in power efficiency. Despite these advantages, binary models typically incur a noticeable drop in accuracy compared with full‑precision networks, especially on challenging datasets.

[ANSWER]: Existing approaches to human‑AI shared control can be grouped into two broad families. In the first family, human input is used only during training to shape the learned policy, after which the autonomous system operates without further human involvement. Early work demonstrated that incorporating human demonstrations or feedback improves performance on visual control benchmarks such as Atari games, and subsequent studies extended these ideas to robotic manipulation and navigation, showing gains in sample efficiency and task success. The second family retains a human in the loop at test time, allowing the operator to intervene or provide high‑level commands while the agent executes its policy. A number of investigations have applied this paradigm to Atari environments, autonomous driving platforms, robot arms, and multi‑agent scenarios, reporting that continuous human assistance can increase safety, robustness, and overall task effectiveness.

A parallel line of research focuses on interpreting the internal representations of deep neural networks. Most interpretability techniques have been developed for vision models, where unit‑level visualizations, saliency maps, and feature‑attribution methods reveal how convolutional layers encode objects, textures, and spatial layouts. Empirical analyses have shown that training for a primary objective often yields emergent, semantically meaningful structures such as object detectors within classifiers or disentangled latent factors in generative models, and that manipulating these latent directions can steer the output of image synthesis systems. For visuomotor policies, researchers have adapted saliency‑based explanations and activation mapping to relate visual inputs to selected actions, providing insights into decision making in agents that process raw images.

Neuroscience‑inspired perspectives have also been leveraged to understand and design control policies. Techniques originally devised for studying animal brains—such as representational similarity analysis (RSA), centered kernel alignment (CKA), and other similarity metrics—have been applied to deep reinforcement‑learning agents, revealing that distinct neural layers encode behaviors operating at different temporal scales. Concepts like motor primitives, drawn from motor‑cortex research, have motivated the development of hierarchical and compositional controllers that aim to improve generalization across tasks and environments. Recent work has explored building biologically plausible policy architectures and using neuroscientific analysis tools to dissect the dynamics of learned agents.

In this work we introduce a minimalist, frequency‑based method that aligns the internal activations of a pre‑trained reinforcement‑learning policy with kinematic attributes of the agent’s behavior, thereby constructing an interpretable control interface without altering the original network or retraining it. By identifying units whose activation spectra match specific motion primitives and modulating their intensity, the approach enables fine‑grained human‑in‑the‑loop control at test time, extending beyond goal‑conditioned or high‑level command schemes. Experiments on autonomous‑driving and quadrupedal locomotion benchmarks demonstrate that the resulting shared‑control system improves safety and performance in previously unseen traffic scenarios and endows a forward‑only locomotion policy with obstacle‑avoidance capabilities. Compared with prior human‑in‑the‑loop methods, our technique does not require additional training data or reward redesign; unlike vision‑centric interpretability tools, it operates directly on the latent dynamics of control policies; and whereas earlier neuroscience‑inspired analyses have been primarily diagnostic, our method leverages those insights to create a practical, real‑time interface for human‑AI collaboration.

[ANSWER]: Several benchmarks have been proposed to evaluate robust imitation learning (IL). Early efforts adapted standard Gym MuJoCo environments by introducing “test” variants that altered dynamics or visual observations, using simple reward functions such as “run forward” to assess generalisation in inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) settings [1, 2, 3, 4]. These modifications, however, often involve trivial objectives and provide limited opportunities for cross‑environment transfer. Gridworld‑based suites have been employed to study meta‑ and multi‑task IRL, explicitly separating demonstration and execution environments within meta‑testing tasks [5, 6]. Although these benchmarks share the notion of a demonstration/test split, they typically involve comparatively simple tasks. Dedicated IL benchmarks targeting challenging robotics problems have also been introduced, focusing on task performance rather than systematic evaluation of generalisation [7, 8].

Outside of IL, a variety of machine‑learning benchmarks assess generalisation in reinforcement learning (RL). Collections of environments designed to probe RL generalisation [9, 10, 11] and meta‑ or multi‑task RL [12] define clear objectives through reward functions, thereby eliminating ambiguity about the training goal. The primary challenge in these settings is to learn policies that achieve the specified goals across diverse contexts, solve multiple tasks simultaneously, or adapt to new tasks with limited interaction. Instruction‑following benchmarks for natural‑language understanding similarly incorporate train/test splits to probe different facets of generalisation [13, 14]. Although not centered on IL, they share a methodological emphasis on systematic evaluation across varied conditions. The Abstract Reasoning Corpus (ARC) evaluates supervised learners’ ability to extrapolate geometric patterns in a human‑like manner [15]; despite lacking a sequential decision‑making component, it motivates the development of priors such as objectness and goal‑directedness that are also relevant to IL research.

A range of algorithmic approaches aim to improve robustness in IL. Meta‑IL methods assume access to abundant demonstrations for a set of training tasks and only a few demonstrations for novel test tasks, leveraging shared structure to transfer knowledge [16, 17]. Analogous meta‑IRL techniques operate under similar assumptions, treating each test task as having a distinct but related objective [5, 2]. Multi‑task IL and IRL frameworks also explore knowledge sharing across tasks [6, 18, 19, 20]. These approaches generally presuppose that meta‑train and meta‑test environments are similar, focusing on transferring intent or behavior rather than handling substantial discrepancies between demonstration and execution settings.

[ANSWER]: Benchmarking graph learning methods has revealed numerous pitfalls that can obscure true progress, especially in static settings. Prior work such as Dwivedi et al. identified inconsistent experimental configurations that lead to unreliable comparisons, while Shchur et al. demonstrated that reusing identical train‑test splits induces overfitting and can flip method rankings. The Open Graph Benchmark (OGB) later responded by offering diverse datasets together with unified splits and metrics to improve reproducibility. Building on this line of inquiry, our study extends the scrutiny to dynamic link prediction, where the evaluation challenges are even more pronounced. By designing stricter negative‑sampling protocols, introducing a memorization‑based baseline, and releasing six new dynamic datasets, we provide a complementary suite of tools that address the specific shortcomings of current dynamic‑graph benchmarks.

Research on dynamic graphs has largely concentrated on differentiating dynamic from static link prediction (e.g., Junuthula et al.) and on leveraging auxiliary information such as friendship networks to improve future‑link forecasts. Comprehensive surveys (e.g., Haghani and Keyvanpour) have categorized dynamic link prediction into missing‑link and future‑link tasks, but they stop short of questioning the adequacy of existing evaluation pipelines. In contrast, our work adopts a different perspective: we keep the task definition unchanged but fundamentally reshape the evaluation landscape. Our two novel negative‑sampling strategies—historical and inductive—target the bias introduced by easy negatives, while EdgeBank, a simple memorization baseline, highlights how current protocols can be inadvertently over‑optimistic. Moreover, by curating datasets from politics, economics, and transportation, we broaden the domain coverage beyond the socially‑biased benchmarks that dominate prior studies.

Negative sampling has long been recognized as a critical component of graph representation learning. Early approaches for static link prediction relied on uniformly random negatives, whereas later methods introduced degree‑aware or distance‑aware schemes to generate harder examples. Empirical analyses by Kotnis and Nastase showed that training‑time negative‑sampling choices can markedly affect learned embeddings in knowledge‑graph settings. However, the impact of negative sampling during **evaluation** has received little attention. Our contribution fills this gap by systematically investigating how different test‑time negative‑sampling strategies influence reported performance. The historical and inductive samplers we propose are specifically engineered for dynamic graphs, leveraging edge‑recurrence patterns to produce challenging negatives that better discriminate among competing models. This advances the state of the art beyond existing static‑focused sampling heuristics and aligns evaluation more closely with real‑world deployment scenarios.

The rapid growth of dynamic graph representation learning has produced a rich ecosystem of temporal graph neural networks, from early surveys by Kazemi et al. and Skardinga et al. to concrete architectures such as JODIE, DyRep, TGAT, TGN, and CAWN. While these models have reported near‑perfect link‑prediction scores on popular benchmarks, such results often stem from evaluation protocols that fail to stress the models with difficult negatives or diverse graph domains. Our experiments confirm that, under the proposed harder negative‑sampling regimes and on the newly introduced multi‑domain datasets, the apparent superiority of many existing DGNNs diminishes, exposing the need for more rigorous assessment. Consequently, our work not only supplies stronger baselines and richer datasets but also redefines the evaluation standards that future dynamic‑graph research should adopt.

[ANSWER]: Recent unsupervised foreground‑background segmentation approaches that rely on generative adversarial networks (GANs) typically embed the mask generation inside a generative pipeline. Methods such as LR‑GAN, ReDO, SEIGAN and OneGAN succeed in producing masks while learning to synthesize backgrounds and foregrounds, but they either require additional clean‑background images, assume that foreground replacement does not affect the data distribution, or depend on oversimplified objectness priors that restrict their applicability to datasets with diverse object shapes. In contrast, our method does not need any external background collection or adversarial training; by formulating foreground‑background partition as an Expectation‑Maximization problem over a mixture‑of‑experts model, we obtain masks directly from a single image and preserve a principled probabilistic interpretation of the segmentation. This positions our approach as a fully unsupervised alternative that is both simpler to train and more robust to varied real‑world imagery.

Compositional scene‑decomposition models such as IODINE, Slot‑Attention, MONet and GENESIS share a common design: a set of latent slots, often with fully shared parameters, are used to explain an image as a spatial mixture of objects and a “virtual” background. While these slot‑based techniques achieve impressive results on synthetic benchmarks with simple textures, they frequently collapse or produce fragmented masks when confronted with complex natural backgrounds, because the shared generator cannot capture the regularities that distinguish background regions from foreground objects. Our approach departs from this paradigm by introducing a dedicated pixel‑reassignment module that explicitly encodes background regularities as an inductive bias, and by employing a mixture‑of‑experts generator whose components interact during the EM‑based competition. Consequently, DRC retains the compositional spirit of slot models but overcomes their background‑modeling limitations, enabling reliable foreground extraction on challenging real‑world and multi‑object scenes.

A second family of unsupervised methods builds upon deep feature extractors or data‑driven energy functions to define region properties, exemplified by W‑Net, deep clustering‑based segmentation, invariant‑information clustering, and independence‑based partitioning. These techniques often hinge on a downstream post‑processing step (e.g., CRF smoothing, normalized cuts) or require auxiliary modalities such as depth, which constrains their ability to produce a single, well‑defined foreground mask. By reconciling an energy‑based prior with a generative mixture‑of‑experts model and solving the resulting formulation with EM, our method provides a unified probabilistic framework that simultaneously learns feature representations, enforces a foreground‑background energy bias, and yields explicit masks without any additional processing. Thus, DRC bridges the gap between classical energy‑based segmentation and modern generative modeling, offering a principled and end‑to‑end unsupervised solution that outperforms prior feature‑centric and energy‑centric baselines on complex datasets.

We normalize TDMR tuples for accurate leaderboard construction and propose novel experimental settings that reflect realistic, dynamic scenarios, including cold-start conditions without any pre-defined TDM taxonomy. By simultaneously constructing a scientific knowledge graph and linking publications to it in an open-domain setting, our method is inherently adaptable to the evolving research landscape.

[ANSWER]: Graph neural networks (GNNs) have become the de‑facto standard for learning representations on graph‑structured data, achieving state‑of‑the‑art performance on node classification [1]–[4], graph classification [5] and link prediction [6].  The line of work began with early spectral and spatial formulations [7], [8] and has since evolved to incorporate message‑passing mechanisms that aggregate neighbor information, graph‑convolutional filters [9], [10] and attention‑based weighting of edges [3].  Although these advances have dramatically improved predictive accuracy, they provide little insight into which substructures or features drive a model’s decisions.  Our method leverages any existing GNN encoder as a black‑box predictor and augments it with a dedicated explanation module, thereby addressing the critical gap between high‑performance graph learning and model interpretability.  

Interpretability techniques for neural networks fall into white‑box (gradient‑ or perturbation‑based) and black‑box (local surrogate) families [15], [16].  Early approaches for GNNs, such as GNNExplainer, adopt a forward‑propagation, instance‑wise strategy that selects a compact subgraph and feature subset by maximizing mutual information with the target prediction [22].  While effective for single‑instance analysis, GNNExplainer must be retrained for each explanation, cannot be directly applied to unseen nodes, and therefore offers limited global insight into the trained model.  Model‑level methods like XGNN produce explanations that do not necessarily correspond to actual substructures of the input graph, sacrificing local fidelity [23].  In contrast, PGExplainer introduces a parameterized, end‑to‑end explainer that shares its neural‑network parameters across all instances, enabling simultaneous, high‑fidelity explanations for multiple nodes or graphs, and supporting inductive generalization without re‑training.  This design yields both instance‑specific relevance and a coherent global view of the underlying GNN, addressing the shortcomings of prior local‑only or fidelity‑limited methods.  

Probabilistic graph generation has a long history, from the Erdős–Rényi model that samples each edge independently [24], [25] to more sophisticated constructions that enforce degree distributions, distances or spectral characteristics [26]–[29].  Recent deep generative models further improve realism by learning complex structural priors directly from data [30]–[32].  However, these models are primarily aimed at recreating entire graphs rather than extracting explanatory substructures.  PGExplainer repurposes the generative paradigm for explanation: it learns edge‑wise Bernoulli distributions conditioned on the GNN’s hidden representations, using a shared neural network to parameterize the distribution.  This enables efficient sampling of explanatory subgraphs that are directly tied to the predictive behavior of the target GNN, distinguishing our approach from conventional graph generators that do not consider model‑specific relevance.

[ANSWER]: The perceptual score evaluates how much a classifier depends on a specific input modality, a topic that intersects with research on dataset biases, bias‑mitigation methods, and feature‑importance techniques. Over the past two to three decades, data has been a pivotal driver of machine‑learning progress, exemplified by the ImageNet challenge and the emergence of AlexNet, which catalyzed the deep‑learning era. As datasets have expanded, various biases have been identified. For example, background cues in ImageNet can reveal object classes, and crowdsourced annotations from platforms such as Amazon Mechanical Turk can introduce uncontrolled socio‑economic signals. In visual question answering, studies have shown that the question alone can be sufficient to predict the answer, indicating that image information may be unnecessary. Attempts to address this bias include re‑annotation of the dataset and restructuring of train‑test splits. Similar observations have been made in audio‑visual scene‑aware dialog, where question cues often dominate, and in textual inference tasks such as SNLI, where linguistic features unrelated to the premise correlate with labels. In the Story Cloze dataset, story length alone proves to be a strong predictor of the correct ending. The SocialIQ dataset, designed for reasoning about social situations in videos, also exhibits a bias whereby textual sentiment correlates strongly with correctness, allowing models to perform well without visual input. These findings highlight the difficulty of constructing completely unbiased datasets and motivate the development of automated bias‑measurement techniques.

A range of methods has been proposed to reduce bias in datasets. Some approaches require prior knowledge of the biased variables; for instance, gender bias in vision has been mitigated by masking gender‑related features such as faces. Other techniques rely on access to the test set to perform re‑balancing. In visual question answering, several strategies employ a classifier trained solely on the question modality to regularize bias. One method masks the main model’s softmax output with the prediction of a question‑only classifier, while another combines a full classifier with a question‑only classifier using a product‑of‑experts framework. These approaches share the use of a subset classifier to indicate reliance on a particular data subset.

Feature‑importance methods aim to explain model predictions by quantifying the contribution of individual features. LIME introduces a local, linear approximation around a given example to provide explanations, whereas DeepLIFT is tailored for deep networks and connects to Shapley‑value concepts. SHAP estimates feature importance through an approximation of Shapley values without assuming linearity, and the combination of DeepLIFT with Shapley values has been termed Deep SHAP. While these techniques focus on the importance of individual features, other work has examined the contribution of entire modalities. EMAP, for example, assesses how much a multimodal classifier benefits from cross‑modal interactions by approximating it with single‑modality classifiers. This line of research seeks to determine the reliance of classifiers on each modality, often on a per‑instance basis.

[ANSWER]: Machine‑learning‑based MRI reconstruction has rapidly advanced, yet most prior studies focus on improving quantitative fidelity (e.g., SSIM, NMSE) rather than addressing the perceptual artifacts that hinder clinical adoption.  Earlier works on banding artifacts are confined to balanced steady‑state free precession (bSSFP) sequences, where physics‑based models [1] and supervised deep networks [2] have been employed to suppress sequence‑specific banding.  Those approaches are not designed for the anisotropic, phase‑encoding‑direction banding that arises from Cartesian subsampling in accelerated acquisitions, and they rely on explicit knowledge of the pulse sequence.  In contrast, the present study introduces the first method that directly targets acceleration‑induced banding in deep‑learning reconstructions, using an unsupervised adversarial loss that requires no additional annotations, sequence parameters, or post‑processing steps.  This positions our work as a novel contribution to the reconstruction literature, filling a gap that has been largely ignored despite its importance for clinical translation.

Adversarial learning has been leveraged in medical imaging for tasks such as segmentation [3], MR angiography synthesis [4], super‑resolution [5], and data anonymization [6], and more recently for end‑to‑end MRI reconstruction [7].  However, existing adversarial reconstruction frameworks typically penalize deviations from fully sampled ground‑truth images, which can induce hallucinated anatomical detail and compromise diagnostic safety.  Our approach departs from these designs by employing an orientation‑specific adversary that operates solely on reconstructed images, thereby discouraging banding without forcing the network to imitate the ground‑truth intensity distribution.  This orientation‑aware adversarial loss preserves genuine anatomical features while eliminating the characteristic streaks, and it does so without incurring extra computational overhead at inference time.  Consequently, our method advances adversarial training for MRI reconstruction by providing a safer, annotation‑free mechanism that directly addresses a clinically relevant artifact.

[ANSWER]: The extensive body of work on defending classifiers against adversarial perturbations demonstrates that most heuristic defenses have been defeated by stronger adaptive attacks, and that even the most successful scalable approach—PGD‑based adversarial training and its variants—still achieves limited robust accuracy on benchmark datasets such as CIFAR‑10. In contrast, our paper does not aim to propose a new defense; instead, it provides a diagnostic tool that explains why existing defenses fall short by revealing that the error regions of state‑of‑the‑art models exhibit high label uncertainty, a phenomenon that current robustness evaluations overlook. This perspective clarifies the gap between reported robust accuracies and the intrinsic difficulty of the classification task, thereby offering a complementary explanation to the empirical shortcomings of current defenses.  

Certified defenses based on linear programming, semidefinite programming, interval bound propagation, and randomized smoothing have shown that provable robustness can be attained for small networks, yet they often sacrifice empirical performance and do not scale to the larger models used in practice. Our contribution differs from these works in that we do not seek to certify robustness for a particular model; rather, we introduce a label‑uncertainty‑aware concentration framework that can be applied to any classifier to assess whether the underlying data distribution itself permits high‑accuracy robust classification. By incorporating label information into the concentration measure, we provide a more realistic intrinsic robustness limit that is applicable to the large‑scale models typically evaluated by certified methods.  

Theoretical analyses that connect concentration of measure with intrinsic robustness limits have established that, in concentrated metric probability spaces, adversarially robust classifiers cannot exist. These results, however, treat the input distribution as label‑agnostic and therefore yield limits that may be overly pessimistic for supervised learning tasks. Our work builds directly on this line of research by identifying the insufficiency of label‑agnostic concentration and formally defining label uncertainty as the average ambiguity of label assignments within a region. We then integrate this notion into the concentration function, yielding a label‑uncertainty‑constrained concentration measure that refines existing theoretical limits and makes them directly relevant to classification problems.  

Empirical methods for estimating concentration, such as the data‑driven estimator of Mahloujifar et al. and its later refinements, have enabled practitioners to compute intrinsic robustness bounds for image benchmarks. Nevertheless, these estimators ignore the relationship between the geometry of input regions and their associated labels, which leads to underestimates of the true robustness barrier. By adapting the Mahloujifar estimator to incorporate label uncertainty, we propose a novel algorithm that more accurately quantifies the intrinsic robustness of CIFAR‑10 and CIFAR‑10H. Our experiments demonstrate that the resulting bounds are tighter than those obtained by standard concentration estimates and align better with observed clean and robust accuracies, highlighting the practical impact of accounting for label information.  

Finally, prior works that estimate lower bounds on concentration via generative modeling or half‑space approximations have advanced the methodological toolbox but still operate without label awareness. Our results show that label information is essential for understanding intrinsic robustness limits: error regions produced by contemporary robust classifiers systematically exhibit higher label uncertainty than random subsets, and models that explicitly consider this uncertainty—e.g., by abstaining on high‑uncertainty examples—can achieve improved robustness‑accuracy trade‑offs. Thus, our contribution not only extends existing intrinsic‑robustness estimation techniques but also opens a new research direction centered on label‑aware analysis and abstention strategies for adversarial defense.

[ANSWER]: Domain adaptation methods aim to reduce the shift between source and target distributions by exploiting target data that is available during training, often through adversarial alignment, discrepancy minimization, or self‑supervised objectives.  While these techniques achieve impressive results when unlabeled or partially labeled target samples can be accessed, the core assumption that target data are present at training time is violated in many real‑world scenarios, such as privacy‑restricted or continuously evolving environments.  Recent few‑shot and one‑shot adaptation approaches relax this assumption but still require at least a handful of target examples to guide the learning process.  In contrast, our work operates under the strict domain‑generalization setting where **no target sample is seen during training**; we embed the ability to adapt directly into the model so that, at test time, each individual sample can be accommodated with a single forward pass and without any fine‑tuning or auxiliary networks.

Domain‑generalization research has largely followed two complementary directions: learning representations that are invariant across the observed source domains, and augmenting the source data to mimic unseen variations.  Invariant‑feature methods minimize statistical distances (e.g., MMD, CORAL, or adversarial losses) among source domains, thereby hoping that the learned features will transfer to novel domains.  Data‑augmentation strategies synthesize new styles, perturbations, or virtual domains to expose the model to a broader range of distributions.  Both families improve average performance on unseen targets, yet they still suffer from an “adaptivity gap” because the trained model is static once training ends.  Our contribution is orthogonal to these lines: instead of enforcing invariance or expanding the source manifold, we **learn a variational posterior that conditions model parameters on each test sample**, effectively treating every test instance as its own domain.  This per‑sample adaptation bridges the adaptivity gap without altering the source‑training objectives of existing invariant or augmentation methods.

Meta‑learning has been adopted for domain generalization by treating each source domain as a pseudo‑task and training the model to quickly adapt to a held‑out meta‑target domain.  Approaches based on Model‑Agnostic Meta‑Learning (MAML) and its variants introduce inner‑loop updates that require gradient steps on the meta‑target data, while regularization schemes encourage smooth adaptation across tasks.  Our framework also leverages a meta‑learning paradigm, but we **replace the inner‑loop gradient update with a closed‑form variational inference step that generates task‑specific parameters in one forward pass**.  By randomly splitting source domains into meta‑source and meta‑target sets during training, we expose the model to realistic domain shifts and teach it to infer suitable parameters directly from a single sample.  Consequently, our method retains the fast‑adaptation spirit of meta‑learning while eliminating the computational overhead of test‑time optimization.

Test‑time adaptation methods seek to refine a pretrained source model using unlabeled target data at deployment, typically through entropy minimization, self‑supervised objectives, or learned domain embeddings.  These techniques usually require a batch of target samples, an additional fine‑tuning stage, or an auxiliary network that must be trained jointly with the main model.  Even when single‑sample adaptation is reported, performance degrades sharply without a supporting batch.  Our approach differs fundamentally: **the adaptation mechanism is baked into the model during source‑only training**, so that at deployment the model generates a sample‑specific set of parameters on the fly, using only the information contained in that single test point.  No gradient updates, no extra networks, and no reliance on batches of target data are needed, making our method uniquely suited for scenarios with isolated or streaming test samples from multiple unknown domains.

Overall, our contributions lie at the intersection of these four research strands.  We advance domain‑generalization by introducing a variational, meta‑learned adaptation process that operates at the granularity of individual test samples, thereby closing the adaptivity gap left by invariant‑feature and augmentation methods, avoiding the computational burden of gradient‑based meta‑learning, and surpassing test‑time adaptation techniques that depend on target batches or fine‑tuning.  Empirical results on standard benchmarks confirm that this single‑sample generalization yields performance that is comparable to or exceeds state‑of‑the‑art domain‑generalization methods across diverse domain shifts.
