[ANSWER]: State‑of‑the‑art lexical semantic change detection models are based on vector‑space representations.  These approaches can be divided into type‑based (static) models, which learn a single vector per word form, and token‑based (contextualized) models, which generate a representation for each occurrence of a word in its surrounding context.  Both families have been shown to capture diachronic semantic shifts, with static models such as SGNS‑based embeddings and token‑based models relying on contextualized language models.  

A large portion of the literature has focused on the construction of evaluation datasets and on the systematic development, testing, and analysis of change‑detection models.  Common evaluation protocols involve annotating a set of target words that have been selected from historical dictionaries or lexical resources and measuring model performance against these gold standards.  This line of work includes numerous studies that create and exploit such annotated corpora across different languages and time spans.  

Beyond benchmark evaluation, only a limited number of studies have attempted to discover previously unknown instances of semantic change.  These works typically validate the top‑ranked candidates generated by their models using author intuition, known historical facts, or external lexical resources.  While they demonstrate the feasibility of discovery, the validation procedures remain informal and are not grounded in systematic human annotation.  

A small subset of research has introduced more rigorous annotation schemes for discovered changes.  One study asked annotators to rate a random sample of one hundred words on a four‑point scale ranging from “no change” to “significant change,” but did not tie the ratings to a specific evaluation dataset.  Another effort collaborated closely with a professional lexicographer, who examined twenty model‑predicted lemmas together with ten randomly selected ones; both studies evaluated predictions at the lemma level.  These approaches highlight the potential of human‑in‑the‑loop validation but stop short of analyzing semantic change at the finer granularity of individual word usages.  

Earlier approaches to semantic change detection have generally relied on off‑the‑shelf static or contextualized embeddings trained on large corpora without further adaptation to the diachronic task.  Consequently, they lack mechanisms for fully automated prediction of changing words across arbitrary corpora, and they do not provide end‑to‑end frameworks that combine model training, discovery, and evaluation in a reproducible pipeline.  

In this work we bridge these gaps by fine‑tuning both static and contextualized embedding models on recently released high‑quality German diachronic corpora, thereby improving their ability to uncover novel semantic shifts.  We introduce a usage‑level annotation protocol that enables annotators to assess change for individual word occurrences, facilitating the construction of sense‑based usage clusters and their visualization.  Our evaluation incorporates both crowd‑sourced judgments and expert feedback from a professional lexicographer, demonstrating the practical utility of the discovered changes for lexicographic work.  Finally, we provide a largely automated, general‑purpose framework that integrates model fine‑tuning, candidate discovery, and systematic evaluation, making it straightforward to apply the approach to other languages and corpora.

[ANSWER]: Knowledge can be elicited from pretrained language models. Numerous studies have shown that large‑scale models implicitly store vast amounts of factual and commonsense information that can be retrieved via conditional generation [1–3]. Leveraging this property, researchers have demonstrated that such models can directly perform inference on a variety of downstream tasks, including commonsense reasoning [4,5], text classification [6], and natural language inference [6,7]. This line of work motivates the use of symbolic, explicit knowledge extracted from language models to guide downstream predictions.

External commonsense knowledge bases have also been employed to improve performance on NLP tasks that require reasoning. One family of approaches injects knowledge into language models either by pretraining on structured resources [8] or by fine‑tuning the models to incorporate retrieved facts during inference [8]. Another direction grounds the input question in a knowledge graph and applies graph‑based reasoning mechanisms [9–11]. These methods typically rely on the availability of a high‑quality, high‑coverage, in‑domain commonsense knowledge base [12] to provide the necessary background information.

A limitation of knowledge‑base‑centric methods is their dependence on the alignment between the target dataset and the underlying resource. Several benchmark datasets are derived from existing graphs—for example, CommonsenseQA originates from ConceptNet [13,14] and the NLI split used in ROC‑Stories [15,16]. When the dataset’s domain matches the source graph, incorporating the corresponding knowledge yields substantial gains [8]. However, performance deteriorates when there is a domain mismatch, or when no suitable knowledge base exists (e.g., NumerSense [17]). This mismatch creates a bottleneck for tasks that lie outside the coverage of current commonsense resources.

A complementary strategy augments the input question with model‑generated text such as clarifications, explanations, or implications. Self‑Talk elicits clarifications of ambiguous concepts and appends them to the model’s input [18]. Contrastive Explanations generate explanations that differentiate between answer choices and feed them to the inference model [19]. These methods rely on hand‑crafted, task‑specific prompts to steer the generator, limiting the variety of knowledge that can be obtained and requiring additional engineering effort for new tasks. Other explanation‑based approaches fine‑tune the generator to produce explanations that are then used for question augmentation [20].

In this work we introduce a simple, task‑agnostic framework that generates arbitrary knowledge statements from a pretrained language model and incorporates them into the inference process without requiring external knowledge bases or task‑specific supervision. By prompting a “knowledge” model with generic demonstrations, we obtain multiple question‑related statements; an “inference” model then evaluates the original question together with each statement and selects the answer associated with the highest probability. Experiments on four commonsense benchmarks—NumerSense, CommonsenseQA 2.0, QASC, and an additional scientific commonsense dataset—show that the approach consistently improves both off‑the‑shelf and fine‑tuned models, achieving state‑of‑the‑art results on three of them. Ablation studies reveal that performance scales with the quality and quantity of generated statements and depends on the inference strategy used to integrate them. Notably, the method remains effective when the same base language model is employed for both knowledge generation and inference, highlighting the flexibility of large language models as sources of symbolic knowledge and offering an engineering‑efficient alternative to existing template‑driven or knowledge‑base‑dependent techniques.

[ANSWER]: GFlowNets instantiated on tree‑structured directed acyclic graphs can be interpreted as reinforcement‑learning agents that optimize a soft Q‑function with an entropy regularizer, or equivalently as instances of control‑as‑inference and soft Q‑learning. In this setting the generative process follows a single parent‑child lineage, and the learning objectives reduce to familiar RL losses. Empirical studies have shown that such autoregressive formulations break down when the underlying state‑transition graph contains multiple convergent pathways, i.e., in the general DAG case where many distinct action sequences can lead to the same terminal object.  

A number of reinforcement‑learning works have explored loss functions that are evaluated on sub‑trajectories rather than on individual transitions. These approaches compute a training signal by aggregating rewards and value estimates along short fragments of an episode, with the aim of improving credit assignment over longer horizons. The idea of propagating information over entire sampled trajectories bears a conceptual resemblance to these sub‑trajectory losses, although the precise formulation and theoretical guarantees differ.  

Methods for sampling from unnormalized densities on discrete spaces have traditionally relied on Markov‑chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) techniques. Prior research has proposed various strategies to accelerate or partially amortize MCMC, including specialized proposals, adaptive schemes, and domain‑specific heuristics. While such approaches seek to reduce the computational burden of exact sampling, they typically treat the compositional structure of the target objects as a fixed bias in the sampler. In contrast, GFlowNets learn a stochastic policy that explicitly exploits the compositional nature of the data, shifting the cost of handling structure from the generation phase to the learning phase.  

In this work we introduce a trajectory‑balance learning objective for GFlowNets that operates on complete sampled action sequences from the initial state to a terminal state. We prove that any global minimizer of this objective defines a policy that samples exactly from the desired unnormalized target distribution, thereby guaranteeing correctness without requiring the flow‑matching or detailed‑balance constraints of earlier formulations. Empirically, the trajectory‑balance objective yields faster convergence, higher sample diversity, and greater robustness to long action sequences and large action spaces than the previously used flow‑matching and detailed‑balance objectives. We evaluate the approach on four benchmark domains—an illustrative hypergrid environment, a molecular synthesis task, synthetic sequence generation, and real antimicrobial‑peptide sequence design—demonstrating consistent improvements across settings with multimodal rewards, high‑dimensional combinatorial spaces, and long generation horizons. These results position trajectory balance as an effective alternative to both autoregressive RL‑style GFlowNets and traditional MCMC‑based samplers, addressing credit‑assignment inefficiencies and extending the applicability of GFlowNets to richer, more complex generative problems.

SciREX [7] extends information extraction to the document level, capturing relationships that span across sections, which is critical for leaderboard generation from full papers.

[ANSWER]: To alleviate the limited‑information challenge inherent in few‑shot NER, many studies have turned to external knowledge sources.  Early approaches leveraged pretrained language models (PLMs) as richer encoders for the scarce examples [1, 2], and later works framed NER as a prompt‑based task to more directly exploit the knowledge encoded in PLMs [3‑11].  Another line of research reuses annotations from existing NER corpora, either by pre‑training NER models on these datasets or by transferring learned representations [12‑14].  Distantly supervised datasets have also been constructed automatically from web resources, with the goal of providing additional training signals; however, such datasets often contain partially labeled instances [18‑20] and noisy annotations [21‑22].  

The knowledge‑mismatch problem—where entity schemas from different resources diverge—has been addressed through several complementary strategies.  Label‑projection methods map labels across heterogeneous schemas to a common space [23‑25, 12].  Manually authored label descriptions have been used to enrich label semantics and facilitate transfer [26‑28].  Some approaches merge labels from disparate schemas into a unified taxonomy, enabling knowledge sharing across resources [29].  Additionally, noisy external datasets have been relabeled according to the target label set to reduce inconsistency [17].

In this work, we introduce a self‑describing mechanism that represents both entity types and mentions with a universal set of concepts, thereby simultaneously tackling the limited‑information and knowledge‑mismatch challenges.  Building on this mechanism, we propose Self‑describing Networks (SDNet), a sequence‑to‑sequence generation model that (i) generates concept‑based descriptions for mentions, (ii) automatically maps novel entity types to the shared concept space, and (iii) performs on‑demand entity recognition via concept‑enriched prefix prompts.  By pre‑training SDNet on a large‑scale corpus harvested from Wikipedia anchors linked to Wikidata items—covering millions of sentences and tens of thousands of concepts—we obtain a model that can be fine‑tuned on few‑shot NER benchmarks across diverse domains.  Extensive experiments on eight benchmarks demonstrate that SDNet achieves competitive results and sets new state‑of‑the‑art performance on the majority of them, confirming the effectiveness of the universal concept mapping and the generation‑based framework for few‑shot NER.

While these sources are popular, they lack rigorous quality control, including standardized TDM entity representation and complete publication coverage. These studies typically focus on linking scientific content to structured entities but often assume that the schema or triples are known in advance.
In contrast to prior work, our approach addresses several critical limitations. We construct our dataset manually from publications to ensure comprehensive TDMR annotations, rather than relying on incomplete or unstandardized third-party sources. We normalize TDMR tuples for accurate leaderboard construction and propose novel experimental settings that reflect realistic, dynamic scenarios, including cold-start conditions without any pre-defined TDM taxonomy. By simultaneously constructing a scientific knowledge graph and linking publications to it in an open-domain setting, our method is inherently adaptable to the evolving research landscape.

[ANSWER]: Many classical optimization methods have been proposed to improve the practical efficiency of solving optimization problems. General techniques such as constraint and variable elimination—removing non‑active constraints or redundant variables—are widely used, while problem‑specific techniques, for example KKT factorization for convex quadratic programs, have also been developed [1]. In the deep‑learning literature, a substantial body of work has sought to approximate or accelerate optimization models. Reviews of combinatorial optimization [2] and optimal power flow [3] highlight two main approaches. The first class trains machine‑learning models to map directly from optimization inputs to full solutions, a strategy related to surrogate modeling [4]; however, these methods often struggle to produce solutions that are simultaneously feasible and near‑optimal. The second class integrates machine‑learning components with optimization procedures, for instance by learning warm‑start points [5, 6] or by predicting active constraints to enable constraint‑elimination techniques [7].  

Incorporating constraints into neural networks is common despite the perception of deep learning as unconstrained. Simple constraints appear implicitly in many standard layers: softmax enforces simplex constraints, sigmoids impose upper and lower bounds, ReLUs project onto the positive orthant, and convolutional layers encode translational equivariance—a principle extended in group‑equivariant networks [8]. More specialized constraints have also been embedded, such as energy conservation [9, 10] and homogeneous linear inequality constraints [11]. While these examples illustrate a variety of “special case” constraints, relatively little work has addressed the integration of more general hard constraints into deep‑learning models.  

Implicit layers have attracted significant interest as structured neural‑network components that define implicit relationships between inputs and outputs. Examples include layers designed for SAT solving [12], ordinary differential equations [13], normal and extensive‑form games [14], rigid‑body physics [15], sequence modeling [16], and various classes of optimization problems [17‑22]. Softmax, sigmoid, and ReLU can also be interpreted as implicit layers [23], although explicit implementations are typically more efficient. In principle, implicit layers enable direct enforcement of constraints within neural networks, for instance by projecting outputs onto a constraint set using quadratic‑programming layers for linear constraints [17] or convex‑optimization layers for general convex constraints [24]. However, the computational expense of such projection‑based layers can be substantial, motivating alternative strategies such as implicit differentiation to backpropagate through equality‑constraint completions when explicit solutions are unavailable.

[ANSWER]: The Bayesian optimization (BO) framework [1] traces its origins to the seminal work of [2] and has been applied to a diverse set of problems, including hyperparameter tuning of machine learning algorithms [3], [4], materials design [5], drug discovery [6], and robot locomotion [7], [8]. A substantial body of research within BO addresses the presence of heterogeneous evaluation costs across the search space, often by incorporating cost-awareness into the acquisition strategy. Much of this work is situated in the multi‑fidelity setting, where cheaper approximations of the objective function are available; representative contributions include [9]–[13] and the earlier study [4]. In the single‑fidelity regime, cost‑aware methods have been proposed in [3], [14] and [15].

The approach of expected improvement per unit of cost (EI‑PUC) was introduced in [3]. This acquisition function takes the form  

\[
\text{EI‑PUC}(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{\mathbb{E}[\max(0, f(\mathbf{x}) - f_{\text{best}})]}{c(\mathbf{x})},
\]

where \(c(\mathbf{x})\) denotes the cost of evaluating the objective at \(\mathbf{x}\) and the numerator is the expected improvement. When the cost function is unknown and must be learned, the expectation can be taken over the distribution of improvement‑to‑cost ratios, or the denominator can be replaced by the mean cost. A simple variation, expected improvement per unit of cost with cost cooling (EI‑PUC‑CC), was proposed in [14]. EI‑PUC‑CC introduces a cost exponent that depends on the ratio between the remaining and initial budgets, thereby discouraging high‑cost evaluations early in the BO loop and allowing them as the budget is depleted. Neither EI‑PUC nor EI‑PUC‑CC incorporates uncertainty in the cost model or quantifies the budget‑dependent value of information in a principled manner.

A non‑myopic treatment of budgeted BO with heterogeneous costs was presented in [15]. That work models the optimization process as a finite‑horizon constrained Markov decision process (MDP) and proposes a rollout acquisition function based on a heuristic base policy: a fixed number of look‑ahead steps using EI‑PUC followed by a final step using standard EI. The resulting acquisition function can be interpreted as a single step of policy improvement over the “EI‑PUC followed by EI” heuristic described in [16].

Dividing a cost‑agnostic acquisition function by a cost term is a common practical technique for handling cost heterogeneity and is closely related to the “value‑divided‑by‑cost” heuristic employed in knapsack problems [17]. In the classical knapsack setting, selecting items that maximize the ratio of value to cost yields a solution that attains at least a \((1 - 1/e)\) fraction of the optimal value [18]. This guarantee relies on the additive nature of value in the knapsack formulation. In BO, however, the value of a set of evaluations is defined by the maximum observed objective value rather than the sum, and consequently the “value‑divided‑by‑cost” approach can perform arbitrarily worse than the optimal policy.

The multi‑armed bandit literature has also examined heterogeneous evaluation costs [17], [19], [20]. These studies develop optimistic algorithms that maximize a reward‑to‑cost ratio, which is appropriate when performance is measured by cumulative regret. In contrast, optimization‑oriented settings such as best‑arm identification require different considerations.

Non‑myopic BO, which accounts for the impact of future evaluations when assessing the utility of a candidate point, has been explored in a series of works [21]–[26]. Among these, only the approach of [15] explicitly handles heterogeneous evaluation costs while maintaining a non‑myopic perspective.

[ANSWER]: Over the past several years, a substantial body of research has employed information‑theoretic tools to both interpret the behavior of deep neural networks [1], [2], [3] and to derive generalization bounds [4]–[10]. The earliest contributions in this line are found in [4] and [5], which introduced information‑stability bounds that involve the mutual information between the algorithm’s output and the training samples. Subsequent works [6]–[7] extended this approach, but the resulting bounds can degenerate when the data distribution is continuous. This limitation was highlighted in [8], which proposed a non‑degenerate bound based on conditional mutual information (CMI). The CMI framework was further developed in [9] and [10]; in particular, [10] derived bounds that depend on the algorithm’s predictions rather than its internal representations, and combined these with CMI to obtain some of the strongest information‑theoretic generalization guarantees currently available.

The computability of the bounds in [10] has been noted as a potential concern, since evaluating them may require operations exponential in the dataset size. Related work has also explored the interpretability of such bounds, seeking connections with classical validation techniques. Deterministic algorithm outputs have been addressed in [10] and [11], where the authors argue that only a finite number of runs of a (potentially randomized) algorithm can be observed on a given dataset. Building on this perspective, later studies have introduced geometry‑aware methods that incorporate properties of the loss landscape into the analysis of algorithmic outputs.

Parallel to the information‑theoretic literature, there is a line of research linking stochastic gradient descent (SGD) to generalization. Early contributions such as [12] investigated the empirical behavior of SGD, while more recent works [13] and [11] have connected SGD dynamics to information‑theoretic generalization bounds. Finally, the relationship between information‑theoretic approaches and classical notions of algorithmic stability has been examined, demonstrating that stability concepts can be recovered within the CMI framework.

[ANSWER]: Federated learning enables a set of clients to collaboratively train a model while preserving data privacy and reducing communication overhead. Early work such as FedAvg introduced a simple scheme in which each client performs local updates and the server aggregates them by averaging, providing a baseline for many subsequent studies. A substantial body of research has focused on improving convergence through advanced optimization techniques, enhancing privacy guarantees, increasing robustness to statistical heterogeneity, and lowering communication costs. These efforts typically aim to learn a single global model that is shared across all participants, which can be limiting when client data distributions differ markedly.  

To address client heterogeneity, personalized federated learning (PFL) has emerged as a distinct line of work. Approaches in this area include multitask learning formulations that treat each client as a related task, meta‑learning strategies that adapt a shared initialization to individual client data, and model‑mixing schemes that combine global and local parameters. Regularization‑based methods enforce soft parameter sharing, while clustering techniques group similar clients so that they exert greater influence on one another. More recent proposals have introduced central hypernetworks that generate personalized models conditioned on client‑specific representation vectors.  

A complementary perspective treats federated learning from a Bayesian standpoint. Several studies have applied variational inference to Bayesian neural networks within the federated setting, enabling uncertainty quantification across distributed data. Matching algorithms based on the Beta‑Bernoulli process have been employed to align local models before constructing a global aggregate. Gaussian‑process (GP) frameworks have also been explored, including global GP regression models and client‑selection strategies that leverage GP priors. Stein‑variational gradient descent has been proposed for Bayesian federated optimization, though scalability remains a challenge for large networks. Other Bayesian aggregation schemes rely on multivariate Gaussian product mechanisms, which can be less effective when client data are highly heterogeneous.  

Gaussian‑process classification differs from regression in that the non‑Gaussian likelihood requires approximate inference. Classical approximations include the Laplace method, expectation‑propagation, and least‑squares approaches. More recent advances exploit Pólya‑Gamma augmentation to handle multinomial likelihoods, facilitating GP classification, few‑shot learning, and incremental learning scenarios. Variational inference remains a common tool for GP classification, but alternative strategies that retain conjugacy allow direct Gibbs sampling from the posterior, yielding well‑calibrated and accurate predictive models.

[ANSWER]: Recent offline reinforcement‑learning algorithms rely on a variety of algorithmic components, most notably value‑function learning, dynamics modelling, and uncertainty quantification.  While these approaches have achieved strong performance, they typically require intricate stabilisation tricks, extensive hyper‑parameter tuning, and often blend model‑based and model‑free ideas.  In contrast, our work deliberately eschews value functions and focuses on the simplest possible formulation: conditional behavior cloning trained by maximum‑likelihood.  By restricting attention to this class of methods, we are able to isolate the elements that truly matter for offline RL and to provide a clear baseline against which more elaborate techniques can be measured.

Goal‑conditioned and reward‑conditioned behavior cloning are the most common instantiations of supervised offline RL.  Prior studies have demonstrated that conditioning on a target goal or on a scalar reward can transform sub‑optimal trajectories into useful supervision, and many of these works augment the basic scheme with iterative data collection or offline‑online loops that are not permissible in the strict offline setting.  Our experiments evaluate pure goal‑ and reward‑conditioning in a fully offline regime and show that, when paired with a modest two‑layer feed‑forward network, these approaches achieve performance on par with the best value‑based and transformer‑based baselines across all D4RL suites we consider.

A number of recent papers have explored richer conditioning signals—such as multi‑step history, inferred task embeddings from inverse RL, or other auxiliary task descriptors—and have argued that high‑capacity autoregressive Transformers or advantage‑weighting schemes are required to unlock the full potential of RvS methods.  We directly compare against these proposals and find that the additional modelling complexity does not translate into systematic gains.  Instead, careful control of model capacity (through regularisation or architecture choice) and an appropriate selection of the conditioning variable (goal versus reward) are sufficient to reach state‑of‑the‑art results.

Finally, several works have claimed that advantage weighting or other forms of re‑weighting are essential for strong offline performance, especially on domains with sparse near‑optimal trajectories such as Franka Kitchen or AntMaze.  By conducting a comprehensive ablation across diverse tasks, we demonstrate that simple maximum‑likelihood training without any weighting or sequence modelling already attains or exceeds the reported scores of those methods.  This positions our contribution as a field guide for practitioners: RvS can be implemented with a small feed‑forward network, modest hyper‑parameter effort, and without the need for Transformers, advantage weighting, or iterative data collection, thereby clarifying the true limits and necessary components of supervised offline RL.

[ANSWER]: In the literature on intransitive pairwise‑preference modeling, several parametric frameworks have been proposed to capture violations of transitivity. Early low‑rank approaches [1] introduced a rank‑constrained ranking model, but their analysis was limited to the subclass of tournaments that are already transitive, leaving the broader family of intransitive tournaments unexplored. Subsequent extensions of the Bradley‑Terry‑Luce family [2, 3] and more recent models [4‑7] broadened the expressive power of the likelihood functions yet provided no structural description of the tournaments that can be realized at a given representation dimension. By contrast, our work supplies a complete structural characterization of the tournaments representable in any fixed dimension $d$ through the identification of forbidden configurations that must belong to unions of flip classes. In particular, we prove that rank‑2 representable tournaments coincide exactly with locally transitive tournaments, and we leverage this insight to obtain a Quicksort‑based algorithm for the minimum feedback‑arc‑set problem on this class—capabilities that are absent from prior preference‑modeling literature.

The concept of flip classes was originally introduced in [8] as a tool for analyzing equilibria of generalized rock‑paper‑scissors games on tournaments. Although the initial motivation was game‑theoretic, the definition partitions the space of all $n$‑node tournaments into equivalence classes based on edge‑flip operations. Our contribution repurposes and extends this notion to the study of parametric tournament representations: we show that the set of forbidden configurations for any rank‑$d$ tournament class necessarily forms a union of flip classes, and we explicitly construct the forbidden flip class for rank‑2 and for general rank $d$ (including a coned‑doubly regular tournament of size $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt d)$). This establishes flip classes as a fundamental combinatorial invariant for representation theory, a role not recognized in the original work.

Research on dimension complexity and sign rank of sign‑pattern matrices has produced powerful lower‑bound techniques (e.g., Forster’s theorem [9]) and upper‑bound results based on Vapnik–Chervonenkis dimension [10, 11]. These contributions have been instrumental in learning theory and circuit complexity, but they do not connect the algebraic rank of a tournament’s skew‑symmetric matrix to graph‑theoretic properties of the tournament itself. Building on Forster’s framework, we prove a tight $\Omega(\sqrt n)$ lower bound on the minimal dimension required to represent arbitrary $n$‑node tournaments, thereby extending the classic dimension‑complexity results to the tournament setting. Moreover, we introduce the Flip Feedback Node set—a graph‑theoretic parameter that captures the smallest number of distinct vertices appearing in any feedback‑arc‑set of the relevant flip class—and use it to derive a novel linear upper bound on the representation dimension of a given tournament. This yields new sign‑rank upper bounds that depend on structural features of the underlying tournament rather than on VC dimension, offering an alternative to the existing algebraic approaches.

[ANSWER]: Many approaches have sought to reduce the computational burden of Neural Architecture Search by employing proxies such as reduced model size, smaller training datasets, or fewer training steps. While these shortcuts can accelerate the evaluation of candidate architectures, they often yield inaccurate performance estimates and consequently degrade the quality of the searched models.  

Another line of work focuses on parameter sharing across the search space, allowing multiple architectures to be trained jointly within a single supernet. By reusing weights, the training time for each individual candidate is reduced, yet the shared‑weight paradigm still faces challenges in providing fair and precise performance assessments for all architectures, especially when the search space is large and diverse.  

A further set of methods builds explicit performance predictors that map architectural descriptors—such as network topology, depth, and model size—to expected accuracy, latency, or other metrics. Examples include approaches that encode architectures into feature vectors and train multilayer perceptrons or other regression models to forecast hardware latency or validation performance. Although predictor‑based techniques markedly speed up the estimation phase, they typically require a substantial number of fully trained samples to achieve reliable accuracy, which in turn raises the overall search cost.  

In contrast, the present work formulates Neural Architecture Search as a ranking problem and introduces a pairwise ranking model that learns to distinguish higher‑quality architectures from lower‑quality ones without requiring precise performance predictions. By reducing the ranking task to binary classification over architecture pairs, the method dramatically cuts the number of required training examples. An architecture selection strategy is also proposed to prune the search space toward promising candidates based on feature importance. Extensive experiments on machine translation and language‑modeling benchmarks demonstrate that this ranking‑based approach attains state‑of‑the‑art results while being orders of magnitude faster than conventional NAS systems, including hardware‑aware baselines, and discovers novel architectures that surpass strong existing models with significantly lower search cost.

[ANSWER]: The statistical learning literature on binary classification has traditionally focused on risk measured by accuracy, with a rich collection of finite‑sample guarantees for this metric (e.g., Mohri et al., 2018). In settings where one class dominates, accuracy becomes a poor proxy for practical performance, motivating alternatives such as cost‑weighting, where class‑specific misclassification costs are introduced. Early work on cost‑weighted learning showed that, for calibrated losses, minimizing a surrogate loss yields a Bayes‑optimal classifier in the classical sense (e.g., Bartlett et al., 2006; Steinwart, 2007). These results, however, are confined to surrogate losses that correspond to the zero‑one loss and do not address performance metrics that are arbitrary functions of the confusion matrix.

A broad family of practical techniques for handling severe class imbalance has been developed, including resampling strategies, margin adjustments, and data‑augmentation methods. Undersampling of the majority class and oversampling of the minority class are widely used (e.g., Chawla et al., 2002; He & Garcia, 2009), and recent advances in generative modeling have enabled sophisticated synthetic data generation (e.g., GAN‑based oversampling). Although undersampling often yields computational benefits with modest statistical cost, the theoretical impact of oversampling schemes such as SMOTE remains largely unexplored. Margin‑adjusted support‑vector machines and related deep‑learning extensions modify the decision boundary to reflect class‑specific weights (e.g., Wu & Zhou, 2016; Liu et al., 2020), yet the statistical properties of these adjustments are typically established only in asymptotic regimes.

From a decision‑theoretic perspective, Neyman–Pearson classification treats the imbalanced problem as a constrained hypothesis‑testing task, minimizing the error of one class while bounding the error of the other. This framework has attracted considerable theoretical attention, leading to finite‑sample risk bounds and optimality results under the Neyman–Pearson constraint (e.g., Scott et al., 2012; Tong et al., 2020). While these guarantees are robust within the Neyman–Pearson setting, they do not directly extend to more general performance measures that are arbitrary functions of the confusion matrix, such as the F‑measure or class‑weighted accuracy.

The nearest‑neighbor (NN) family of non‑parametric classifiers has been studied extensively in terms of misclassification risk. Classical results established that the risk of the 1‑NN rule is at most twice the Bayes risk (Cover & Hart, 1967) and that k‑NN classifiers are Bayes‑consistent under appropriate conditions on k and the sample size (Stone, 1977). Subsequent work has refined these accuracy‑based bounds, providing rates that depend on the smoothness of the regression function and the intrinsic dimension of the feature space (e.g., Devroye et al., 1996; Biau et al., 2015). Parallel developments in NN regression have yielded uniform‑error convergence rates under weighted risk criteria (e.g., Audibert & Tsybakov, 2007), typically assuming additive noise with finite variance and a covariate density bounded away from zero.

Algorithmic adaptations of NN methods to imbalanced data have also been proposed. Prototype‑selection techniques aim to retain representative minority points (e.g., Wilson & Martinez, 2000), while gravitational‑based distance modifications alter the influence of samples according to class frequency (e.g., Yang & Chen, 2015). Although these methods demonstrate empirical improvements on skewed datasets, formal statistical guarantees for their performance under imbalance have not been established.

In this work we bridge these gaps by (i) deriving a general Bayes‑optimal characterization for any performance metric that is a function of the confusion matrix, thereby extending the classical accuracy‑optimal result to a broad family of measures and showing that stochastic classifiers can strictly dominate deterministic ones; (ii) eliminating the absolute‑continuity assumption that underlies many existing optimality proofs, thus broadening applicability to realistic data‑generating processes; (iii) establishing regret bounds that relate the excess performance under arbitrary metrics to the uniform‑loss error of estimated class‑probability functions, providing a unified analysis that connects cost‑weighting, Neyman–Pearson constraints, and general metric optimization; (iv) introducing the notion of Uniform Class Imbalance, a formal model of severe skew that captures how the minority‑class proportion vanishes uniformly across the feature space, and demonstrating how this model influences the optimal classifier and the achievable performance; (v) applying the generalized Bayes framework to k‑NN classification, we obtain the first finite‑sample uniform‑error guarantees for k‑NN under severe imbalance, contrasting with prior accuracy‑only results and with NN‑regression bounds that rely on weighted risk; and (vi) showing that the derived guarantees subsume and sharpen existing results for cost‑weighted and Neyman–Pearson classifiers while also providing a theoretical foundation for the observed empirical benefits of NN‑based algorithmic modifications in imbalanced settings. Together, these contributions deliver a comprehensive statistical theory for binary classification under class imbalance that unifies performance‑metric generalization, uniform‑loss analysis, and non‑parametric learning.

[ANSWER]: Attention mechanisms have become a cornerstone of sequence‑to‑sequence modeling, offering a flexible way to capture relationships between source and target tokens [1, 2, 3]. This flexibility, however, can make learning the desired alignment more challenging, prompting research on incorporating inductive biases. For instance, structural alignment biases—including a positional bias that reflects the tendency of source and target tokens to occupy similar relative positions—were introduced to neural machine translation models [4]. While such biases improve performance, they do not explicitly enforce monotonic alignments.

A line of work has focused on hard monotonic attention, which restricts the attention distribution to a single source position that moves monotonically through the input. Early proposals modified the attention mechanism to produce hard monotonic alignments rather than soft distributions over the entire source sequence [5]. Subsequent extensions refined this idea by allowing a pointer to traverse the source sequence while computing soft attention over a local window [6] or over all positions up to the current pointer [7]. Hard monotonic attention has been shown to accelerate decoding and enable online processing in settings such as simultaneous translation and automatic speech recognition. Moreover, several studies reported performance gains on broadly monotonic tasks—especially under low‑resource conditions—by biasing attention toward monotonicity; a notable example demonstrated strong results for morphological inflection when the hard monotonic attention was guided by an external alignment [8].

Probabilistic latent‑variable formulations have also been explored for attention that is hard but not necessarily monotonic [9]. Building on this, later work presented an exact hard monotonic attention model that learns the alignment jointly with the sequence‑to‑sequence parameters [10]. This jointly learned monotonic attention achieved state‑of‑the‑art results on grapheme‑to‑phoneme conversion, transliteration, and morphological inflection, highlighting the benefit of integrating alignment learning directly into the model rather than relying on external supervision.

The transformer architecture [3] has recently surpassed recurrent models on a variety of low‑resource, character‑level transduction tasks [11, 12] as well as on neural machine translation [13]. While some studies have extended hard monotonic and locally constrained attention mechanisms to multi‑head settings [14], there remains a paucity of investigations into how monotonicity bias interacts with standard transformer attention on inherently monotonic tasks such as grapheme‑to‑phoneme conversion, transliteration, or morphological inflection.

In this work we introduce a model‑agnostic monotonicity loss that can be applied to both recurrent and transformer sequence‑to‑sequence architectures. The loss quantifies the degree of monotonic behavior exhibited by the soft attention weights during training and includes two hyperparameters that control the strength of the monotonicity bias. By incorporating monotonicity through a regularization term rather than altering the attention mechanism itself, the approach retains the benefits of soft attention while enabling a continuum from unconstrained to fully monotonic behavior. Moreover, the loss can be applied selectively to individual attention heads, allowing exploration of trade‑offs between representation capacity and monotonic bias. Experiments across grapheme‑to‑phoneme conversion, morphological inflection, transliteration, and dialect normalization demonstrate that the proposed loss yields highly monotonic attention patterns and improves performance for recurrent models; for transformers, biasing all heads can be detrimental, whereas constraining a subset of heads leads to modest gains on several tasks.

[ANSWER]: Explainable machine learning has been explored through a variety of complementary approaches. A substantial body of work focuses on quantifying the importance of training instances, often by measuring the effect of individual examples on model parameters or predictions; representative methods include those described in [1]–[5]. Feature‑level explanations constitute another major strand, encompassing both feature‑importance scores derived from model internals and gradient‑based techniques that propagate relevance backward through the network; notable contributions in this area are found in [6]–[12]. Perturbation‑based strategies, which assess the impact of systematically altering inputs, overlap with gradient methods and are detailed in [13]–[16]. Self‑explaining models aim to embed interpretability directly into the architecture, with several proposals presented in [17]–[19]. Counterfactual explanations, which identify minimal changes to inputs that would alter the model’s output, have been investigated in [20]–[24]. Concept‑based methods seek to relate model behavior to human‑understandable concepts, as discussed in [25] and [26].

Within natural‑language processing, data‑importance techniques have been applied to diagnose and mitigate dataset artifacts, with studies such as [27] and [28] highlighting the detection of spurious patterns. Leveraging instance‑level influence measures, other works have demonstrated improvements in model robustness and performance, exemplified by the approaches in [29] and [30] that build on influence functions and the TracIn algorithm. Collectively, these lines of research provide a rich toolkit for interpreting and refining machine‑learning systems across domains.

[ANSWER]: Network architecture evaluation has long been recognized as a bottleneck for neural architecture search (NAS). Early approaches relied on training each candidate from scratch, incurring prohibitive computational costs. To alleviate this, weight‑sharing schemes were introduced, whereby subnetworks sampled from a supernet inherit shared parameters. Although this dramatically reduces training time, subsequent analyses revealed a weak correlation between the shared‑weight performance and the true ranking of architectures, causing instability even in modest search spaces. Further investigations highlighted that inaccurate evaluation can render NAS essentially random. More recent zero‑cost methods attempt to score architectures using only the initial random weights and a single forward–backward pass, offering substantial speedups. However, their predictive power deteriorates in larger benchmarks such as NASBench‑101, where they struggle to distinguish high‑performing models. Complementary to algorithmic advances, curated benchmarks containing fully trained networks for all architectures in a given search space have been released, enabling the community to isolate and compare search strategies under controlled evaluation conditions.

The expense of evaluating architectures on target tasks has motivated the use of proxy tasks and datasets. A common practice is to search on a small, well‑studied dataset (e.g., CIFAR‑10) and then transfer the discovered architecture to a larger, more demanding benchmark (e.g., ImageNet). Empirical studies have examined how well performance on one classification dataset predicts performance on others, reporting varying degrees of transferability across both supervised and unsupervised settings. Analyses on NASBench‑201 quantified ranking correlations across multiple datasets, while other work explored transfer across task modalities. Despite these efforts, even training on a reduced proxy dataset typically requires several epochs of full training, limiting the overall efficiency gains that proxy‑based NAS can achieve.

Self‑supervised learning has emerged as an alternative avenue for architecture evaluation without reliance on labeled data. By constructing surrogate objectives from automatically generated pseudo‑labels, researchers have leveraged tasks such as rotation prediction, colorization, and solving jigsaw puzzles to assess network quality. These proxy tasks have been employed to rank architectures and guide search, demonstrating that unsupervised signals can capture useful representational capacities. Nevertheless, most existing self‑supervised evaluations still adopt classification‑oriented objectives and require processing the entire dataset, leaving open questions about the optimality of the chosen tasks and the potential benefits of alternative supervision signals.

In this work we introduce a generic NAS framework that departs from classification‑based evaluation and instead employs a regression‑driven proxy task built on manually designed synthetic signal bases. By training architectures to regress onto these task‑agnostic signals, the method directly measures intrinsic representation power, fully exploits every training sample, and eliminates the need for task‑specific labels. A lightweight automated search identifies the most suitable combination of synthetic bases for a given search space, further enhancing correlation with downstream performance. Extensive experiments across thirteen convolutional and one language modeling search space demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves substantially higher ranking correlations (Spearman’s ρ) with the true downstream accuracies while requiring only a single batch of unlabeled data and a few seconds of training. Compared with weight‑sharing, zero‑cost, and self‑supervised classification proxies, the regression‑based strategy delivers orders‑of‑magnitude speedups and consistently discovers top‑performing architectures, establishing a new baseline for efficient, task‑agnostic neural architecture search.

[ANSWER]: Two principal families of structure‑learning methods have been investigated in the literature: score‑based and constraint‑based approaches. Score‑based techniques evaluate candidate graphs using a scoring function such as the Bayesian Dirichlet equivalent (BDe) score [1] and then explore the space of structures with heuristics like greedy equivalence search [2]. Constraint‑based methods, in contrast, rely on conditional‑independence (CI) tests to identify the optimal structure in the large‑sample limit [3, 4]. These methods tend to be faster, scale more readily to high‑dimensional domains, and possess clear stopping criteria (e.g., a maximal conditioning set size). Their main limitation is sensitivity to errors in CI testing, especially for high‑order tests and limited data. Hybrid algorithms that combine scoring and CI testing have been proposed and shown empirically to improve performance [5].

Recent work has focused on improving the efficiency of exact structure discovery. Algorithms that compute the maximum‑a‑posteriori (MAP) structure more rapidly have been introduced [6–9]. In parallel, several techniques for enumerating the top‑k most probable networks have been developed [10–12]; many of these rely on novel search spaces and specialized search strategies, yet they remain practical only for relatively small problems (typically up to about 25 variables). An alternative line of research employs Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling to approximate the posterior over graphs [13–17]. While MCMC provides a flexible framework for Bayesian inference, it offers no finite‑run guarantees on approximation quality, may suffer from poor mixing, and incurs substantial computational cost, limiting its applicability to modest‑size domains.

While these sources are popular, they lack rigorous quality control, including standardized TDM entity representation and complete publication coverage. Some efforts, such as [1] and [2], go further by extracting TDM triples and result values, and normalizing them for leaderboard construction. However, these approaches are constrained by their reliance on a closed domain, requiring extracted triples to match a pre-defined TDM taxonomy. Other studies, such as [3] and [4], only extract TDM triples without result values, thereby forgoing leaderboard construction. Meanwhile, [6] focuses on extracting result values, but only in the context of pre-defined TDM triples, making it similar in limitation to [1]. Consequently, these methods are not adaptable to the dynamic nature of emerging benchmarks, where new tasks, datasets, and metrics are frequently introduced.
In a parallel research thread, scientific leaderboards have been treated as a form of scientific knowledge graphs composed of Task, Dataset, and Metric entities. Information extraction in this context has been widely studied, as seen in works such as [5, 7, 8, 9, 10]. These studies typically focus on linking scientific content to structured entities but often assume that the schema or triples are known in advance.
In contrast to prior work, our approach addresses several critical limitations. We construct our dataset manually from publications to ensure comprehensive TDMR annotations, rather than relying on incomplete or unstandardized third-party sources. We normalize TDMR tuples for accurate leaderboard construction and propose novel experimental settings that reflect realistic, dynamic scenarios, including cold-start conditions without any pre-defined TDM taxonomy. By simultaneously constructing a scientific knowledge graph and linking publications to it in an open-domain setting, our method is inherently adaptable to the evolving research landscape.

[ANSWER]: Besag’s auto‑models provide a framework that accommodates a variety of non‑Gaussian distributions, including logistic auto‑models for binary data, which have also been studied in subsequent works on binary observations. Although Gaussian assumptions are often adopted for computational tractability, the resulting linear‑least‑squares formulation yields the optimal fit for arbitrary distributions under certain conditions.  

Sparse inverse covariance estimation has progressed far beyond the original graphical lasso, with a focus on efficient optimization of the full likelihood in both high‑dimensional regimes (large numbers of variables relative to observations) and settings with limited data. Node‑wise regression approaches have been proposed for structure learning and for parameter estimation, closely related to Besag’s pseudo‑likelihood methodology and its later generalizations.  

Dependency networks share the idea of regressing each node on its neighbors, but they may produce inconsistent joint distributions when trained on finite data; consequently, Gibbs sampling is often employed to enforce consistency, at the cost of increased computational overhead.  

In the SLIM family of models, the objective includes sparsity‑promoting ℓ₁ regularization and non‑negativity constraints on regression parameters, which can affect both predictive accuracy and training time. Variants such as fsSLIM first determine a sparsity pattern using a k‑nearest‑neighbor heuristic before solving separate regression problems for each node, a special case of set‑wise approximations that consider groups of variables jointly. Other extensions drop the zero‑diagonal constraint to gain computational speed, though this property is essential in the original auto‑model formulation. Logistic‑loss based versions require solving a logistic regression for each node, which can be computationally demanding.  

Deep learning and autoencoder‑based methods have yielded substantial improvements in collaborative filtering, typically learning low‑dimensional embeddings through encoder–decoder architectures. In contrast, approaches based on pseudo‑likelihood of Markov random fields aim to reconstruct observed variables directly, without explicit hidden layers, and often employ full‑rank parameter matrices with constraints that promote generalization to unseen data. Recent research has combined low‑rank and full‑rank components, and has explored fully invertible deep networks and flow‑based generative models as alternatives for recommendation tasks.  

Neighborhood‑based methods traditionally rely on heuristic similarity measures such as cosine similarity. More recent work emphasizes principled learning of similarity matrices from data, highlighting that the appropriate similarity is linked to the inverse of the covariance matrix rather than the covariance itself, and that the resulting similarity may be asymmetric.

[ANSWER]: Distributed inference techniques that scale computation by delivering tasks across parallel nodes have been explored in several recent studies. The approach of distributing calculus operations such as vector products or matrix inversions, followed by centralization, is presented in [1]. Although this method does not produce usable models under the definition of a module, it demonstrates a strategy for handling pre‑trained, non‑obsolete models.  

Research on nodes that contain usable Gaussian‑process (GP) models includes the works of [2]–[5]. These studies share the idea of maintaining functional GP models on distributed devices, while differing in the treatment of non‑Gaussian likelihoods. Variational approximation methods for such likelihoods have been introduced in later extensions.  

The exploitation of full stochastic process properties to replace likelihood terms in general bounds was initially considered in [6] and further developed in [7], which derived expectation‑propagation (EP) methods for streaming inference in GPs. Federated and continual learning settings have been addressed in [8], focusing on EP, and in the Bayesian framework of [9]; both provide analyses of GP applications to continual learning, albeit at a smaller scale.  

The use of inducing points as pseudo‑approximations of local data subsets is discussed in [10], where potential applications to distributed environments are highlighted. In the context of dynamical modular models, [11] proposes a factorization across tasks that resembles the approach of [12] and aligns closely with state‑space methods.  

Distinct from approaches that treat model collections as dictionaries of kernels, [13] adopts a kernel‑dictionary perspective rather than a model‑centric one. Finally, [14] incorporates uncertainty into large‑population analyses using optimal‑transport concepts, specifically GPs combined with 2‑Wasserstein metrics.

[ANSWER]: Past work has investigated a variety of lexical and semantic diversity metrics as well as methods for evaluating these metrics. Traditionally, diversity has been measured over a model’s predictions on an entire test set, a setting often referred to as Test Set Diversity. In this formulation, a model generates a single response for each conversation in the test set, and diversity scores are computed across all predictions. More recent studies have focused on the model’s ability to generate multiple responses for a single conversation, a notion sometimes called Multi‑Response Diversity. In this setting, a fixed number of responses are produced for each dialogue turn, and the resulting sets of responses are treated as individual “test sets” whose diversity scores are then averaged.

Lexical diversity metrics assess variation in word choice. Distinct‑n, introduced early in the literature, measures the number of unique n‑grams divided by the total number of n‑grams generated in the Test Set Diversity scenario and has subsequently been applied to Multi‑Response Diversity. Other approaches quantify the proportion of unique responses across a dataset, while BLEU‑based scores have been used to evaluate diversity among model outputs in the test‑set setting.

Semantic diversity metrics compare the content of responses rather than surface forms. Many of these measures adapt semantic similarity scores, interpreting lower similarity as higher diversity. BERTScore computes token‑level similarity using BERT embeddings, and Bert‑STS assigns a similarity score to sentence pairs based on contextual embeddings. The Sent‑BERT metric evaluates cosine similarity between sentence‑level BERT embeddings. Some work identifies diverse paraphrases by detecting outlier embeddings in the representation space.

Human evaluation has also been employed to assess diversity. Early experiments asked annotators to choose the more specific response given a conversational context. Subsequent studies collected rankings of dialogue responses on dimensions such as interestingness and inquisitiveness. Other investigations examined participants’ ability to judge diversity either by pairwise comparison of responses or by rating individual responses on a Likert scale, finding comparable performance across these methods and noting that humans are more sensitive to semantic than lexical diversity.

Diversity metrics have been incorporated into dialogue dataset creation pipelines. One line of work measures the diversity of crowd‑worker contributions relative to an existing corpus and uses this information to decide when to stop data collection from a worker, thereby yielding a more diverse dataset.

Reliability of diversity metrics has been explored through frameworks that introduce a “diversity parameter” (e.g., the nucleus‑sampling cutoff) to generate response sets with controllable diversity levels. These parameters serve as pseudo‑gold standards, and metric scores are evaluated based on their correlation with the parameter. Two benchmark datasets have been released for this purpose: one containing model‑generated responses with varying lexical diversity, and another comprising human‑written responses that exhibit high lexical diversity to facilitate focused evaluation of semantic diversity.

In the domain of Natural Language Inference (NLI), models are typically trained on datasets such as SNLI, MNLI, FEVER, and ANLI, and have achieved high performance with transformer architectures. NLI has been applied in dialogue systems to improve consistency between a persona and model outputs by incorporating an NLI‑based reward into reinforcement‑learning training procedures.

Generating multiple dialogue responses has attracted attention only recently. Parallel research in other areas has examined the creation of diverse hypothesis sets. For document summarization, maximal mutual relevance has been used to reduce redundancy without sacrificing relevance. In computer vision, greedy iterative algorithms have been proposed to produce diverse, probable hypotheses for multiple tasks. Similar techniques have been adapted to machine translation, where sets of translations are generated instead of a single best translation.

[ANSWER]: Fast optimization literature has long explored techniques such as constraint and variable elimination to reduce problem size and accelerate solution times, as well as problem‑specific factorizations for convex quadratic programs.  Building on these ideas, our method explicitly eliminates the degrees of freedom associated with equality constraints by training a network to output a partial solution whose codimension equals the number of equalities, and then deterministically “completes’’ the missing variables.  This variable‑elimination step is performed within the learning pipeline and is differentiable, allowing the network to focus its capacity on the truly free variables while guaranteeing feasibility with respect to all equalities—an integration of classical elimination concepts into a deep‑learning‑based optimizer.

A substantial body of deep‑learning work seeks to accelerate optimization either by learning direct mappings from problem data to full solutions (surrogate modeling) or by providing auxiliary information such as warm‑starts or active‑set predictions.  Prior surrogate approaches often suffer from infeasible or sub‑optimal outputs, whereas warm‑start and active‑set methods rely on additional optimization steps to enforce feasibility.  Our contribution occupies the surrogate‑modeling side of this spectrum but distinguishes itself by embedding a differentiable correction phase that enforces inequality constraints after equality completion.  By jointly training the network with these completion and correction modules, we achieve near‑optimal objective values while rigorously preserving feasibility, thereby addressing the primary shortcoming of earlier pure‑prediction methods.

Recent advances in implicit neural layers have shown how to embed optimization problems, differential equations, and game‑theoretic equilibria within end‑to‑end trainable architectures, often using projection‑based layers that solve a sub‑optimization problem at each forward pass.  While such layers can enforce constraints, the required inner solves are computationally intensive and limit scalability.  In contrast, our approach leverages implicit differentiation only for the equality‑completion step—when an explicit solution is unavailable (e.g., in AC optimal power flow)—and uses a lightweight, unrolled gradient descent correction for inequalities.  This design yields a tractable, fully differentiable pipeline that enforces hard constraints far more efficiently than projection‑based implicit layers, positioning our method as a scalable alternative for large‑scale, non‑convex constrained optimization.

[ANSWER]: Model selection is a classical topic in machine learning that traditionally treats each candidate model in isolation and aims to identify the model with the best predictive performance on unseen data using an offline dataset. Established techniques for estimating predictive power include cross‑validation, bootstrapping, the Akaike information criterion, and the Bayesian information criterion. Because evaluating every candidate becomes infeasible for complex problems, recent research has emphasized efficient search over large continuous or combinatorial configuration spaces. This line of work encompasses hyper‑parameter optimization, automated statistical modeling, and neural‑network architecture search, often employing Bayesian optimization or related surrogate‑model approaches to guide the search. Some studies have jointly addressed scoring and searching to improve computational efficiency.

A distinct strand of research extends model selection to online settings, where data arrive sequentially and models are continuously updated. Online model selection maintains the isolation of individual models but adapts the evaluation process to the streaming nature of the data. In reinforcement learning, models correspond to policies that are assessed by their cumulative rewards. Off‑policy evaluation methods predict the value of a new policy from data logged by a different behavior policy, providing estimators of policy value based on offline trajectories. Several works have leveraged Bayesian modeling and Bayesian optimization in this context, using Gaussian processes to model value functions and obtain policy gradients via Bayesian quadrature. Other contributions introduce Bayesian representations of belief over latent state spaces with trust‑region updates, or apply multi‑fidelity Bayesian optimization to correlate offline and online metrics for policy search. These approaches typically assume the existence of a low‑dimensional search space or a reliable offline proxy for online performance.

Research on best‑arm identification in contextual bandits also relates to model selection, albeit at a finer granularity, by focusing on the selection of actions with maximal expected reward under contextual information. Optimal experimental design offers another perspective on resource‑efficient learning, aiming to choose data collection strategies that maximize information gain. Bayesian optimal experimental design constructs predictive models of potential experimental outcomes and optimizes the expected information gain based on posterior predictions. This paradigm has been applied to diverse domains such as bioinformatics, active learning, and neuroscience, where the emphasis lies on optimizing the data acquisition process rather than directly selecting models from logged data.

[ANSWER]: Recent work on personalized machine translation has mainly relied on domain‑adaptation techniques that inject single‑dimensional user signals such as personality, gender, or politeness as auxiliary labels. Studies have demonstrated that these labels can bias translation outputs toward the desired trait, but they require explicit annotation for every user and assume that the same users appear during training and testing. Consequently, they cannot handle the realistic scenario in which a production MT system must serve a large, constantly evolving user base without pre‑collected user‑specific data. In contrast, our approach defines user behavior through readily available historical inputs and designs a zero‑shot framework that can generate personalized translations for completely unseen users, thereby overcoming the limitations of label‑based personalization and expanding applicability to real‑world online MT services.

Cache mechanisms have been introduced in both statistical and neural translation to exploit recent contextual information, typically by storing bilingual phrase pairs or document‑level representations and updating them online. These methods improve consistency across sentences within the same document but treat the cache as a short‑term memory of the current text rather than a long‑term model of individual user traits. Our contribution repurposes the cache as a persistent store of each user’s historical inputs, enabling the NMT system to retrieve and update a user‑specific portrait over time. By doing so, we shift the focus of caching from document coherence to fine‑grained, cross‑session personalization, addressing a gap left by prior document‑level cache designs.

Contrastive learning has recently been applied to NMT to strengthen semantic alignment between model outputs and reference translations or to mitigate specific error types such as word omission. These efforts treat the positive and negative samples as variations of a single sentence pair, without considering inter‑user relationships. We extend contrastive learning to the user‑driven setting by constructing positive pairs from translations associated with similar users and negative pairs from dissimilar users. This regularization explicitly encourages the model to share knowledge across users with comparable traits while preserving diversity for users with different preferences, a capability that previous contrastive NMT works do not provide.

[ANSWER]: Predicting sets with a simple multilayer perceptron (MLP) decoder has been widely adopted for tasks such as point‑cloud generation [1, 2], bounding‑box prediction [3, 4], and graph construction (sets of nodes and edges) [5, 6]. These approaches output an ordered list and treat it as if it were unordered, which gives rise to the responsibility problem. Some point‑cloud methods mitigate this issue by imposing domain‑specific assumptions, for example assuming independence of points within a set [7] or exploiting grid‑like structures [8]. To avoid the computational cost of graph‑matching losses, permutation‑invariant losses have been computed in latent space, for instance by comparing graphs adversarially [9].

Another line of work employs recurrent neural network (RNN) decoders to generate the list representation [10‑12]. When a canonical ordering can be derived from domain knowledge, the set prediction problem simplifies to a sequence problem [12]; an example is the generation of graph nodes ordered by a breadth‑first‑search traversal [13].

Iterative energy‑minimisation approaches have also been explored. Mordatch [14] introduced a method that iteratively minimises an energy function within each forward pass and differentiates through the iteration to learn network parameters. This technique has been demonstrated on small 2‑D element sets, showing the feasibility of differentiable optimisation loops for set‑structured outputs. Zhang et al. [15] constructed an auto‑encoder that pools a set into a feature vector and shares encoder information with a permutation‑equivariant decoder, thereby avoiding the responsibility problem; however, the requirement of an encoder at inference time restricts the approach to auto‑encoding rather than open‑set prediction. Greff et al. [16] proposed an auto‑encoder with a set‑structured latent space for images, enabling the discovery of latent object sets under task‑specific assumptions, which is of interest for representation learning.

The idea of using back‑propagation through an optimisation process as a decoder originates from introspective neural networks [17, 18], where the optimisation loops for prediction and weight learning are performed sequentially. Subsequent work on nested optimisation for structured outputs [19, 20] generalises this concept, allowing the outer optimisation to differentiate through the inner optimisation. Related studies also differentiate through optimisation procedures, as in [14] and [16], highlighting the broader applicability of such techniques to set‑valued outputs. In contrast, differentiating through a decoder to learn representations rather than predictions has been investigated in [21].

It is useful to distinguish vector‑to‑set settings from set‑to‑set mappings. Equivariant variants of Deep Sets [22] and self‑attention mechanisms [23] address set‑to‑set transformations, where the output ordering typically mirrors the input and the responsibility problem does not arise. Methods that predict permutation matrices for sets, effectively converting a set to a list, have been studied in [24, 25]. These approaches require an input set, whereas vector‑to‑set models are necessary for tasks such as object detection, where the input is a feature vector derived from an image encoder and must be transformed into an output set.

[ANSWER]: Recent video‑captioning approaches have progressed from simple frame‑level averaging followed by image‑captioning pipelines to more sophisticated recurrent architectures with attention. Early methods such as mean‑pooled feature encoders work for short clips but degrade on longer, untrimmed videos, prompting the adoption of RNN‑based models (e.g., S2VT) that can capture temporal dynamics. Subsequent work extended single‑sentence generation to multi‑sentence or paragraph descriptions, and some recent weakly‑supervised techniques generate diverse captions for different spatial regions. Unlike these spatially oriented weakly‑supervised methods, our approach exploits the one‑to‑one correspondence between temporal events and captions, thereby localizing events in time and producing semantically meaningful descriptions for each event rather than merely diversifying captions over visual patches.  

Dense event captioning (DEC) has been tackled by a “detect‑then‑describe’’ paradigm, where event proposals are generated by methods such as DAP or grouping‑based highlight detectors, and captions are produced by context‑aware LSTMs or attribute‑augmented models. More recent studies have attempted to jointly refine proposals using generated sentences or to replace DAP with bidirectional temporal attention mechanisms. All of these works rely on dense temporal annotations during training, treating event detection as a one‑to‑many mapping problem. In contrast, our method eliminates the need for any ground‑truth segment labels; we decompose DEC into a dual cycle of sentence localization and caption generation, enabling the model to learn event boundaries and descriptions solely from paragraph‑level captions. This weakly supervised formulation positions our work as the first to achieve DEC without explicit temporal supervision.  

Sentence‑to‑video localization has evolved from domain‑specific setups to general‑purpose models that align a query sentence with video moments. Two‑stage “scan‑and‑localize’’ frameworks, exemplified by Moment Context Networks and Cross‑modal Temporal Regression Localizers, first generate candidate clips and then match them to the query. A third line of work directly regresses temporal boundaries from joint video‑sentence embeddings. While these methods assume supervised moment annotations for training, we reinterpret sentence localization as an auxiliary task that closes the training loop for weakly supervised dense captioning. By integrating a learnable sentence localizer into our cycle‑consistency architecture, we not only provide the necessary supervision for caption generation but also obtain an unsupervised solution for sentence localization itself, thereby extending the applicability of moment‑retrieval techniques to settings without annotated temporal spans.
