[ANSWER]: Neural topic models (NTM) build on variational auto‑encoders to encode large document collections into latent vectors. Early NTM work inherited VAE techniques such as the re‑parameterization trick and neural variational inference, and explored a range of prior distributions—including Gaussian and logistic‑normal—to model document‑topic proportions. More recent studies have shifted attention toward enhancing topic coherence by explicitly optimizing coherence‑related objectives, incorporating contextual language knowledge, or injecting external signals such as sentiment or document metadata as additional inputs. Across these efforts, the overarching goal has been to produce human‑interpretable topics while preserving the expressive power of deep generative models.

Adversarial topic models (ATM) adopt a generative‑adversarial framework in which a generator maps randomly sampled document‑topic vectors to realistic document‑word distributions and a discriminator distinguishes generated samples from true documents. This adversarial training encourages the generator to capture salient semantic patterns. Extensions have introduced cycle‑consistent constraints to better align the encoder and generator, thereby improving the quality of learned representations. Nevertheless, ATM typically relies on an unconditioned prior for negative sampling and does not explicitly model relationships among documents that share similar word distributions, limiting its capacity to leverage mutual information between related texts or to incorporate auxiliary information such as sentiment.

Contrastive learning has emerged as a powerful paradigm for acquiring informative representations across a variety of domains. In computer vision, contrastive objectives have been applied to image classification, object detection, segmentation, and related tasks, often accompanied by sophisticated positive‑sample augmentations that preserve semantic content and carefully designed negative‑sampling strategies to avoid false negatives. Similar ideas have been transferred to graphs, sequential data, and adversarial training. However, the bulk of this literature focuses on modalities other than text, and there has been little work adapting contrastive objectives and sampling techniques to neural topic modeling, where the definition of positive and negative document pairs and the integration of external signals pose distinct challenges.

In contrast to these prior approaches, the present work reformulates document representation learning for neural topic models as a contrastive optimization problem that is jointly trained with the evidence lower bound. By treating the influence of positive samples as the primary driver of meaningful representations and employing an adaptive weighting scheme that balances positive and negative contributions, the method addresses the limitations of adversarial sampling in ATMs. A novel sampling strategy, inspired by human judgments of document similarity, constructs positive and negative documents through manipulation of salient word frequencies derived from reconstructed outputs, thereby enabling the seamless incorporation of external variables such as sentiment. Empirical evaluation on diverse benchmark corpora demonstrates that this contrastive framework yields superior topic coherence compared with state‑of‑the‑art neural and adversarial topic models.

[ANSWER]: Recent work has explored reconstruction from noisy measurements on high‑dimensional data.  The PriView algorithm introduced a technique for resolving inconsistencies in noisy measured marginals, guaranteeing only local consistency of the resulting distribution.  This approach has been incorporated into several subsequent mechanisms and has been shown empirically to achieve error rates comparable to other locally consistent methods, though with slightly higher error in some settings.  A notable limitation is that the method is restricted to marginal queries, whereas other frameworks allow arbitrary convex loss functions to be specified over a broader class of linear queries, facilitating integration with mechanisms such as HDMM, FEM, and MWEM.

Scalable instantiations of the MWEM algorithm have been proposed independently by Aydore et al. and Liu et al. Both lines of work avoid an explicit data‑vector representation by employing compact representations of the data distribution.  Although presented in the context of MWEM‑style algorithms, the core ideas—different approximations that mitigate the computational hardness of marginal inference—are applicable to a wider range of reconstruction problems.  These alternatives illustrate various strategies for addressing the scalability challenges inherent in private marginal estimation.

Liu et al. also investigated a variant that restricts the support of the data vector to domain elements observed in a public dataset.  This restriction yields substantial computational savings, but its performance can degrade when the public domain differs significantly from the true input domain, potentially leading to poor reconstruction quality.

Finally, Dwork et al. described an algorithm that projects onto an outer approximation of the marginal polytope using the Frank‑Wolfe optimization method.  The outer approximation is constructed through geometric techniques that differ from the local polytope used in other approaches.  Their analysis provides favorable error bounds and polynomial‑time guarantees, although the practical implementation and empirical evaluation of the method remain open questions.

[ANSWER]: Early‑exit techniques that rely on the confidence or entropy of predictions have been explored in several works. BranchyNet introduced auxiliary classifiers at intermediate layers and used an entropy‑based criterion to decide when to halt inference. FastBERT and DeeBERT extended this idea by dynamically selecting exit points based on the entropy of the soft‑max distribution, thereby reducing computation while preserving accuracy. Empirical analyses, however, have highlighted that overthinking—when a model continues processing beyond the point of sufficient information—can lower the entropy of incorrect predictions, making pure entropy thresholds brittle. Moreover, extending entropy‑based exits to regression tasks remains non‑trivial because regression outputs lack a natural categorical probability distribution from which entropy can be computed.

Consensus‑based early‑exit methods address some of the shortcomings of entropy thresholds by aggregating decisions from multiple classifier heads. PABEE proposes to monitor a sequence of consecutive classifier outputs; when a configurable patience window of heads produces identical predictions, the model exits early. LeeBERT builds on this consensus idea while incorporating self‑distillation and cross‑level optimization, encouraging lower‑layer classifiers to mimic higher‑layer behavior and improving robustness. These approaches demonstrate that agreement among diverse classifiers can serve as a reliable signal for terminating computation, and they have been applied successfully to a range of natural‑language processing benchmarks.

Variational early‑exit frameworks offer a different perspective by treating the exit layer index as a latent variable. The original Ponder architecture introduced auxiliary Lambda layers that predict, at each depth, a probability of exiting based on the current hidden representation. PonderNet formalized this idea within a variational inference setting, training the model to maximize a lower bound on the marginal likelihood of the data. During inference, the trained posterior over exit indices is sampled, allowing the model to stochastically decide when to stop processing. This probabilistic formulation provides a principled way to balance computational cost against predictive performance.

Subsequent analyses of variational exit strategies have identified two notable limitations. First, sampling from the posterior distribution at test time introduces variance in the selected exit layer, which can lead to inconsistent predictions for identical inputs. Second, the Lambda layers in the original design condition only on the hidden state of the current layer, ignoring the temporal dynamics of representations across earlier layers and the number of already‑executed steps. These factors suggest that richer contextual information may be required for more stable and accurate early‑exit decisions, especially in settings where deterministic behavior is desirable or where the task extends beyond classification.

In light of these observations, this work introduces a deterministic early‑exit mechanism that replaces stochastic sampling with a zero‑variance criterion based on the cumulative distribution function of the learned exit‑probability distribution (Q‑exit). The architecture of the auxiliary Lambda modules is revised to incorporate both the hidden states of preceding layers and an explicit encoding of the current depth, enabling the exit predictor to capture inter‑layer dynamics. The proposed method is integrated with ALBERT and RoBERTa fine‑tuning pipelines and evaluated on the GLUE benchmark. Empirical results show consistent improvements over the original PonderNet formulation and over consensus‑based methods such as PABEE, achieving higher accuracy while reducing inference latency. The deterministic exit strategy also alleviates the output variance associated with posterior sampling, making the approach applicable to both classification and regression scenarios and offering a practical avenue for adaptive computation in modern transformer‑based models.

[ANSWER]: Fair resource allocation via utility maximization has been extensively studied in economics, mechanism design, and network management. Early work introduced logarithmic utility functions to obtain Nash bargaining solutions for shared‑resource allocation problems, and later applied the same approach to communication networks. A broader class of iso‑elastic (α‑fair) utilities was proposed as a unifying framework for fairness across diverse economic settings. In time‑varying dynamical systems, Lyapunov drift analysis has become a standard tool for designing and analyzing stochastic control policies. This methodology underlies many results on wireless scheduling, multi‑user resource sharing, and stochastic game theory, where drift‑plus‑penalty techniques are used to derive online algorithms that achieve desired performance guarantees. Most of these Lyapunov‑based approaches assume that random system quantities (e.g., arrivals, channel states, rewards) are observed before each decision or that first‑ and second‑order statistics are known a priori. Consequently, they are typically formulated in discrete‑time settings where each action occupies a unit time slot. The only known continuous‑time utility‑maximization framework for fair allocation assumes knowledge of first‑order statistics and does not address learning the underlying distributions.  

The problem of online learning under resource constraints has been formalized in the bandits‑with‑knapsacks (BwK) paradigm, where each arm consumes a random amount of a limited resource while yielding a stochastic reward. This framework has been extended to handle various dynamics, including stochastic costs, multiple knapsacks, and contextual information. Several works incorporate interrupt or deadline mechanisms to capture continuous‑time dynamics within a budget‑constrained learning model, thereby allowing actions to be pre‑empted when resources are exhausted. Other extensions consider concave reward functions and convex constraints, often requiring the solution of linear programs at each round and guaranteeing vanishing constraint violations in expectation. However, these studies focus primarily on reward maximization and do not integrate fairness considerations or deadline‑based time‑efficiency mechanisms.  

In this work we develop a continuous‑time online learning framework that simultaneously addresses fairness, random completion times, and deadline constraints. By formulating the allocation problem as a utility‑maximization task, we recover reward‑maximizing, max‑min fair, and proportionally fair policies as special cases of a single utility function. We characterize the offline optimal policy that distributes a total time budget among heterogeneous groups in a provably optimal fair manner, and we show that imposing group‑dependent deadlines improves time‑efficiency. Because the offline problem is PSPACE‑hard, we derive a tractable approximation based on Lagrange duality and renewal theory, proving its asymptotic optimality. Building on these offline insights, we propose a low‑complexity online algorithm rooted in dual ascent and Lyapunov drift‑plus‑penalty techniques that learns the unknown reward‑time distributions while respecting the budget. The algorithm attains a regret bound of \(\tilde O(B^{-1/2})\) for a total time budget \(B\), thereby matching the best known rates for BwK‑type problems despite the added fairness and deadline dimensions. Our results bridge the gap between continuous‑time fair resource allocation and budget‑constrained online learning, extending the applicability of utility‑maximization and Lyapunov‑based methods to settings where actions have stochastic durations and fairness across demographic groups is paramount.

[ANSWER]: Instance‑specific explanation methods are commonly assessed through visual inspection, a practice that can be highly subjective.  Recent “sanity‑check’’ studies have demonstrated that many popular gradient‑based techniques—including guided backpropagation, gradient × input, and integrated gradients—can remain unchanged after randomizing model parameters or training data, indicating a lack of fidelity relative to vanilla input gradients.  Additional investigations using purpose‑built datasets have shown that several explanation approaches are prone to producing false‑positive attributions, whereas vanilla input gradients tend to be more reliable.  Relationships between vanilla gradients and other methods such as DeepLIFT have been identified, and recent work has linked the visual alignment of gradient‑based attributions to implicit density modeling, underscoring the importance of a deeper understanding of gradient fidelity.

A separate line of research focuses on quantitative metrics for explanation fidelity.  Many proposals measure the impact on model performance when the most or least important pixels, as identified by an explanation, are altered or removed.  However, such modification‑based metrics can conflate attribution quality with distribution shift effects introduced by masking, making it difficult to isolate the true contribution of the explanation.  Analyses have further revealed inconsistencies in ranking explanation methods when distribution‑shift penalties are applied.  The remove‑and‑retrain paradigm addresses this concern by retraining models on perturbed data, thereby accounting for distribution changes; yet studies employing this framework have found that even input gradients can underperform compared to random baselines, highlighting the need for more nuanced evaluation strategies.

Research on adversarial robustness has uncovered several side effects of adversarial training beyond increased resistance to perturbations.  Models trained with adversarial objectives have been shown to yield more transferable features, produce representations that align better with human perception, and generate input gradients with improved visual quality.  Moreover, evaluations using the remove‑and‑retrain approach have reported that the gradients of adversarially trained models exhibit markedly higher fidelity than random attributions.  These findings suggest a link between robustness and the reliability of gradient‑based explanations, motivating further analysis of how training regimes affect attribution behavior.

In this work we introduce a systematic framework for testing the core assumption that large‑magnitude input gradients reliably indicate task‑relevant features.  Building on the identified gaps in visual and modification‑based evaluations, we propose DiffROAR, a quantitative benchmark that measures attribution fidelity while explicitly controlling for distribution shift.  To enable controlled experiments with known discriminative features, we construct BlockMNIST, a semi‑real dataset that encodes ground‑truth signal coordinates.  Using DiffROAR, we reveal a phenomenon we term feature inversion in gradients (FIG), whereby standard models trained on natural data assign higher gradient magnitudes to irrelevant features, violating the assumed correspondence between gradient magnitude and importance.  Conversely, we show that adversarially robust models eliminate FIG, a behavior we label robustness‑fixes‑FIG (RF‑FIG).  Complementing empirical results on CIFAR‑10, ImageNet‑10, and BlockMNIST, we provide theoretical guarantees on a simplified version of the synthetic dataset, proving that standard one‑hidden‑layer networks inevitably exhibit FIG while adversarial training enforces RF‑FIG.  Together, these contributions offer concrete sanity checks for gradient‑based interpretability methods and clarify the role of adversarial robustness in improving attribution fidelity.

[ANSWER]: Commonsense knowledge has become a focal point in natural‑language processing because it often fills gaps left by explicitly stated information. Early studies demonstrated its utility for open‑ended generation tasks such as story explanation [1], story ending [2], and abductive reasoning [3], highlighting that models equipped with commonsense can produce more coherent and plausible outputs.

A number of approaches have leveraged publicly available commonsense knowledge bases (CSKBs) to enhance downstream tasks. ConceptNet [9] is among the most widely used resources; it encodes relational triples between concepts and has been integrated into systems for sentence ordering [4], emotion recognition [5], story generation [6, 7], and dialogue generation [8]. Despite its broad coverage, ConceptNet is context‑free: the stored relations do not take into account the surrounding discourse, limiting its effectiveness for inference that depends on conversational context.

To address the contextual limitation of static CSKBs, generative models such as COMET [10] have been proposed. COMET learns to produce plausible commonsense statements conditioned on a textual prompt, thereby enabling inference at the sentence level. Nevertheless, COMET is trained on existing knowledge bases and therefore inherits their lack of dialogue‑specific detail, which can impede performance on tasks that require fine‑grained, turn‑by‑turn reasoning.

Several curated datasets target commonsense inference in specific textual settings. ATOMIC (Sap 2019; Hwang 2020) provides a large collection of inferential triples spanning multiple relation types, but its instances are isolated phrases without surrounding dialogue, making it unsuitable for reasoning that relies on multi‑turn context. GLUCOSE (Mostafazadeh 2020) focuses exclusively on causal relations (cause, enable, result) derived from monologic narratives, and does not cover dialogue‑oriented dimensions such as motivation or emotional reaction. CIDER (Ghosal 2021) introduces commonsense inference annotations for dialogues, yet limits itself to inferences that are explicitly observable in the conversation, thereby restricting the ability of models to speculate about implicit information.

The existing resources collectively illustrate a progression from generic, context‑independent knowledge graphs toward more specialized corpora that incorporate discourse structure. However, gaps remain in providing dialogue‑centric, context‑aware commonsense annotations that span a diverse set of reasoning types and support both explicit and implicit inference.

In this work, we introduce a new dialogue‑focused dataset that combines the strengths of prior resources while addressing their shortcomings. The dataset comprises over fifty thousand utterance‑level inferences drawn from thousands of dyadic conversations, covering five reasoning categories—cause, subsequent event, prerequisite, motivation, and listener emotional reaction. By grounding each inference in its surrounding dialogue, the collection enables models to reason with contextual commonsense, including speculative inference where the answer is not directly stated. Experiments on generative and discriminative tasks demonstrate that the dataset substantially improves performance on dialogue reasoning benchmarks, underscoring its value for advancing commonsense‑driven conversational AI.

[ANSWER]: Selective rationalization has been pioneered by the generator‑predictor framework that couples a binary rationale selector with a downstream predictor [1]. Subsequent work has enriched this paradigm with game‑theoretic objectives that promote desirable properties such as Shapley‑based scores [2], comprehensiveness [3], multi‑aspect support [4, 5] and invariance [6]. A parallel line of research has focused on alleviating the notorious training difficulties of hard selection by replacing policy‑gradient with Gumbel‑softmax [7], pre‑training the predictor before end‑to‑end fine‑tuning [3], altering the update dynamics between generator and predictor [5], or employing rectified continuous random variables for constrained optimization [8]. More recent proposals have added adversarial information calibration to a black‑box predictor [9]. While these advances improve stability or predictive performance, they do not address the fundamental “model interlocking” phenomenon that we uncover: the predictor’s over‑fitting to the generator’s current selections reinforces sub‑optimal rationales and traps both components in a poor equilibrium. Our A2R framework directly tackles this gap by introducing a soft‑attention predictor that provides a convex training signal and a gap‑minimization objective that forces the hard‑selection generator to align with the more reliable soft predictor, thereby breaking the interlocking loop.

Attention mechanisms have long been used as a convenient proxy for model explanations [10‑12]. Although easy to obtain, raw attention weights lack faithfulness because low‑weight inputs can still exert strong influence on predictions, and multiple attention patterns can yield identical outputs [13‑17]. To mitigate these issues, recent studies have regularized hidden representations [18, 19] or weighted losses of predefined rationale candidates [20] to improve attention fidelity, while sparsemax‑based sparsity [21, 22] seeks to make attention resemble hard rationales [23]. Nevertheless, the underlying loss landscape remains concave, and the predictor still underfits inputs receiving zero attention, perpetuating the same interlocking problem identified in selective rationalization. In contrast, our method leverages attention not as a final explanation but as an auxiliary training signal: the soft‑attention predictor supplies a convex objective that guides the generator toward better rationales, while the final hard‑selection predictor preserves the certification of exclusion that pure attention cannot provide.

Beyond selective rationalization, a variety of interpretability frameworks have been proposed. Module‑network approaches [24‑26] compose neural modules according to logical programs, but they require predefined modules and programs, limiting scalability. Gradient‑based feature importance methods [13, 27‑29] and post‑hoc techniques such as local perturbations [30, 31], locally interpretable surrogate models [32, 33], and contrastive input edits [34] offer flexible explanations yet cannot enforce structural constraints or guarantee that unselected features truly have no effect. Our contribution occupies a distinct niche: we retain the structural guarantees of selective rationalization (certification of exclusion) while overcoming its optimization pathology through a hybrid architecture that combines soft attention’s convexity with hard selection’s faithfulness. This positions A2R as a scalable, theoretically grounded alternative to both traditional rationalization pipelines and post‑hoc explanation methods.

[ANSWER]: Various post‑hoc interpretation techniques have been proposed to shed light on the decisions of black‑box NLP models. Existing approaches are commonly grouped into three families: gradient‑based methods that exploit local derivatives of the prediction function, reference‑based methods that compare model outputs against a baseline input, and perturbation‑based methods that query the model on altered versions of the original text. While these families have advanced our ability to assign token‑level importance scores, they remain largely disconnected from the literature on adversarial robustness. Our work bridges this gap by introducing a new class of robustness‑based interpretation methods that leverage adversarial attack and certification techniques. By doing so, we unify the strengths of gradient‑based sensitivity with reference‑based stability, offering explanations that are both theoretically grounded and empirically resilient to small perturbations.

A substantial body of research has focused on evaluating the faithfulness of explanations. The dominant paradigm in NLP employs removal‑based criteria, wherein tokens deemed important are masked or deleted and the resulting change in model confidence is measured. Complementary lines of work have examined axiom compliance, plausibility to human judgments, and the use of diagnostic suites that combine multiple tests. Recent studies have also highlighted the fragility of explanations by probing their robustness, yet they have not formalized systematic metrics that capture how explanations behave under controlled adversarial changes. Building on these insights, we propose two novel, complementary faithfulness criteria—sensitivity and stability—that directly address the limitations of removal‑based evaluations. Sensitivity quantifies how much a model’s output changes when important tokens are perturbed adversarially rather than removed, preserving sentence structure and contextual interactions. Stability measures the consistency of explanations across semantically similar inputs generated via black‑box adversarial word substitution. By positioning our metrics alongside existing evaluation frameworks, we demonstrate that many prior explanations that perform well on removal‑based tests can fail under our more nuanced notions, thereby redefining what it means for an explanation to be faithful.

Interpretation research has predominantly targeted classification and sequence labeling tasks, with far fewer studies addressing structured prediction problems such as dependency parsing. Existing removal‑based evaluation protocols are ill‑suited for parsing because deleting tokens disrupts the tree structure, rendering the metric unreliable. To fill this gap, we introduce a novel paradigm for interpreting dependency parsers that leverages prepositional‑phrase attachment ambiguities as a probing mechanism. This paradigm enables us to apply both our sensitivity and stability metrics to parsing models, marking the first systematic study of explanation faithfulness for dependency parsing. By extending interpretability analysis to structured outputs, our work not only broadens the applicability of interpretation methods but also validates the relevance of our proposed metrics across diverse NLP tasks.

[ANSWER]: Learning from demonstrations (LfD) traditionally relies on behavior cloning, which directly regresses a policy onto state‑action pairs from expert trajectories. While simple, this approach suffers from distribution shift and compounding errors. Subsequent works introduced no‑regret algorithms such as DAgger and inverse‑reinforcement‑learning (IRL) methods that infer a reward function before applying standard RL. A prominent IRL‑based method, Generative Adversarial Imitation Learning (GAIL), casts imitation as a distribution‑matching problem and uses a GAN‑style discriminator to minimize the Jensen‑Shannon divergence between expert and learner state‑action distributions. Although GAIL and its many variants have spurred considerable progress, they remain fundamentally on‑policy: each policy update requires fresh interactions with the environment, which is sample‑inefficient and limits applicability in settings where data collection is costly. Our work inherits the distribution‑matching perspective of GAIL but departs from its on‑policy requirement by deriving an off‑policy upper bound that can be optimized using replay buffers, thereby achieving principled sample efficiency without sacrificing theoretical guarantees.

Off‑policy extensions of GAIL, such as DAC and related replay‑buffer approaches, improve sample reuse by training on previously collected transitions. However, these methods typically ignore the distributional drift introduced when substituting the on‑policy state‑action distribution with an off‑policy approximation, leading to a mismatch between the optimized objective and the true imitation goal. ValueDICE further addresses off‑policy learning by reformulating the LfD objective via density‑ratio estimation, yet it explicitly requires expert actions to construct the required importance weights. Consequently, existing off‑policy techniques either lack rigorous justification for the distribution shift or cannot be applied when expert actions are unavailable. In contrast, our method establishes a provable bound that eliminates the need for expert actions and explicitly accounts for the off‑policy bias, enabling reliable off‑policy learning in the observation‑only setting.

Model‑free learning from observations (LfO) removes the action component from expert data and extends the GAIL framework to a state‑only discriminator (GAIfO). Subsequent analyses (e.g., IDDM) quantify the theoretical gap between LfD and LfO objectives and propose mutual‑information regularization to narrow this gap, achieving performance comparable to GAIL but still relying on on‑policy sampling. Other model‑free LfO approaches design ranking‑based reward functions or employ adversarial objectives, yet they inherit the same sample‑inefficiency as their LfD counterparts. Our contribution builds on the model‑free LfO literature by preserving the adversarial distribution‑matching formulation while introducing a novel regularizer that promotes mode‑covering from the learner’s perspective. This regularizer is instantiated via an inverse action model, which can be learned offline and used to guide policy updates without additional environment interaction, thus markedly improving sample efficiency.

Model‑based LfO methods either learn a forward dynamics model or an inverse action model to reconstruct missing actions before applying behavior cloning. Forward‑model approaches often require a separate policy for each time step, making them unsuitable for long‑horizon or infinite‑horizon tasks. Inverse‑action techniques (e.g., BCO) infer actions from observations and then clone the resulting state‑action pairs, but they rely on strong assumptions such as deterministic, injective dynamics to guarantee accurate action recovery. Moreover, they do not directly address the distribution‑matching objective underlying modern imitation learning. Our approach leverages an inverse action model not as a preprocessing step but as a regularizer that aligns the learner’s state distribution with the expert’s, under a mild deterministic‑environment assumption. This integration preserves the flexibility of model‑free adversarial methods while benefiting from the additional structure provided by the inverse model, without imposing restrictive dynamics assumptions.

In summary, our method—Off‑Policy Learning from Observations (OPOLO)—unifies the strengths of distribution‑matching imitation learning, off‑policy sample reuse, and inverse‑action regularization. By deriving an off‑policy upper bound that dispenses with expert actions and by employing an inverse model to enforce mode‑covering, OPOLO achieves state‑of‑the‑art performance on challenging locomotion benchmarks with substantially reduced sample complexity. This positions our work as the first principled, sample‑efficient LfO algorithm that combines theoretical rigor with practical effectiveness.

[ANSWER]: Prior work has introduced a variety of reinforcement‑learning benchmarks that are often explicitly episodic [1]–[3] or consist of games that become implicitly episodic after a player dies or completes the game [4], [5]. Within the episodic setting, benchmarks have been employed to study orthogonal questions such as multi‑task learning [4], [6], sequential task learning [7], generalization [8], and multi‑agent learning [9], [10]. A notable recent benchmark, Jelly Bean World [11], provides a collection of procedurally generated grid‑world tasks and also considers a non‑episodic formulation.

The challenge of enabling embodied agents to learn continually with minimal human intervention has motivated several sub‑areas of reinforcement learning. Continual and lifelong learning literature investigates episodic learning in sequential Markov decision processes [12]–[15] and focuses on objectives such as forward and backward transfer or adaptation to non‑stationary dynamics [16]–[24]. Unsupervised reinforcement learning approaches aim to acquire skills without external reward signals, thereby reducing the need for manually designed reward functions [25]–[29].

Reset‑free reinforcement learning has been explored with an emphasis on safety [30], automated and unattended real‑world learning [31]–[33], skill discovery [34], [24], and curriculum generation [35]. Methods for learning without resets include training a backward reset controller [30], learning auxiliary tasks that serve as approximate resets [36], [33], and employing novelty‑seeking reset controllers [32]. These lines of work collectively provide a foundation for evaluating algorithms that operate without explicit episode boundaries.

[ANSWER]: Causal perspectives in machine learning have attracted considerable attention in recent years. The literature distinguishes two broad strands: causal discovery, which seeks to uncover the underlying causal graph among variables, and causal inference, which focuses on estimating treatment effects from observational or experimental data. Methods for causal discovery typically rely on conditional independence tests, score‑based search, or constraint‑based algorithms to recover the structure of the causal model, whereas causal inference approaches aim to construct estimators for average or individual treatment effects once a causal graph has been assumed or learned.

A traditional line of work on counterfactual estimation assumes that the data‑generating process can be captured by a specific structural causal model (SCM) or by a particular functional form of the structural equations. By fixing the functional relationships, these methods obtain identifiability of counterfactuals. Synthetic control methods exemplify this strategy by positing a linear factor model for the untreated potential outcomes, which enables a closed‑form reconstruction of the counterfactual trajectory. While such approaches provide strong theoretical guarantees, their reliance on restrictive structural assumptions limits their applicability to complex, nonlinear, or high‑dimensional settings.

More recent research has explored deep‑learning‑based counterfactual estimators that replace explicit parametric structural equations with flexible neural networks. These models aim to capture rich, nonlinear relationships in high‑dimensional data such as images or multivariate time series. However, without additional constraints, they generally lack identifiability guarantees, as the learned representations may fit the observed factual distribution without correctly recovering the unobserved counterfactual distribution. In the discrete‑variable setting, identifiability has been pursued through assumptions such as monotonicity or its extensions, which enable consistent counterfactual inference for binary or categorical treatments. These discrete‑case results do not directly transfer to continuous treatments or high‑dimensional observational spaces, leaving a gap in the literature for methods that combine expressive deep models with provable counterfactual recovery.

In this work we bridge the gap between classical SCM‑based identifiability and modern deep‑learning architectures. By assuming continuous treatments and outcomes together with a small set of categorical latent background variables that dominate treatment response, we demonstrate that counterfactuals can be reliably reconstructed using arbitrary parametric functions, including deep neural networks, to model causal dependencies. We provide theoretical analysis quantifying the discrepancy between the recovered and true counterfactual distributions under these assumptions, and we introduce a practical algorithm that leverages this insight for high‑dimensional data. Empirical evaluations on synthetic and real-world time‑series and image datasets show that the proposed method markedly outperforms existing deep counterfactual estimators, delivering both improved accuracy and theoretical guarantees of identifiability in settings where prior approaches either impose overly restrictive structural forms or lack applicability to continuous, high‑dimensional domains.

ANSWER: Previous studies on TDMR (Task-Dataset-Metric-Result) extraction primarily rely on data sources such as NLP-progress or PapersWithCode. While these sources are popular, they lack rigorous quality control, including standardized TDM entity representation and complete publication coverage. Some efforts, such as [1] and [2], go further by extracting TDM triples and result values, and normalizing them for leaderboard construction. However, these approaches are constrained by their reliance on a closed domain, requiring extracted triples to match a pre-defined TDM taxonomy. Other studies, such as [3] and [4], only extract TDM triples without result values, thereby forgoing leaderboard construction. Meanwhile, [6] focuses on extracting result values, but only in the context of pre-defined TDM triples, making it similar in limitation to [1]. Consequently, these methods are not adaptable to the dynamic nature of emerging benchmarks, where new tasks, datasets, and metrics are frequently introduced.
In a parallel research thread, scientific leaderboards have been treated as a form of scientific knowledge graphs composed of Task, Dataset, and Metric entities. Information extraction in this context has been widely studied, as seen in works such as [5, 7, 8, 9, 10]. These studies typically focus on linking scientific content to structured entities but often assume that the schema or triples are known in advance.
In contrast to prior work, our approach addresses several critical limitations. We construct our dataset manually from publications to ensure comprehensive TDMR annotations, rather than relying on incomplete or unstandardized third-party sources. We normalize TDMR tuples for accurate leaderboard construction and propose novel experimental settings that reflect realistic, dynamic scenarios, including cold-start conditions without any pre-defined TDM taxonomy. By simultaneously constructing a scientific knowledge graph and linking publications to it in an open-domain setting, our method is inherently adaptable to the evolving research landscape.

SciREX [7] extends information extraction to the document level, capturing relationships that span across sections, which is critical for leaderboard generation from full papers.

[ANSWER]: The idea of representing data with multiple variables that each convey distinct information dates back to the 1980s, when researchers examined how intelligent systems such as the human brain should code information.  The minimum‑entropy coding principle proposed that data be represented by random variables with minimal marginal entropies, thereby reducing redundancy in sensory stimuli.  Representations that minimize total correlation—often called factorial codes—emerged from this principle and have been shown to be useful for unsupervised learning.  Recent advances in disentangled representation learning based on variational autoencoders have leveraged regularization of the latent‑variable total correlation, a practice that can be interpreted as a modern incarnation of the minimum‑entropy coding idea.

From a generative standpoint, many data sources are assumed to arise from a set of underlying generative factors that become entangled in a high‑dimensional sensory space.  Because these factors are valuable for downstream tasks, a large body of work has focused on learning representations that recover them.  Early efforts established a standard evaluation protocol that uses controlled datasets with balanced, fully annotated ground‑truth factors.  Building on this protocol, a variety of disentanglement metrics have been proposed.  Some methods assess the deviation of each latent variable conditioned on a specific generative factor, which captures geometric relationships but is sensitive to latent‑space scaling and lacks a unified formulation across heterogeneous variable types.  Other approaches, such as the Mutual Information Gap (MIG), rely on pairwise mutual information between latents and factors, providing an information‑theoretic perspective while still considering only two‑variable interactions.

A complementary line of research investigates redundancy, synergy, and uniqueness of information shared among multiple random variables.  The Partial Information Decomposition (PID) framework formalizes this by splitting the mutual information that a set of variables holds about a target into non‑negative redundant, unique, and synergistic components.  Initial formulations of PID offered concrete definitions for these components, but subsequent analyses highlighted limitations—most notably an tendency to overestimate redundancy.  Consequently, several alternative definitions and refinements have been introduced, and the quest for robust, universally applicable PID measures remains an active area of study.

PID has also been applied to the analysis of machine‑learning models.  For example, studies have measured PID components in restricted Boltzmann machines by decomposing the mutual information between hidden representations and class labels, typically using early PID definitions for computational convenience despite their known shortcomings.  Other investigations have employed linear combinations of mutual‑information terms to approximate PID quantities in supervised convolutional networks, using these estimates to compare information flow across architectural variants and hyperparameter settings.  These works illustrate how PID can illuminate learning dynamics, though they have primarily focused on supervised settings rather than unsupervised representation learning.

In this work we introduce an information‑theoretic framework for evaluating disentanglement that directly leverages PID to capture interactions among any number of latent variables.  By focusing on the unique‑information component, we derive tractable lower and upper bounds that depend only on mutual‑information terms, making the bounds compatible with any concrete PID definition.  We further construct analogous bounds for redundancy and synergy, enabling a fine‑grained analysis of how representations encode generative factors.  To assess the behavior of these metrics, we design an experimental protocol that subjects representations to controlled “entanglement attacks” injecting redundancy or synergy, thereby unit‑testing the metrics’ sensitivity.  Applying the framework to variational autoencoders, we demonstrate that models with comparable traditional disentanglement scores can exhibit markedly different patterns of redundancy and synergy, indicating that distinct strategies may be required to obtain truly disentangled representations.  Overall, our contributions provide a unified, multivariate perspective on disentanglement, bridge the gap between pairwise‑based metrics and full‑information decompositions, and offer practical tools for diagnosing and improving unsupervised representation learning.

[ANSWER]: The brain’s capacity for Bayesian inference—combining prior knowledge with uncertain sensory observations—has been extensively investigated in neuroscience (e.g., [1]–[4]). Formal accounts emphasize the optimal integration of prior information from past experience or contextual cues with the likelihood of current sensory inputs ([5]–[8]). Parallel lines of research on efficient coding focus on the representation of sensory observations themselves, aiming to maximize information transmission under constraints imposed by limited biological resources ([9]–[13]). Predictive coding frameworks address similar constraints by employing top‑down feedback predictions to suppress already‑known components of the sensory signal, thereby transmitting only the novel residual to lower‑level processing stages ([14]–[20]). Recent work has highlighted the relevance of predictive feedback for understanding and improving deep‑learning algorithms, generating substantial interest across computational neuroscience and machine‑learning communities ([21]–[28]).

Integrative efforts have sought to bridge these theories. Park et al. ([29]) combined Bayesian inference and efficient coding by showing that optimal neural codes can be derived from posterior distributions while respecting limits on firing rates. Chalk et al. ([17]) unified predictive coding, efficient coding, and sparse coding within a three‑dimensional constraint space defined by channel capacity, the amount of past information about the world state, and the temporal target of the estimate. Młynarski et al. ([30]) examined adaptive sensory coding that balances inference costs against metabolic expenses required for high‑fidelity encoding. Aitchison et al. ([31]) argued that predictive coding can implement Bayesian inference, though it is not a necessary condition. Collectively, these studies advance the understanding of optimal encoding strategies under various resource constraints, though many assume costless computation and often omit explicit considerations of constraints on feedback pathways.

[ANSWER]: Model learning has been leveraged in reinforcement learning for a variety of purposes. Early work demonstrated that learned models can serve as auxiliary tasks that improve representation learning, enabling agents to extract richer features from raw observations (e.g., [2]–[6]). More directly, models have been employed for planning, allowing agents to improve policy optimisation and value estimation by simulating future trajectories (e.g., [7]–[10]). Local planning methods that use short‑horizon rollouts for action selection have also been explored (e.g., [11], [12]). Comprehensive surveys outline the diverse ways in which models are incorporated into RL pipelines ([13], [14]).

The majority of model‑learning approaches are grounded in maximum‑likelihood estimation (MLE), fitting transition and reward predictors to observed experience (e.g., [15]–[18]). Notably, deep generative models have been shown to enable “Dream‑like” learning, where policies and value functions are trained entirely within imagined rollouts and achieve performance comparable to model‑free baselines on Atari domains ([19]).

A complementary line of research has focused on learning models that are explicitly aligned with control‑relevant quantities. RL‑informed objectives have been introduced to bias model learning toward aspects of the environment that matter for decision making ([20]–[27], [6]). This perspective connects to theoretical developments on “value‑aware” model learning, which study conditions under which model updates preserve Bellman equivalence for worst‑case functions ([28]) and for sequences of value functions ([29]). The broader “value equivalence” (VE) principle formalises when two models induce identical Bellman operators over arbitrary function classes ([30]), providing a unifying framework for many recent model‑learning methods.

Several works have examined self‑consistency as a learning signal. In the context of Markov reward processes, self‑consistency updates have been used to improve temporally abstract models by enforcing agreement between imagined and real dynamics ([21]). Offline multi‑task inverse‑reinforcement learning approaches have also incorporated self‑consistency losses to align inferred reward structures across tasks ([31]). Outside reinforcement learning, cyclic consistency ideas appear in back‑translation for neural machine translation ([32], [33]) and in CycleGANs for image‑to‑image translation ([34]).

Model architectures are not limited to forward dynamics. Backward models that propagate credit backwards in time have been proposed for improved credit assignment ([35], [36]). Recent concurrent work has introduced a forward–backward system in which the two models are trained to be cyclically consistent, highlighting the flexibility of consistency‑based objectives in model‑based reinforcement learning ([37]).

In this paper we introduce a set of self‑consistency updates that jointly adjust a learned forward model and the accompanying value function so that they satisfy the Bellman equation with respect to each other. Building on the MLE‑based and value‑aware foundations, our method leverages imagined experience to regularise both components, rather than treating the model as a fixed target for value updates. We develop several variants, identify a semi‑gradient temporal‑difference formulation that reliably improves both policy evaluation and control, and investigate search‑control strategies that determine which states and actions are used in the consistency updates. Empirical evaluation spans tabular domains and deep RL benchmarks, including Atari, Sokoban, and Go, where the proposed updates yield notable gains in sample efficiency. By comparing against pure model‑based planning, value‑aware learning, and backward‑model approaches, we demonstrate that joint model‑value self‑consistency provides a complementary mechanism that enhances learning without requiring additional real‑world interactions.

[ANSWER]: Regression is a fundamental problem studied across many disciplines. In the nonparametric iid setting, [1] shows that only minimal assumptions are required for one‑sided isometry, which enables learning. Building on this intuition, [2] provide mixing‑free rates for linear regression over trajectories generated by a linear dynamical system. The technical developments in the literature synthesize ideas from two main lines of work: nonparametric regression with iid data and learning from dependent data.  

Beyond the seminal result of [1], the papers [3], [4], and [5] study iid regression with square loss under various moment‑equivalence conditions. The notion of offset Rademacher complexity, introduced by [6] for iid regression, is further developed in a martingale setting by [7], where a martingale analogue of the offset complexity characterizes the rate of the least‑squares estimator.  

For learning from dependent data, many results reduce the problem to independent learning via the blocking technique [8]; this approach incurs a sample‑complexity penalty proportional to the mixing time. A lower bound for linear regression in a worst‑case agnostic model is proved by [9], showing that such a penalty is unavoidable. In the realizable linear‑regression setting, [9] also give matching upper and lower bounds, demonstrating that the mixing time influences only the burn‑in period and not the final risk; the upper bound is obtained for a specific modification of stochastic gradient descent. An information‑theoretic analysis of the nonparametric least‑squares estimator appears in [7]; the approach relies on two‑sided concentration and consequently yields bounds that depend on the mixing time.  

Extensions of the results in [1] to dependent data are presented in [10]. By following Mendelson’s argument, these works handle non‑realizability and heavy‑tailed noise, but the proofs still depend on two‑sided concentration for both the version space and the noise interaction, leading to rates that degrade for slower‑mixing processes. This degradation aligns with the lower bounds established in [9] for the non‑realizable case.  

The measure of dependence used for processes such as \({{formula:182771ac-fbac-45d8-9530-370d4695711a}}\) originates in [11]. A similar dependence measure is employed by [12] to study learning when covariates lack an obvious sequential ordering, for example in graph‑structured or Ising‑model data; their risk bounds deteriorate as the correlation measure increases.  

In linear system identification, the lack of ergodicity does not degrade parameter‑recovery rates, as shown in [2], [13], [14], [15], [16], and [17]. Beyond linear systems, parameter‑recovery bounds for dynamical systems driven by generalized linear‑model transitions are provided in [18], [19], [20], and [21]. Notably, [20] and [21] demonstrate that non‑ergodicity does not impair rates, with [21] achieving semiparametric results even when the link function is unknown.

[ANSWER]: Traditional recommender systems that rely solely on user‑item rating matrices often suffer from severe data sparsity, which limits recommendation accuracy. To mitigate this issue, researchers have incorporated auxiliary signals such as social relations, textual reviews, geographic locations, and temporal dynamics, demonstrating consistent performance gains. More recently, cross‑platform and cross‑domain recommendation approaches have been proposed to leverage complementary information from multiple sources, aiming to alleviate sparsity by sharing user or item representations across platforms. While these methods improve accuracy, they typically assume unrestricted data sharing and therefore do not address the practical challenge of data isolation between independent services.

Privacy‑preserving techniques have been explored to enable collaborative recommendation without exposing raw data. Garbled‑circuit protocols have been applied to secure matrix factorization, offering strong cryptographic guarantees but incurring prohibitive computational overhead. Homomorphic‑encryption‑based solutions for federated matrix factorization allow arithmetic on encrypted data, yet often rely on a semi‑honest server and lack formal security proofs. Differential‑privacy mechanisms have been employed to protect user attributes such as location in transfer‑learning scenarios, but the injected noise degrades model utility and the guarantees are not composable with other cryptographic primitives. Collectively, these approaches illustrate a trade‑off between privacy, efficiency, and recommendation quality.

Secure social recommendation specifically targets the integration of user‑user social graphs with rating data. The SeSoRec framework introduced a protocol that computes social regularization terms while reducing communication costs; however, it achieves efficiency by revealing aggregated rows or columns of the social matrix, which can leak fine‑grained relationship information, especially when social ties are binary. Moreover, SeSoRec treats the social adjacency matrix as dense, leading to substantial overhead in realistic settings where social graphs are extremely sparse (densities often below one percent). Subsequent work on secure computation over sparse data, such as the ROOM framework, provides protocols for column‑ or row‑sparse matrices but requires pre‑generated correlated randomness and is limited to specific sparsity patterns. Other proposals combine homomorphic encryption with secret sharing to multiply a sparse matrix by a dense one, yet their performance deteriorates when the dense operand is large.

The most closely related line of research focuses on designing secure matrix multiplication primitives that are aware of sparsity. Techniques that exploit private information retrieval (PIR) enable hiding the locations of non‑zero entries, while homomorphic encryption supports arithmetic on ciphertexts without decryption. By integrating these primitives, recent protocols have achieved lower communication complexity for sparse‑matrix operations under both insensitive and sensitive sparsity assumptions, where either the pattern of non‑zeros or merely the count of zeros is public. These advances lay the groundwork for efficient, privacy‑preserving collaborative learning on highly sparse social data.

In this work we introduce a sparsity‑aware secure cross‑platform social recommendation framework that builds upon the classic social regularization model. Our contributions are threefold: (1) we devise a general protocol for securely computing the social regularization term between a rating platform and a social platform under a semi‑honest threat model; (2) we propose two novel secure sparse matrix multiplication protocols—one for insensitive sparsity, where both parties know the non‑zero locations, and one for sensitive sparsity, where only the density is public—leveraging PIR and homomorphic encryption to avoid reliance on pre‑generated correlated randomness; and (3) we empirically demonstrate that the proposed framework attains recommendation accuracy comparable to existing social recommendation models while reducing computation time and communication volume by large margins relative to the state‑of‑the‑art secure solution, thereby addressing both the privacy and efficiency challenges identified in prior work.

[ANSWER]: Recent work has extended the highly generalizable Vision Transformer (ViT) architecture from image to video domains. TimeSformer introduced four factorized space‑time variants that retain the standard ViT structure while separating spatial and temporal attention. VTN employed a temporal‑only attention module to aggregate information across all clips of a video. MViT built a multi‑scale design by integrating pooling and down‑sampling techniques originally used in image backbones. ViViT proposed three factorized space‑time attention variants, leveraging tubelet embedding to lift image patches into the temporal dimension. Video Swin adapted the hierarchical Swin‑Transformer to video by incorporating shifted windows in both spatial and temporal axes, further demonstrating the versatility of transformer‑based video models.

Early video‑action‑recognition research relied on hand‑crafted descriptors such as dense trajectories (DT) and improved dense trajectories (iDT). The rise of deep learning shifted the focus toward learnable video representations. Two‑stream architectures combined 2‑D CNNs on RGB frames with separate streams for optical flow, while 3‑D CNNs directly modeled spatiotemporal convolutions. Variants of these approaches incorporated recurrent units (LSTM, GRU, RNN) to capture longer‑range dynamics. More recently, transformer‑based designs have supplanted many convolutional models, with ViT‑inspired frameworks such as VTN, TimeSformer, ViViT, and Video Swin employing attention mechanisms to encode temporal relationships and achieve state‑of‑the‑art performance on action‑recognition benchmarks.

Modeling temporal correspondences remains a central challenge in video understanding. Optical‑flow methods estimate pixel‑wise displacements between successive frames, providing explicit motion cues that can be fused with appearance features. Patch‑level matching approaches reduce the memory burden of dense flow; for example, SIFT‑flow matches SIFT descriptors across patches, while classical local descriptors such as HOG and MBH capture statistical motion patterns. Correlation‑based techniques that align patches or other inter‑frame components have been shown to improve the quality of visual representations learned by deep models.

Building on these strands of research, this work proposes an Alignment‑guided Temporal Attention (ATA) module that reconciles the efficiency of factorized spatial‑temporal designs with the interaction richness of joint 3‑D approaches. By explicitly aligning semantically similar patches between neighboring frames using a parameter‑free matching step based on cosine similarity and the Kuhn‑Munkres algorithm, ATA increases the mutual information shared across frame representations, thereby enriching task‑relevant signals without incurring the high computational cost of fully global 3‑D operations. A theoretical analysis demonstrates that such frame‑by‑frame alignment maximizes the lower bound of mutual information between adjacent frame embeddings. The resulting temporal attention mechanism retains the lightweight nature of factorized models, introduces no additional learnable parameters, and can be seamlessly inserted into a wide range of image backbones—including TimeSformer, CycleMLP, and ConvNeXt—effectively turning them into video models. Extensive experiments on multiple action‑recognition benchmarks validate the superior accuracy‑efficiency trade‑off of ATA, confirming its utility as a general plug‑and‑play component that bridges image‑focused architectures to video‑centric tasks.

[ANSWER]: Machine‑learning–based MRI reconstruction has progressed rapidly, yet most studies focus on improving quantitative metrics such as SSIM or PSNR rather than on artifacts that affect clinical interpretation. Early deep‑learning approaches demonstrated superior reconstruction quality compared with classical penalized least‑squares methods (e.g., Hammernik 2018; Schlemper 2018; Aggarwal 2017). Subsequent work has highlighted the emergence of structured artifacts—particularly streak‑like or banding patterns—that appear in Cartesian‑accelerated reconstructions, especially in low‑signal‑to‑noise regions. These artifacts have been reported as a key barrier to clinical adoption, but the literature contains few systematic analyses of their origin or mitigation strategies.

Banding artifacts originating from balanced steady‑state free‑precession (bSSFP) sequences have been investigated using both physics‑based models and supervised deep‑learning techniques. Physics‑driven methods exploit knowledge of the bSSFP signal equation to suppress banding by modifying acquisition parameters or applying post‑processing filters. Supervised neural networks have been trained on paired data to learn a mapping from banded to artifact‑free images. While effective for bSSFP‑related banding, these approaches rely on sequence‑specific signal characteristics and therefore do not directly address the banding that arises from one‑dimensional k‑space subsampling in accelerated Cartesian MRI.

Adversarial learning has become a popular tool for enhancing realism in medical image synthesis and reconstruction. Generative adversarial networks (GANs) have been employed for tasks such as organ segmentation, magnetic resonance angiography generation, super‑resolution, and the creation of anonymized datasets. In the context of MRI reconstruction, adversarial losses have been incorporated to encourage reconstructions to resemble fully sampled ground‑truth images. However, applying such penalties indiscriminately can introduce spurious anatomical details, raising concerns about diagnostic fidelity. Recent studies therefore emphasize careful design of adversarial objectives to balance realism with preservation of true anatomical information.

In contrast to prior work, the present study introduces an orientation‑aware adversarial loss that specifically targets banding structures without requiring ground‑truth annotations. By training the discriminator solely on reconstructed images and conditioning it on the phase‑encoding direction, the method penalizes anisotropic streaks while leaving genuine image detail untouched. The approach is evaluated against a strong baseline using a blind reader study with board‑certified radiologists, demonstrating a statistically significant reduction in banding and no measurable loss of diagnostic detail. This contribution fills a gap in the literature by providing a practical, annotation‑free solution to the banding artifact that limits the clinical translation of deep‑learning MRI reconstructions.

[ANSWER]: Adversarial neural networks have been employed to reduce distributional divergence between source and target domains. Early work introduced gradient‑reversal layers combined with a shared feature extractor to align binary domains, enabling domain‑invariant representations. Subsequent extensions considered multinomial domain adaptation, where multiple source domains are jointly aligned through a categorical discriminator, allowing richer domain‑aware feature learning.  

In the weak‑supervision setting, several studies have incorporated adversarial ideas for related but distinct purposes. One line of work applies virtual adversarial training to perturb input representations, treating the resulting regularization as a generic robustness technique rather than a method tailored to heuristic labeling. Another strand adopts generative‑adversarial mechanisms to synthesize challenging negative examples that are hard to distinguish from heuristically annotated instances, thereby improving classifier discrimination without explicitly addressing label noise.  

A substantial body of research focuses on denoising the noisy labels produced by labeling functions or distant supervision. Approaches such as probabilistic label models estimate the reliability and correlations of labeling functions, aggregating their outputs into cleaner supervision signals. These methods primarily operate on the label side, modeling biases and dependencies among labeling functions, while largely ignoring potential biases that arise from the feature representations themselves.  

More recent efforts provide benchmarking frameworks that compare a variety of weak‑supervision techniques across multiple datasets. Such frameworks facilitate systematic evaluation of label‑denoising strategies and other enhancements, but they generally do not incorporate mechanisms that modify the influence of labeling‑function signals during model training.  

Building on these strands of research, the present work introduces an adversarial architecture that learns representations invariant to the specific signals encoded by labeling functions, thereby directly addressing feature‑side biases in weak supervision. By treating each labeling function as a separate domain and employing a multinomial discriminator, the method extends binary‑domain adversarial alignment to a multi‑source setting. The approach also differs from virtual adversarial training and GAN‑based negative‑example generation by targeting the soft removal of labeling‑function information rather than applying generic regularization or data augmentation. Compared with label‑denoising systems that only reweight or model labeling‑function outputs, the proposed scheme jointly optimizes a task classifier and an adversarial discriminator, offering a controllable trade‑off between reliance on heuristic cues and exploitation of broader task‑relevant signals. Experiments on benchmark weak‑supervision datasets demonstrate consistent improvements over label‑model baselines and feature‑based methods, with a hyperparameter governing the strength of adversarial regularization that can be tuned per dataset. The contributions include (i) a novel adversarial framework for controllable attenuation of labeling‑function signals, (ii) empirical validation of its effectiveness across diverse weakly supervised tasks, and (iii) an open‑source implementation to facilitate further research in adversarial weak supervision.

[ANSWER]: Knowledge distillation has traditionally relied on access to the original training data, either by directly transferring soft predictions of a teacher [1, 2] or by matching intermediate representations [3, 4].  Recent data‑free distillation methods attempt to bypass this requirement by synthesizing images from scratch [5–8], yet they often suffer from low fidelity and limited diversity, which hampers performance on complex downstream tasks such as semantic segmentation [9, 10].  A complementary line of work explores the use of out‑of‑domain (OOD) samples for distillation, but naïve application of OOD data typically yields students that fail to generalize to the target domain [11, 12].  Our approach, **MosaicKD**, advances this literature by introducing the first dedicated framework for OOD‑KD that explicitly leverages the shared local statistics of OOD images to reconstruct approximate in‑domain samples.  By embedding the teacher’s guidance into a four‑player adversarial game, MosaicKD generates synthetic data with realistic patches and coherent global semantics, thereby achieving substantially higher student performance than prior data‑free and OOD‑based distillation methods.

Domain adaptation and generalization aim to bridge distribution gaps between source and target domains, often by aligning feature spaces [13–15] or by learning domain‑invariant representations [16, 17].  While unsupervised adaptation assumes access to unlabeled target data during training, domain‑generalization techniques restrict themselves to source data only and seek robustness to unseen domains [18–21].  These paradigms, however, are designed for supervised tasks and do not address the unique challenges of knowledge transfer from a fixed teacher when the original training data are unavailable.  MosaicKD situates itself at the intersection of these fields: it treats the teacher’s pre‑trained model as a source of privileged knowledge, yet discards any reliance on source or target data, instead synthesizing surrogate in‑domain inputs from OOD patches.  This novel formulation extends the scope of domain‑generalization ideas to the distillation setting, providing a principled solution to the OOD‑KD problem that outperforms both adaptation‑based and generalization‑based baselines.

Generative adversarial networks (GANs) have become the de‑facto tool for realistic image synthesis through a two‑player minimax game between a generator and a discriminator [22].  Subsequent work has focused on improving image quality [23, 24], enhancing diversity [25, 26], and stabilizing training [27, 28], as well as adapting GANs to low‑data regimes via transfer learning [29] or data augmentation [30].  Nonetheless, standard GAN formulations presume the availability of real samples from the target distribution, an assumption that breaks down in OOD‑KD where the original data are inaccessible.  MosaicKD augments the classic GAN framework with two additional participants—a pre‑trained teacher and a student network—forming a four‑player min‑max game.  The discriminator operates on local patches to enforce authenticity, while the generator is driven not only by adversarial loss but also by the teacher’s soft predictions, ensuring that the synthesized images possess globally meaningful semantics for effective distillation.  This extended adversarial scheme uniquely addresses data synthesis under strict OOD constraints and yields synthetic samples that are both locally realistic and globally useful for knowledge transfer, surpassing existing GAN‑based data‑free distillation approaches.

[ANSWER]: Early ensemble‑distillation methods focused on reproducing only the average output of a teacher ensemble.  These works showed that a single neural network can be trained to mimic the mean predictions of a large collection of models, typically by minimizing a cross‑entropy loss against the ensemble’s soft targets or by using a convex combination of the teachers’ outputs.  While effective for improving speed at test time, such approaches do not preserve the variability among ensemble members, which limits their usefulness for uncertainty quantification.  Representative examples include the original demonstrations that a student network can learn the knowledge of a big ensemble, methods that augment the standard loss with a convex combination of teacher predictions, and online student‑teacher schemes where the teacher is obtained via Markov‑chain Monte‑Carlo training.  

Subsequent research introduced techniques aimed at capturing the diversity of the teacher ensemble.  Ensemble Distribution Distillation (EnDD) was among the first to model the full predictive distribution by fitting a Dirichlet‑parameterized network to the ensemble outputs.  Building on this idea, later works also assumed a Dirichlet form for the predictive distribution, learning separate networks for the Dirichlet mean (class probabilities) and concentration (confidence).  To mitigate over‑confidence and low diversity on the training set, these methods employed unsupervised auxiliary data and temperature‑annealing schedules.  Although these approaches improve uncertainty estimates, they remain restricted to classification tasks and rely on the Dirichlet assumption, which can be overly limiting for more general predictive scenarios.  

Another line of work extended the basic distillation framework by assigning each teacher member a dedicated task head while sharing a common backbone.  In this setting the backbone learns a shared representation, and each head is trained to reproduce the predictions of a specific ensemble member using standard cross‑entropy loss.  Weight‑sharing strategies such as BatchEnsemble have also been incorporated, together with data‑perturbation schemes—including Gaussian noise, Output‑Diversified Sampling (ODS), and confidence‑aware ODS—to increase diversity among the heads on the training data.  These perturbation‑based methods, however, introduce additional computational overhead because they require per‑iteration generation of adversarial‑like perturbations, making training slower and less scalable to large ensembles.  

In contrast, the proposed approach introduces a flexible functional ensemble distillation framework that captures both the predictive mean and the full covariance structure of the teacher ensemble without restricting the predictive distribution to a Dirichlet form.  By transferring stochasticity to a latent noise variable, the distilled model can generate diverse function samples and compute inter‑prediction covariances, which are valuable for downstream tasks such as correlated object‑detection decisions.  To address the common over‑fitting regime where ensemble members achieve near‑zero training loss yet exhibit divergent test‑time behavior, the method leverages mixup augmentation to create an auxiliary dataset that is both sufficiently different from the original training set to elicit diverse teacher predictions and sufficiently close to preserve performance on the target domain.  This auxiliary data is generated without requiring any external unlabeled samples, keeping the pipeline simple and scalable.  Extensive experiments on several image‑classification benchmarks demonstrate that the resulting distilled model attains superior accuracy, calibration, and uncertainty estimation compared with prior ensemble‑distillation techniques, while remaining computationally efficient at inference time.

[ANSWER]: Energy‑Based Models (EBMs) have long been defined as unnormalized densities, exemplified by exponential‑family distributions, Boltzmann machines, and the FRAME model.  The introduction of convolutional networks as expressive potential functions dramatically increased their capacity, enabling high‑quality image synthesis and improved adversarial robustness.  Recent approaches have paired EBMs with auxiliary generators to sidestep costly MCMC: Kim and Bengio jointly train an EBM and generator using generated samples as direct density approximations; EGAN augments this with a maximum‑entropy objective; VERA follows a similar line; cooperative learning uses the generator to initialise EBM samples and a reconstruction loss to update the generator; and Flow‑Contrastive EBM trains an EBM via noise‑contrastive estimation with a flow model.  While these methods successfully combine EBMs with specific auxiliary networks, they either rely on a particular generator architecture or require additional objectives to align the two models.  In contrast, our formulation—called the Hat EBM—integrates an arbitrary generator directly into the EBM’s forward pass, requiring only a residual “hat” network to model the discrepancy between generator output and the image manifold.  This design eliminates the need for specialized training tricks or auxiliary loss terms, and works uniformly with any pretrained generator, whether probabilistic (GANs, VAEs) or deterministic (autoencoders).

Learning EBMs in latent space has been proposed as a remedy for the multimodality and sampling difficulty of data‑space EBMs.  Prior work defines a prior EBM over a low‑dimensional latent variable, then maps latent samples to images through a learned generator; the prior serves as a correction to a simple uniform or isotropic Gaussian.  Because the latent posterior must be inferred, these methods depend on either amortised inference networks or expensive MCMC, which can limit scalability.  Several recent studies exploit a pretrained GAN or VAE to define a latent‑space EBM, often using the discriminator or encoder as a surrogate energy and demonstrating improved synthesis over the original generator.  Our approach also places an energy on the latent space, but crucially does not require any posterior inference for observed data.  By treating the latent variables as part of the Hat EBM’s joint distribution and sampling jointly over image residuals and latents, we obtain a simpler, more scalable learning procedure.  Moreover, because the Hat EBM does not assume a probabilistic latent prior, it can be applied to any pretrained generator—including deterministic autoencoders that lack an inherent latent distribution—thereby offering a more general framework for enhancing and sampling from existing generative models.
