[ANSWER]: The success of macro‑operators and abstraction in classical planning systems [1, 2] has inspired a large body of work on hierarchical approaches to reinforcement learning [3‑8]. Decomposing control across multiple levels of abstraction offers intuitive benefits such as simpler individual learning problems and the ability to make long‑term decisions; recent studies have identified improved exploration capabilities as a primary advantage of hierarchical reinforcement learning, particularly for modern neural‑network‑based agents [9, 10]. From a design perspective, hierarchical reinforcement learning enables the separate acquisition of low‑level policies (often referred to as options or skills), which can dramatically accelerate learning on downstream tasks.

A variety of methods have been proposed for discovering low‑level primitives. Some approaches rely on random walks [11, 12], while others employ mutual‑information objectives [13‑16] or make use of datasets comprising agent or expert traces [17‑19]. Motion‑capture data has also been leveraged to learn skills [20‑22], and dedicated pre‑training tasks have been introduced for skill acquisition [23, 24].

To realize their full potential, low‑level skills must be useful across a broad range of downstream tasks. In practice, however, a trade‑off between generality (wide applicability) and specificity (benefits for particular tasks) often emerges. Much of the prior work on option discovery and hierarchical reinforcement learning resolves this trade‑off, explicitly or implicitly, in favor of specificity. Early benchmarks typically involved navigation in grid‑world mazes [3, 4, 25], and recent simulated‑robotics research continues to favor navigation‑oriented problems [7, 14, 18, 24, 26]. In these settings, directed locomotion—translation of the robot’s center of mass—is the primary required motor skill, and algorithms consequently incorporate corresponding priors. Some methods make this prior explicit by partitioning the state space according to the agent’s position [14, 15], while others rely on the implicit contribution of position features to reward signals [7, 18, 27]. Works targeting non‑navigation environments acquire skills through tailored pre‑training tasks [28] or by using in‑domain motion‑capture data [20, 21, 29].

[ANSWER]: Robust control focuses on designing feedback controllers that guarantee performance in the presence of modeling uncertainties and external disturbances. Classical approaches formulate robustness criteria as linear matrix inequalities (LMIs), which can be solved efficiently with interior‑point or gradient‑based algorithms for problems of moderate size. These techniques often rely on linear or low‑order controllers to obtain provable stability, which can limit average‑case performance when richer nonlinear policies might be beneficial.  

Reinforcement learning (RL), and in particular deep RL, removes the restriction to simple controller structures by learning policies represented by neural networks through direct interaction with an environment. This paradigm has achieved notable success on a wide range of complex control tasks, as documented in several recent benchmarks and surveys. Because deep RL optimizes expected return without explicit safety constraints, most applications have been confined to simulated settings or highly controlled real‑world domains where failures are tolerable.  

To mitigate the safety and stability shortcomings of RL, a number of research directions have emerged. One line of work integrates control‑theoretic concepts, especially robust control, with the expressive power of neural‑network policies. Examples include methods that jointly learn Lyapunov functions and control policies to enforce stochastic stability, and approaches that cast continuous‑time control synthesis as min‑max differential games in which the controller and disturbance play opposing roles. These hybrid strategies aim to retain robustness guarantees while exploiting nonlinear policy representations.  

Another strand of research is safe RL, which seeks to enforce safety during learning and deployment. Many safe‑RL algorithms constrain exploration to regions of the state space that satisfy safety criteria, often assuming smooth dynamics that can be modeled with Gaussian processes or that satisfy Lipschitz continuity. Such assumptions enable probabilistic safety certificates but can be restrictive when dynamics exhibit sharp discontinuities.  

A third category leverages constrained Markov decision processes (C‑MDPs), where the objective is to maximize a discounted reward while keeping a separate discounted cost below a prescribed threshold. C‑MDPs do not require prior knowledge of the cost functions, yet they typically guarantee constraint satisfaction only at test time and may produce stochastic optimal policies whose constraints hold for only a subset of states.  

Parallel to these developments, differentiable optimization layers have become a popular tool for embedding optimization problems within neural networks. Recent work has introduced layers that solve quadratic programs, SAT instances, submodular optimization problems, cone programs, and other convex formulations in a way that permits gradient‑based learning. By embedding such layers, networks can be endowed with inductive biases that respect hard constraints of the underlying domain, enabling the projection of network outputs onto feasible sets defined by stability or safety requirements.

[ANSWER]: Metric learning seeks embeddings in which examples sharing a class label (positive pairs) lie close together while examples from different classes (negative pairs) are pushed apart.  In deep metric learning this goal is achieved by learning a non‑linear mapping from raw inputs to a low‑dimensional space where Euclidean distance exhibits the desired behavior.  Most prior work follows a fully supervised paradigm that defines positives and negatives directly from class labels, and the literature has largely focused on two families of loss functions: pair‑based losses that operate on explicit pairs, triplets, quadruplets or higher‑order tuples, and proxy‑based losses that replace data points with learnable class proxies.  Pair‑based losses capture fine‑grained data‑to‑data relationships but are sensitive to noisy labels and often produce zero‑gradient constraints, which in turn motivates sophisticated hard‑example mining strategies.  Proxy‑based losses alleviate these issues by aggregating data through proxies, yielding denser gradients and faster convergence at the cost of reduced granularity.  Our contribution situates itself between these two streams: by formulating a generic loss abstraction that encompasses both pair‑based and proxy‑based objectives, we enable the seamless integration of mixup‑style augmentation into any existing metric‑learning loss, thereby unifying and extending the current landscape rather than replacing one family with the other.  

Mixup and its variants have become a staple of data augmentation for classification, where two or more inputs are linearly combined in either the raw pixel space (input mixup) or in intermediate feature spaces (manifold mixup), and the corresponding one‑hot class labels are interpolated in the same proportion.  The majority of mixup research targets cross‑entropy loss and assumes an additive label structure, while only a handful of works explore its interaction with pairwise objectives.  Existing studies on embedding‑level interpolation for metric learning either restrict themselves to deterministic synthesis within a single class or to proxy interpolation across classes, and they do not address the challenge of label interpolation for pairwise losses.  By extending the mixup principle to the metric‑learning setting, we introduce a principled way to interpolate not only inputs, features or embeddings but also the binary “positive/negative’’ label associated with each anchor‑pair.  This enables the model to receive smoothly varying supervisory signals that reflect the degree of mixing, a capability absent from prior mixup‑based metric‑learning methods.  

Prior attempts to apply interpolation to pairwise loss functions either avoid label interpolation altogether or rely on ad‑hoc heuristics that can generate false negatives when the mixing coefficient is extreme.  For example, embedding‑expansion and symmetric synthesis methods interpolate embeddings within the same class but keep labels binary, while proxy‑synthesis interpolates between class proxies without adjusting the underlying pairwise supervision, risking label corruption.  Contrastive representation approaches such as MoCHi and OptTransMix introduce thresholds to sidestep false negatives, and i‑mix linearly interpolates anchor embeddings and a single virtual class label, yet they cannot exploit the relative weighting of multiple positives and negatives per anchor.  In contrast, our proposed Metric Mix (Metrix) provides a unified formulation that supports both pair‑based and proxy‑based losses, permits mixing at any stage of the network (input, manifold or embedding), and interpolates the binary positive/negative label in a mathematically sound manner.  Consequently, the relative contribution of positives versus negatives is continuously modulated by the mixing factor, a property that directly addresses the shortcomings of earlier interpolation schemes and positions our work as the first general‑purpose mixup framework for deep metric learning.

[ANSWER]: Visual domain adaptation has attracted considerable attention in recent years, with research spanning a variety of task settings such as unsupervised domain adaptation, multi‑source adaptation, semi‑supervised adaptation, partial adaptation, and unsupervised open‑set recognition. Deep domain‑adaptation approaches typically address distribution mismatch through two complementary strategies: moment alignment and adversarial training. Moment‑alignment methods employ classical statistical distances, including maximum mean discrepancy, second‑order statistics, and other task‑specific representation metrics, to reduce the gap between source and target distributions. Adversarial techniques, inspired by generative adversarial networks, introduce domain classifiers that are trained to be indistinguishable across domains; examples include domain‑adversarial neural networks that use gradient reversal, as well as more elaborate frameworks such as ADDA, CyCADA, MCD, CDAN, and GVB.

Beyond pure distribution matching, several works incorporate additional prior knowledge to guide the adaptation process. Heuristic information has been used to enhance reinforcement‑learning‑based transfer learning, while other studies promote transferability by minimizing norms of domain‑specific representations and enforce discriminability to avoid spurious predictions. Some approaches explicitly model domain‑specific properties, assuming that these are easier to obtain than domain‑invariant ones, and construct representations that separate the two components.

Reconstruction‑based methods also play a role in domain adaptation. Techniques such as domain separation networks and DLOW reconstruct input images to strengthen domain‑specific features, although image reconstruction can require substantial domain information and may complicate the removal of domain characteristics. Methods that directly reduce domain discrepancy, like GVB, focus on alignment but may lack precise modeling of domain‑specific components. Collectively, these lines of research illustrate a broad spectrum of strategies for mitigating domain shift, ranging from statistical alignment and adversarial objectives to the exploitation of heuristic priors and reconstruction mechanisms.

[ANSWER]: Deep generative models, and in particular variational auto‑encoders (VAEs), formulate unsupervised representation learning as a probabilistic inference problem by introducing an approximate posterior over latent representations. Although VAEs have become a standard tool, their model parameters remain deterministic, which makes them vulnerable to poor local minima and over‑fitting to the training data. This deterministic treatment can limit generalisation performance and lead to unreliable uncertainty estimates.  

Bayesian neural networks (BNNs) address these shortcomings by placing probability distributions over the network weights, thereby propagating weight uncertainty to the data space. While BNNs can in principle provide calibrated uncertainties, their practical adoption for autoencoder‑style architectures has been hampered by high computational cost, the need for careful tuning, and complex implementation requirements. Consequently, alternative approaches such as deep ensembles, stochastic weight averaging (SWAG), and Monte‑Carlo dropout have been proposed as scalable approximations to the weight posterior. These methods, however, typically increase training time, may suffer from sub‑optimal empirical performance, or lack a rigorous Bayesian interpretation.  

The Laplace approximation (LA) offers a tractable post‑hoc strategy for approximating the intractable weight posterior after maximum‑a‑posteriori (MAP) training. Early work demonstrated that LA can be applied to classification and regression tasks, providing scalable uncertainty estimates and enabling marginal‑likelihood‑based model selection and invariance learning. To make LA computationally feasible, researchers have introduced diagonal, Kronecker‑factored, and other structured Hessian approximations, which have also found use in second‑order optimisation schemes. These approximations reduce the memory and runtime burden of handling full Hessian matrices while preserving many of the benefits of a Gaussian weight posterior.  

A fully Bayesian treatment of VAE weights was explored in prior work that combined VAEs with Hamiltonian Monte‑Carlo sampling to obtain posterior samples over the parameters. This line of research highlighted the potential of exact Bayesian inference for generative models but also underscored the difficulty of scaling Hamiltonian dynamics to large‑scale training regimes and the associated computational overhead.  

In this paper we introduce a Bayesian autoencoder framework that integrates a variational Laplace‑style approximation directly into the training objective, rather than applying it post‑hoc. By deriving a variational lower bound on the autoencoder evidence and maximising it with Monte‑Carlo EM, we obtain a tractable objective that jointly learns representations and weight uncertainty. To overcome the computational challenges of Hessian computation, we propose a novel linear‑time Hessian approximation that scales gracefully with data size and dimensionality, enabling efficient training on high‑dimensional datasets. The resulting model yields well‑calibrated uncertainties in both latent and output spaces, addressing the reliability issues of deterministic VAEs and the scalability limitations of existing Bayesian approaches such as full BNNs, deep ensembles, SWAG, Monte‑Carlo dropout, and post‑hoc Laplace approximations. Empirical evaluations demonstrate that the proposed Bayesian autoencoder improves performance across a range of downstream tasks, confirming the benefits of integrating uncertainty estimation directly into unsupervised representation learning.

[ANSWER]: Early research on Chinese pinyin input methods primarily distinguished between perfect and abbreviated pinyin. Approaches that handle perfect pinyin have evolved from statistical language models [1] and statistical machine translation [2] to neural architectures. Notable neural systems include Moon IME, which combines an attention‑based network with an information‑retrieval module [3], an LSTM‑based encoder‑decoder enhanced by online vocabulary adaptation [4], and a variant that concatenates contextual words with abbreviated pinyin as input to an LSTM encoder‑decoder [5].  

A separate line of work has addressed the presence of typing errors in pinyin entry. One study introduced a typing model that jointly performs spelling correction within a sentence‑based pinyin input framework [1], while another proposed a joint graph model that simultaneously optimizes pinyin conversion and typo correction [6]. These methods aim to improve robustness against user mistakes, an aspect that remains less explored in the broader pinyin input literature.  

Recent advances in pretrained language models have incorporated pinyin information to enrich character representations. A general‑purpose Chinese BERT was extended with dedicated embedding layers for pinyin and glyph features [7]. Task‑specific BERT variants for grammatical error correction—where homophonic confusion is a major error source—have added pinyin embeddings to predict characters among similarly pronounced candidates [8]. PLOME introduced dual GRU‑based embedding modules to inject both pinyin and character shape information [9], and another model employed a hierarchical encoder to fuse pinyin letters at character and sentence levels together with a ResNet encoder for graphic character features [10].  

In this work we present the first systematic exploration of Chinese GPT for the pinyin input method. We evaluate a frozen GPT model on both perfect and abbreviated pinyin, demonstrating state‑of‑the‑art performance on perfect pinyin while revealing a substantial degradation on abbreviated inputs due to the many‑to‑many mapping between initials and full pinyin. To mitigate this issue we propose two complementary strategies: enriching the input sequence with explicit pinyin context and training with a pinyin‑constrained vocabulary that sharpens the model’s ability to distinguish homophones. To support comprehensive evaluation, we construct a new dataset of 270 K instances drawn from 15 news domains, covering diverse context lengths and pinyin types. Experiments show that both proposed strategies consistently improve abbreviated‑pinyin performance across all domains, and that longer character contexts further boost GPT‑based models. Our analysis confirms the individual contributions of context enrichment and pinyin‑constrained training, establishing a solid baseline for future research on GPT‑driven, error‑tolerant pinyin input systems.

[ANSWER]: Recent advances in neural text generation have spurred a wave of research on abstractive summarization, where encoder‑decoder architectures are used to produce novel words and phrases that capture the salient ideas of a source document. Early systems relied on purely sequence‑to‑sequence models, while later approaches leveraged pretrained language models as encoders or employed large‑scale unsupervised pre‑training to boost fluency and salience. Structured representations such as semantic dependency graphs and unified semantic graphs have further demonstrated benefits for content selection and coherence. Our Faithfulness Enhanced Summarization model (FES) builds on this foundation but goes beyond improving fluency by explicitly addressing the encoder’s ability to capture key factual information; we augment the encoder with an auxiliary question‑answering task that forces it to attend to crucial entities and relations, thereby producing representations that are both richer and more faithful to the source than those learned by standard pretrained encoders.

While many prior works have recognized the importance of factual consistency, most solutions either inject external fact‑checking signals (e.g., fact descriptions, entailment classifiers, knowledge‑graph encodings) or rely on contrastive learning with positive and negative summary pairs to train the decoder to avoid hallucinations. A few approaches also modify the decoder’s attention mechanisms to focus on source tokens, yet none have directly penalized the language model’s tendency to generate fluent but unfounded expressions. FES distinguishes itself by coupling the QA‑driven encoder enhancements with a max‑margin loss that measures and reduces the over‑confidence of the underlying language model, a strategy that has not been explored in abstractive summarization and directly curtails extrinsic errors caused by excessive reliance on language‑model priors.

Multi‑task learning has long been employed to share knowledge across related linguistic tasks such as segmentation, tagging, parsing, and classification, yielding more robust representations for each task. In the summarization literature, multi‑task setups have typically paired summarization with auxiliary objectives like sentence ordering or content planning, but they have not used question answering as a fidelity‑focused auxiliary task. Our approach adopts a multi‑task paradigm in which the summarization and QA objectives are trained jointly; the QA task provides a concrete, data‑driven signal of what information must be retained, while the shared encoder benefits from both objectives. This integration enables FES to achieve superior ROUGE performance and markedly higher factual consistency compared with strong baselines, confirming the effectiveness of our joint learning strategy for faithfulness‑enhanced summarization.

[ANSWER]: Many approaches have sought to reduce the computational burden of Neural Architecture Search by employing proxies such as reduced model size, smaller training datasets, or fewer training steps. While these shortcuts can accelerate the evaluation of candidate architectures, they often yield inaccurate performance estimates and consequently degrade the quality of the searched models.  

Another line of work focuses on parameter sharing across the search space, allowing multiple architectures to be trained jointly within a single supernet. By reusing weights, the training time for each individual candidate is reduced, yet the shared‑weight paradigm still faces challenges in providing fair and precise performance assessments for all architectures, especially when the search space is large and diverse.  

A further set of methods builds explicit performance predictors that map architectural descriptors—such as network topology, depth, and model size—to expected accuracy, latency, or other metrics. Examples include approaches that encode architectures into feature vectors and train multilayer perceptrons or other regression models to forecast hardware latency or validation performance. Although predictor‑based techniques markedly speed up the estimation phase, they typically require a substantial number of fully trained samples to achieve reliable accuracy, which in turn raises the overall search cost.  

In contrast, the present work formulates Neural Architecture Search as a ranking problem and introduces a pairwise ranking model that learns to distinguish higher‑quality architectures from lower‑quality ones without requiring precise performance predictions. By reducing the ranking task to binary classification over architecture pairs, the method dramatically cuts the number of required training examples. An architecture selection strategy is also proposed to prune the search space toward promising candidates based on feature importance. Extensive experiments on machine translation and language‑modeling benchmarks demonstrate that this ranking‑based approach attains state‑of‑the‑art results while being orders of magnitude faster than conventional NAS systems, including hardware‑aware baselines, and discovers novel architectures that surpass strong existing models with significantly lower search cost.

[ANSWER]: Continual Learning (CL) methods have been broadly categorized into regularization‑based, parameter‑isolation, and rehearsal‑based approaches. Regularization‑based techniques constrain changes to parameters important for previously learned tasks, thereby mitigating feature drift (e.g., elastic weight consolidation, synaptic intelligence). Parameter‑isolation methods allocate dedicated subsets of model capacity to each task, often through dynamic expansion or sub‑network branching. Rehearsal‑based strategies retain a bounded memory buffer of exemplars from earlier tasks and interleave them with new data to alleviate catastrophic forgetting. While these approaches improve the overall stability–plasticity trade‑off, they generally treat training examples as interchangeable and do not explicitly investigate how individual samples differentially affect stability and plasticity.  

Research on example influence has grown alongside advances in interpretable machine learning. Influence‑based methods aim to quantify how perturbations to a training sample’s weight affect a downstream loss, offering a principled way to assess sample importance. The classic Influence Function computes this effect via a second‑order Taylor expansion that requires the inverse Hessian of the model parameters, which can be prohibitively expensive for deep networks. To address scalability, several works have employed approximation schemes, stochastic estimators, or meta‑learning formulations that sidestep explicit Hessian inversion. In parallel, influence estimates have been leveraged to reweight, prune, or select training instances, thereby improving generalization or robustness in various learning settings.  

In this work we introduce a meta‑learning algorithm that efficiently approximates stability‑aware and plasticity‑aware example influences without requiring Hessian inversion, and we integrate these influences within a rehearsal‑based CL framework. By formulating the combination of the two influences as a dual‑objective optimization problem, we obtain a fused influence that lies on the Pareto frontier of stability and plasticity. The fused influence is then used to (i) modulate per‑example loss scaling during model updates and (ii) guide exemplar selection under a fixed memory budget, ensuring that stored samples collectively maximize positive impact on both stability and plasticity. Extensive experiments on task‑incremental and class‑incremental benchmarks demonstrate that the proposed approach consistently outperforms state‑of‑the‑art CL methods, achieving superior retention of old knowledge while maintaining rapid acquisition of new tasks.

[ANSWER]: Deep learning techniques have become a cornerstone of modern program analysis, with numerous surveys documenting their impact on tasks such as bug detection, code synthesis, and code retrieval. Early efforts demonstrated that neural networks can learn useful representations of source code, enabling automatic classification, clone detection, and recommendation systems. These works established the feasibility of treating code as a first‑class learning signal and set the stage for more specialized retrieval models.

A prominent line of research addresses semantic code search by learning a joint embedding space for natural‑language queries and code snippets. Recurrent neural networks (RNNs) were among the first architectures to map both modalities into a shared latent space, allowing similarity‑based retrieval. Subsequent studies adopted Transformer‑based encoders, which leverage self‑attention to capture richer contextual information and achieve higher retrieval accuracy on large code corpora. Many of these approaches rely on a single similarity metric—either a fixed cosine similarity or a learned distance function—to rank candidate snippets.

Recognizing that source code possesses an intrinsic graph‑like structure, several works have incorporated graph neural networks (GNNs) into the retrieval pipeline. By representing programs as abstract syntax trees, control‑flow graphs, or data‑flow graphs, GNN‑based models perform graph matching between query and code representations, thereby exploiting syntactic and semantic relationships that are invisible to purely sequential encoders. These methods have shown particular strength on queries that refer to structural properties of the code.

Other researchers have enriched code embeddings with auxiliary analyses. Incorporating semantic parses, dependency graphs, or variable‑renaming invariances helps models become robust to superficial code changes. Abstract syntax trees (ASTs) and related hierarchical representations have been combined with token‑level embeddings to capture both fine‑grained lexical information and higher‑level program constructs. Such hybrid models often achieve improvements on benchmarks where code diversity and naming conventions vary widely.

A complementary perspective treats code retrieval and code summarization as dual problems, training models to both locate relevant snippets and generate natural‑language descriptions. Multi‑task frameworks leverage shared encoders and jointly optimize retrieval and summarization losses, thereby encouraging representations that are useful for both directions of the code‑language mapping. Recent investigations have also considered scenarios where only brief annotative comments accompany code fragments, focusing on how limited textual signals affect retrieval performance.

In contrast to these prior approaches, the present work frames semantic code search as a compositional reasoning task that benefits from explicit decomposition of the query into data‑entity and action components. By constructing a semantic layout of the query and executing a Neural Module Network that assembles specialized modules according to this layout, the proposed method directly addresses the challenges of long, multi‑step natural‑language specifications. Empirical evaluation on the CodeSearchNet and CoSQA/WebQueryTest benchmarks demonstrates substantial gains over state‑of‑the‑art baselines, especially for queries with high compositional complexity. Additional experiments with reduced training sizes, adversarial query perturbations, and fine‑grained analysis of multi‑stage reasoning further validate the robustness and effectiveness of the modular architecture.

[ANSWER]: Recent work has highlighted that modern image‑classification benchmarks increasingly emphasize raw accuracy while overlooking how models arrive at their predictions, which encourages reliance on spurious shortcuts such as background cues [1‑7]. These shortcomings are not confined to vision; similar phenomena have been reported in NLP and multimodal settings [8,9]. In contrast to approaches that merely report a single performance number, our study explicitly measures and mitigates shortcut reliance by finetuning pretrained Vision Transformers with foreground‑mask supervision, thereby improving robustness to distribution shifts without requiring additional labeled data.  

The notion of Sufficient Input Subsets (SIS) has been used to expose models that can achieve high confidence from a minimal, often implausibly sparse set of pixels [10,11]. While SIS can reveal over‑interpretation, we find that SIS‑based explanations for ViTs are prone to adversarial manipulation and therefore do not reliably reflect genuine foreground reliance (see Appendix). Consequently, we adopt a suite of distribution‑shift benchmarks—including ImageNet‑v2, ImageNet‑A, ImageNet‑R, ImageNet‑Sketch, ObjectNet, and SI‑Score—to evaluate whether our foreground‑focused finetuning truly enhances generalization, rather than relying on potentially misleading SIS metrics.  

Explainability techniques based on gradients, attention, and attribution propagation have become standard tools for visualizing model decisions [19‑25]. Prior methods improve relevance maps by augmenting gradients with input signals [22‑25] or by employing axiomatic attribution [26,27] and Shapley‑based permutations [28]. For transformer architectures, combining gradient information with attention weights yields interpretable saliency maps [29,30]. Building on this foundation, our approach treats the relevance map itself as an optimization target: we introduce a three‑term loss that explicitly suppresses background relevance, expands foreground coverage, and preserves classifier confidence. This direct manipulation of relevance during a brief finetuning stage differentiates our work from post‑hoc explanation methods that merely visualize but do not alter model behavior.  

Regularizing input gradients or feature representations has been explored as a means to curb reliance on irrelevant cues. Ross et al. introduced a gradient‑based regularizer that discourages attention to background pixels [31], and subsequent studies applied similar ideas to medical imaging [32,33] and context‑aware representation learning [34,35]. However, these methods either focus solely on background suppression or require extensive training from scratch. Our contribution extends this line of research by simultaneously encouraging foreground utilization, maintaining high prediction confidence, and operating as a lightweight finetuning step applicable to large pretrained ViTs. This makes our technique practical for real‑world deployment where full retraining is prohibitive.  

Finally, many domain‑adaptation and data‑augmentation strategies aim to improve robustness by altering the training distribution [6,12‑15]. While effective, such methods often demand sizable auxiliary datasets or complex adversarial objectives. In contrast, our approach leverages a minimal set of foreground masks—either human‑annotated or automatically generated by a self‑supervised ViT—to directly reshape the model’s relevance landscape. [ANSWER]: A range of complexity measures for functions computed by deep neural networks have been considered in the literature, which can be grouped into three broad categories. The first category focuses on worst‑case (or best‑case) scenarios, examining the maximal possible complexity of functions that a given network architecture can represent. This line of work studies expressive power and often demonstrates that deep networks can represent functions that shallow networks cannot. For example, it has been shown that the number of linear regions produced by a deep ReLU network can grow exponentially with depth [1]–[5]. Other investigations consider the degree of polynomials that can be approximated by networks of different depths [6], [7] and the topological invariants associated with network function spaces [8].

The second category addresses the gap between expressive capacity and learnability. Average‑case analyses have revealed that some functions that are readily expressible by a given architecture are provably difficult to learn [9] or occur with vanishing probability in randomly initialized networks [10]–[12]. Conversely, certain functions that are learned more easily by deep architectures are also expressible by shallow ones [13]. In the context of arithmetic circuits, worst‑case and average‑case scenarios are more closely aligned, with a pronounced gap observed in the matricization rank of functions computed by deep versus shallow architectures [14].

Within the average‑case literature for ReLU networks, studies have shown that expected length distortion can grow exponentially with depth [15], [16], and similar analyses have been performed for the curvature of output trajectories [17]. These results often rely on weight initializations that lead to exponentially large activations and exploding gradients [18], [19], which are mitigated by alternative schemes such as He normal initialization [20].

The third category comprises efforts to identify complexity measures that correlate with generalization performance. Proposed measures include classification margins [22], network compressibility [23], and PAC‑Bayes‑based criteria [24], among others [21].

[ANSWER]: Learning from demonstrations (LfD) traditionally relies on behavior cloning, which directly regresses a policy onto state‑action pairs from expert trajectories. While simple, this approach suffers from distribution shift and compounding errors. Subsequent works introduced no‑regret algorithms such as DAgger and inverse‑reinforcement‑learning (IRL) methods that infer a reward function before applying standard RL. A prominent IRL‑based method, Generative Adversarial Imitation Learning (GAIL), casts imitation as a distribution‑matching problem and uses a GAN‑style discriminator to minimize the Jensen‑Shannon divergence between expert and learner state‑action distributions. Although GAIL and its many variants have spurred considerable progress, they remain fundamentally on‑policy: each policy update requires fresh interactions with the environment, which is sample‑inefficient and limits applicability in settings where data collection is costly. Our work inherits the distribution‑matching perspective of GAIL but departs from its on‑policy requirement by deriving an off‑policy upper bound that can be optimized using replay buffers, thereby achieving principled sample efficiency without sacrificing theoretical guarantees.

Off‑policy extensions of GAIL, such as DAC and related replay‑buffer approaches, improve sample reuse by training on previously collected transitions. However, these methods typically ignore the distributional drift introduced when substituting the on‑policy state‑action distribution with an off‑policy approximation, leading to a mismatch between the optimized objective and the true imitation goal. ValueDICE further addresses off‑policy learning by reformulating the LfD objective via density‑ratio estimation, yet it explicitly requires expert actions to construct the required importance weights. Consequently, existing off‑policy techniques either lack rigorous justification for the distribution shift or cannot be applied when expert actions are unavailable. In contrast, our method establishes a provable bound that eliminates the need for expert actions and explicitly accounts for the off‑policy bias, enabling reliable off‑policy learning in the observation‑only setting.

Model‑free learning from observations (LfO) removes the action component from expert data and extends the GAIL framework to a state‑only discriminator (GAIfO). Subsequent analyses (e.g., IDDM) quantify the theoretical gap between LfD and LfO objectives and propose mutual‑information regularization to narrow this gap, achieving performance comparable to GAIL but still relying on on‑policy sampling. Other model‑free LfO approaches design ranking‑based reward functions or employ adversarial objectives, yet they inherit the same sample‑inefficiency as their LfD counterparts. Our contribution builds on the model‑free LfO literature by preserving the adversarial distribution‑matching formulation while introducing a novel regularizer that promotes mode‑covering from the learner’s perspective. This regularizer is instantiated via an inverse action model, which can be learned offline and used to guide policy updates without additional environment interaction, thus markedly improving sample efficiency.

Model‑based LfO methods either learn a forward dynamics model or an inverse action model to reconstruct missing actions before applying behavior cloning. Forward‑model approaches often require a separate policy for each time step, making them unsuitable for long‑horizon or infinite‑horizon tasks. Inverse‑action techniques (e.g., BCO) infer actions from observations and then clone the resulting state‑action pairs, but they rely on strong assumptions such as deterministic, injective dynamics to guarantee accurate action recovery. Moreover, they do not directly address the distribution‑matching objective underlying modern imitation learning. Our approach leverages an inverse action model not as a preprocessing step but as a regularizer that aligns the learner’s state distribution with the expert’s, under a mild deterministic‑environment assumption. This integration preserves the flexibility of model‑free adversarial methods while benefiting from the additional structure provided by the inverse model, without imposing restrictive dynamics assumptions.

In summary, our method—Off‑Policy Learning from Observations (OPOLO)—unifies the strengths of distribution‑matching imitation learning, off‑policy sample reuse, and inverse‑action regularization. By deriving an off‑policy upper bound that dispenses with expert actions and by employing an inverse model to enforce mode‑covering, OPOLO achieves state‑of‑the‑art performance on challenging locomotion benchmarks with substantially reduced sample complexity. This positions our work as the first principled, sample‑efficient LfO algorithm that combines theoretical rigor with practical effectiveness.

[ANSWER]: In deep reinforcement learning, agents are typically trained in an episodic setting and evaluated on the quality of the learned policy. A number of recent works have proposed algorithms that can learn without episodic resets, enabling continual interaction with the environment by training auxiliary controllers that allow the policy to retry tasks multiple times without external resets [1–9].

Reinforcement learning in a continuing setting has been studied extensively, with approaches that aim to maximize the average reward accumulated over an infinite horizon and often consider regret minimization as the primary objective [10–16]. This line of work focuses on lifelong learning and exploration strategies that operate without episodic boundaries.

The use of offline data to accelerate online reinforcement learning has been explored across several related areas. Imitation learning leverages prior interaction data to learn expert policies [17–19], while inverse reinforcement learning seeks to infer reward functions from demonstrations [20–23]. Techniques for sparse‑reward environments [24–28] and offline reinforcement learning [29–33] also aim to derive competent policies that solve tasks efficiently. Recent adversarial inverse‑reinforcement‑learning methods encourage agents to visit states that are common in expert datasets, thereby increasing the likelihood of successful behavior [34–39]. Extensions of adversarial inverse RL and imitation learning to settings with non‑expert data have been investigated as well [40–47].

Transfer and adaptation in reinforcement learning have been addressed through various strategies for handling distribution shifts between training and testing conditions. Works on sim‑to‑real transfer develop methods for bridging the gap between simulated and physical environments [48–51], and meta‑learning approaches enable fast adaptation at test time [52–56]. Other techniques adapt policy parameters to new tasks either via rapid zero‑shot adaptation [57, 58] or through extended episodic online training [59–63]. These studies highlight the challenges of autonomous recovery from mistakes and the need for effective exploration in the presence of changing dynamics.

ANSWER: Previous studies on TDMR (Task-Dataset-Metric-Result) extraction primarily rely on data sources such as NLP-progress or PapersWithCode. While these sources are popular, they lack rigorous quality control, including standardized TDM entity representation and complete publication coverage. Some efforts, such as [1] and [2], go further by extracting TDM triples and result values, and normalizing them for leaderboard construction. However, these approaches are constrained by their reliance on a closed domain, requiring extracted triples to match a pre-defined TDM taxonomy. Other studies, such as [3] and [4], only extract TDM triples without result values, thereby forgoing leaderboard construction. Meanwhile, [6] focuses on extracting result values, but only in the context of pre-defined TDM triples, making it similar in limitation to [1]. Consequently, these methods are not adaptable to the dynamic nature of emerging benchmarks, where new tasks, datasets, and metrics are frequently introduced.
In a parallel research thread, scientific leaderboards have been treated as a form of scientific knowledge graphs composed of Task, Dataset, and Metric entities. Information extraction in this context has been widely studied, as seen in works such as [5, 7, 8, 9, 10]. These studies typically focus on linking scientific content to structured entities but often assume that the schema or triples are known in advance.
In contrast to prior work, our approach addresses several critical limitations. We construct our dataset manually from publications to ensure comprehensive TDMR annotations, rather than relying on incomplete or unstandardized third-party sources. We normalize TDMR tuples for accurate leaderboard construction and propose novel experimental settings that reflect realistic, dynamic scenarios, including cold-start conditions without any pre-defined TDM taxonomy. By simultaneously constructing a scientific knowledge graph and linking publications to it in an open-domain setting, our method is inherently adaptable to the evolving research landscape.

ANSWER: Several efforts have been made to automate the extraction and organization of performance results from scientific publications. Early work by [1] introduced a framework, TDMS-IE, for identifying task, dataset, metric, and score tuples from NLP papers, establishing a foundation for automatic leaderboard construction. Similarly, AxCell [2] proposes a robust pipeline that utilizes structural analysis and novel table segmentation techniques to extract results from machine learning papers, demonstrating significant improvements over prior methods. ORKG-Leaderboards [3] offers a systematic approach that integrates leaderboard extraction into a knowledge graph framework, enabling machine-actionable publishing and dynamic visualization of state-of-the-art performance. TELIN [4] focuses on extracting leaderboard-relevant entities from PDFs using a semi-automated approach that reduces human annotation needs through targeted entity refinement.
In addition to these extraction systems, several datasets and benchmarks have been introduced to facilitate the development and evaluation of leaderboard construction tools. LEGOBench [5] provides a large-scale benchmark derived from arXiv and PapersWithCode, and evaluates both language model-based and graph-based approaches. SciERC and its associated framework SciIE [6] support multi-task extraction of entities and relations, enabling construction of scientific knowledge graphs. SciREX [7] extends information extraction to the document level, capturing relationships that span across sections, which is critical for leaderboard generation from full papers.
Other contributions include TDMSci [8], a specialized corpus annotated with task, dataset, and metric entities, which supports the development of more accurate extraction models. SciNLP-KG [9] introduces methods for extracting entity relations from NLP literature to build a knowledge graph, highlighting its potential use in leaderboard automation. Additionally, a diachronic analysis of NLP research trends by [10] shows the evolving influence of tasks, methods, and datasets, providing a broader context for understanding the dynamic nature of scientific benchmarks.

[ANSWER]: Training‑data influence methods aim to trace how individual examples affect model parameters and downstream predictions. Early work on linear models introduced influence functions for detecting influential points (Cook, 1982), which were later extended to deep networks (Koh & Liang, 2017). Subsequent approaches such as representer point selection (Yeh et al., 2018) and TracIn (Pruthi et al., 2020) compute similarities between gradients of the loss with respect to model weights at training and test instances. Because modern deep networks contain millions of parameters, these methods commonly restrict computation to a subset of weights, typically those in the final layer, to make influence estimation tractable.

In the natural‑language‑processing (NLP) literature, several studies have examined the suitability of different network representations for similarity‑based tasks. Empirical analyses have shown that activations from the last layer often encode task‑specific output information while discarding fine‑grained input characteristics, which limits their effectiveness for measuring example similarity (e.g., B., 2020). Simpler similarity measures based on surface statistics, such as TF‑IDF cosine similarity, have sometimes outperformed last‑layer‑based influence scores on case‑deletion evaluations, highlighting the mismatch between high‑level representations and the low‑level cues needed for accurate influence estimation. Related work on sentence embeddings also reports that deeper layers may be less appropriate for capturing semantic similarity compared with earlier layers.

A complementary line of research has applied data‑importance techniques to downstream NLP tasks. Influence functions and TracIn have been leveraged to uncover dataset artifacts (e.g., Gururangan et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2021) and to guide model improvement through targeted data curation (e.g., Han et al., 2022; Lee et al., 2023). These applications demonstrate the practical utility of training‑example influence but also expose challenges when the underlying influence estimators rely on representations that do not adequately reflect input semantics.

This work introduces a word‑embedding‑based influence estimator that operates on the gradient of the embedding layer rather than on the final‑layer weights. By focusing on the earliest learned representations, the method mitigates the cancellation effect observed when aggregating influences from the last layer, while still capturing high‑level semantic information through gradient chaining. The approach yields fine‑grained, word‑level influence scores, enabling more interpretable debugging of training data. Empirical evaluation on three text classification benchmarks shows substantial gains over existing influence‑based and TF‑IDF baselines on case‑deletion metrics. Moreover, extensions that incorporate synonym matching address the limitation of requiring exact token overlap, further broadening applicability to semantically related examples.

[ANSWER]: Analyzing the expressive power of coupling‑based normalizing flows has attracted considerable attention. Early universality results established that any coupling flow capable of approximating an arbitrary invertible map can represent any target density weakly [1]. This line of work was later extended to specific architectures such as affine couplings [2, 3, 4], Flow++ [5], neural autoregressive flows [6] and SOS‑polynomial flows [7]. While these studies demonstrate weak or Wasserstein convergence, they rely on increasingly ill‑conditioned transformations—functions become highly discontinuous as the approximation error shrinks—whereas empirical practice shows that learned couplings remain smooth. Moreover, the convergence criteria used (weak convergence or Wasserstein distance) do not guarantee reduction of the maximum‑likelihood loss that practitioners actually optimize. An arbitrarily small distance in those metrics can still correspond to an infinite Kullback–Leibler (KL) divergence. In contrast, the present work directly tackles the KL divergence: we decompose the loss into a non‑Gaussianity term and a non‑Standardness term, and we prove quantitative convergence of the latter. This yields the first linear‑rate bound on KL‑based convergence for generic coupling‑based flows, thereby bridging the gap between theoretical guarantees and the loss employed in practice.

Subsequent investigations have examined conditions under which well‑conditioned couplings suffice. For instance, it was shown that well‑conditioned affine couplings can approximate log‑concave padded distributions, albeit again measured in Wasserstein distance [9]. Lipschitz‑constrained flows were found to be unable to capture arbitrary tail behavior, a limitation that can be mitigated by modifying the latent prior [10]. Although SOS‑polynomial flows achieve total‑variation convergence [11] and zero‑padded affine couplings converge weakly [12], none of these results address KL convergence. By focusing on the KL divergence, our analysis complements these findings and clarifies that well‑conditioned couplings can already guarantee meaningful progress on the maximum‑likelihood objective without resorting to pathological conditioning.

A particularly relevant line of work demonstrated that a stack of 48 linear affine coupling blocks can represent any invertible linear map [8], which in turn implies that any Gaussian can be transformed into a standard normal. Building on this observation, we reinterpret the result in terms of the KL loss decomposition introduced above. Our theory shows that the “non‑Standardness” component of the KL divergence contracts at a linear rate with depth, implying that far fewer layers than the 48‑block construction are needed to achieve a comparable KL reduction for typical flow architectures. This positions our contribution as a refinement of existing linear‑algebraic universality statements, providing practical depth estimates that align with the loss actually minimized during training.

[ANSWER]: Sample weighting methods have long been employed to mitigate the adverse effects of biased training data. Early approaches relied on dataset resampling or instance re‑weighting as a preprocessing step, using prior knowledge about class frequencies or label reliability to assign static weights. More recent work shifted toward designing a weighting function that maps the current training loss of each example to a weight, allowing the weights to be updated dynamically during training. Two main families of weighting functions have emerged. The first family is monotonically increasing with loss, thereby emphasizing samples that incur larger losses; this strategy is particularly effective for class‑imbalanced problems. Representative methods include boosting algorithms such as AdaBoost and its variants, hard‑example mining techniques, and the focal loss, all of which assign higher importance to hard or minority‑class examples. The second family adopts a monotonically decreasing mapping, giving larger weights to samples with small losses under the assumption that these are more likely to be correctly labeled and thus trustworthy. This line of work encompasses self‑paced learning (SPL) and its extensions, iterative re‑weighting schemes, and several recent algorithms that focus on easy samples to combat noisy labels. A common limitation of these approaches is the need to manually specify the functional form of the weighting map and to tune associated hyper‑parameters, which hampers their ease of deployment in diverse real‑world settings.

Meta‑learning methods have been proposed to learn adaptive weighting schemes directly from data, reducing the reliance on hand‑crafted functions. Early meta‑learning based weighting frameworks such as learning‑to‑teach, MentorNet, and the Forward‑Loss‑Weighted (FWL) approach treat the weighting function as a separate learnable model—often a Bayesian approximator, a deep network with attention, or a bidirectional LSTM—that ingests additional information beyond the loss, such as sample features or auxiliary signals. While these designs increase flexibility, they also introduce considerable complexity, requiring richer inputs and more intricate architectures that can be difficult to reproduce and to guarantee the desirable properties of traditional weighting schemes. L2RW presents a closely related meta‑learning strategy in which sample weights are learned implicitly through a bi‑level optimization process. Although effective, the lack of an explicit, interpretable weighting function may lead to unstable weight dynamics and limited transferability across tasks.

Methods that address class imbalance without explicit sample re‑weighting have explored alternative mechanisms for leveraging minority‑class information. Knowledge‑transfer techniques aim to transfer representations learned from majority classes to under‑represented ones, while metric‑learning based approaches design specialized loss functions—such as triple‑header loss or range loss—to encourage better discrimination among tail classes. These strategies complement re‑weighting methods but often require additional architectural components or carefully engineered distance metrics.

A substantial body of work tackles corrupted or noisy labels by attempting to correct the noisy supervision rather than re‑weighting examples. Label‑correction frameworks infer cleaner pseudo‑labels through auxiliary networks or statistical estimators, then use the refined labels for subsequent training. Notable examples include loss‑correction methods like GLC, as well as approaches such as Reed’s label smoothing, co‑training schemes, D2L, and S‑Model, which incorporate noise‑robust objectives or auxiliary consistency constraints. While effective in many scenarios, these techniques typically assume access to a clean validation set or rely on specific noise models, and they do not directly address the simultaneous presence of class imbalance and label noise.

In this work we introduce an adaptive sample‑weighting framework that learns an explicit, continuously parameterized weighting function directly from data. The weighting function is instantiated as a shallow multilayer perceptron with a single hidden layer, endowing it with universal approximation capability so that it can represent any continuous mapping from loss to weight, including those employed by traditional increasing‑ or decreasing‑type schemes. Guided by a small unbiased meta‑validation set, the parameters of this Meta‑Weight‑Net are updated jointly with the classifier in a bi‑level optimization loop, eliminating the need for manual specification of functional forms or hyper‑parameter tuning. By retaining an explicit functional form, the proposed method achieves stable weight dynamics, facilitates interpretation, and enables straightforward transfer to related tasks. Extensive experiments on both class‑imbalanced and noisy‑label benchmarks demonstrate that the learned weighting functions naturally converge to patterns akin to focal loss or SPL when appropriate, while also adapting to more complex bias combinations that elude conventional hand‑crafted designs. Compared with implicit meta‑learning approaches such as L2RW and with more elaborate meta‑learners like MentorNet, our method delivers comparable or superior performance with reduced complexity and improved generalization, thereby offering a practical and theoretically grounded solution for robust deep learning under diverse data biases.

ANSWER: Previous studies on TDMR (Task-Dataset-Metric-Result) extraction primarily rely on data sources such as NLP-progress or PapersWithCode. While these sources are popular, they lack rigorous quality control, including standardized TDM entity representation and complete publication coverage. Some efforts, such as [1] and [2], go further by extracting TDM triples and result values, and normalizing them for leaderboard construction. However, these approaches are constrained by their reliance on a closed domain, requiring extracted triples to match a pre-defined TDM taxonomy. Other studies, such as [3] and [4], only extract TDM triples without result values, thereby forgoing leaderboard construction. Meanwhile, [6] focuses on extracting result values, but only in the context of pre-defined TDM triples, making it similar in limitation to [1]. Consequently, these methods are not adaptable to the dynamic nature of emerging benchmarks, where new tasks, datasets, and metrics are frequently introduced.
In a parallel research thread, scientific leaderboards have been treated as a form of scientific knowledge graphs composed of Task, Dataset, and Metric entities. Information extraction in this context has been widely studied, as seen in works such as [5, 7, 8, 9, 10]. These studies typically focus on linking scientific content to structured entities but often assume that the schema or triples are known in advance.
In contrast to prior work, our approach addresses several critical limitations. We construct our dataset manually from publications to ensure comprehensive TDMR annotations, rather than relying on incomplete or unstandardized third-party sources. We normalize TDMR tuples for accurate leaderboard construction and propose novel experimental settings that reflect realistic, dynamic scenarios, including cold-start conditions without any pre-defined TDM taxonomy. By simultaneously constructing a scientific knowledge graph and linking publications to it in an open-domain setting, our method is inherently adaptable to the evolving research landscape.

ANSWER: Several efforts have been made to automate the extraction and organization of performance results from scientific publications. Early work by [1] introduced a framework, TDMS-IE, for identifying task, dataset, metric, and score tuples from NLP papers, establishing a foundation for automatic leaderboard construction. Similarly, AxCell [2] proposes a robust pipeline that utilizes structural analysis and novel table segmentation techniques to extract results from machine learning papers, demonstrating significant improvements over prior methods. ORKG-Leaderboards [3] offers a systematic approach that integrates leaderboard extraction into a knowledge graph framework, enabling machine-actionable publishing and dynamic visualization of state-of-the-art performance. TELIN [4] focuses on extracting leaderboard-relevant entities from PDFs using a semi-automated approach that reduces human annotation needs through targeted entity refinement.
In addition to these extraction systems, several datasets and benchmarks have been introduced to facilitate the development and evaluation of leaderboard construction tools. LEGOBench [5] provides a large-scale benchmark derived from arXiv and PapersWithCode, and evaluates both language model-based and graph-based approaches. SciERC and its associated framework SciIE [6] support multi-task extraction of entities and relations, enabling construction of scientific knowledge graphs. SciREX [7] extends information extraction to the document level, capturing relationships that span across sections, which is critical for leaderboard generation from full papers.
Other contributions include TDMSci [8], a specialized corpus annotated with task, dataset, and metric entities, which supports the development of more accurate extraction models. SciNLP-KG [9] introduces methods for extracting entity relations from NLP literature to build a knowledge graph, highlighting its potential use in leaderboard automation. Additionally, a diachronic analysis of NLP research trends by [10] shows the evolving influence of tasks, methods, and datasets, providing a broader context for understanding the dynamic nature of scientific benchmarks.

[ANSWER]: Bayesian optimization (BO) originated from early work on global optimization of expensive black‑box functions and has since become a standard tool for problems where evaluations are scarce.  It has been successfully applied to hyper‑parameter tuning of machine learning models, materials discovery, drug design, and robot locomotion, among many other domains.  

A substantial strand of the BO literature addresses the fact that evaluation costs may vary across the search space.  Much of this research is situated in the multi‑fidelity setting, where cheaper approximations of the objective are available and algorithms exploit correlations between fidelities to trade off information gain against cost.  In the single‑fidelity case, only a few approaches explicitly model heterogeneous costs, focusing on incorporating cost information into the acquisition function while retaining the standard BO framework.  

One of the earliest cost‑aware acquisition functions is expected improvement per unit of cost (EI‑PUC), which assumes that the evaluation cost at a candidate point is known.  When the cost is unknown, subsequent work has either taken expectations over the improvement‑to‑cost ratio or replaced the denominator by an estimated mean cost.  A related variant, EI‑PUC with cost cooling (EI‑PUC‑CC), introduces a budget‑dependent exponent that gradually relaxes the penalty on expensive evaluations as the remaining budget shrinks.  Both methods treat the cost term deterministically and do not incorporate uncertainty about the cost function nor measure the budget‑dependent value of information in a principled manner.  

A concurrent line of research tackles budgeted BO with heterogeneous costs using a non‑myopic perspective.  One such approach formulates the problem as a finite‑horizon constrained Markov decision process (MDP) and employs a rollout acquisition function based on a heuristic base policy that interleaves a few steps of EI‑PUC with a final step of standard EI.  This method approximates a single step of policy improvement over the heuristic but relies on a fixed horizon and introduces an artificial zero‑reward, zero‑cost state to accommodate short trajectories.  

Dividing a cost‑agnostic acquisition function by a cost term is a widely used heuristic for handling cost heterogeneity.  This idea parallels the “value‑to‑cost” heuristic in knapsack problems, where selecting items with the highest value‑to‑cost ratio yields a provable ½‑approximation to the optimal additive value.  However, the BO objective is non‑additive—the value of a set of evaluations is the maximum observed function value rather than the sum—so the theoretical guarantees for knapsack do not carry over, and the heuristic can be arbitrarily suboptimal in the optimization setting.  

The multi‑armed bandit literature has also explored heterogeneous evaluation costs, proposing optimistic policies that maximize reward‑to‑cost ratios.  While effective for cumulative regret minimization, these strategies are ill‑suited for pure‑exploration or best‑arm identification problems, where the goal is to locate the single best point rather than accrue reward over time.  

Non‑myopic BO methods aim to account for the impact of future evaluations when assessing acquisition values.  Numerous approaches construct look‑ahead acquisition functions or employ dynamic programming techniques, yet most assume homogeneous costs or ignore budget constraints.  Among the few that consider heterogeneous costs, only the aforementioned finite‑horizon constrained MDP method explicitly incorporates a budget, leaving a gap for approaches that handle unknown costs and random evaluation horizons.  

Building on these observations, the present work introduces a principled framework for budgeted BO with unknown and heterogeneous evaluation costs.  It models the optimization process as an MDP with a random horizon dictated by the remaining budget, thereby avoiding artificial zero‑cost states and aligning the decision process with the natural stopping condition.  Within this formulation, a budgeted multi‑step expected improvement (B‑MS‑EI) acquisition function is derived as a tractable approximation to the optimal non‑myopic policy.  Theoretical analysis demonstrates that standard EI and its cost‑normalized counterpart can be arbitrarily worse than the optimal policy under budget constraints, highlighting the need for a truly non‑myopic treatment.  Extensive experiments on synthetic benchmarks and real‑world problems confirm that B‑MS‑EI consistently outperforms existing cost‑aware and non‑myopic acquisition functions, offering a robust solution for optimization tasks where evaluation costs are both heterogeneous and initially unknown.

[ANSWER]: Contrastive learning (CL) was originally introduced for training convolutional neural networks on image data and has since achieved notable success across a range of visual tasks. When the CL paradigm is transferred to graph neural networks (GNNs), the resulting graph contrastive learning (GCL) faces distinct challenges. Unlike images, which naturally yield multiple views through simple transformations such as color jitter or cropping, graphs are irregular structures whose topology encodes essential information. Consequently, constructing meaningful contrastive pairs for GCL requires careful manipulation of graph components. Early GCL approaches generate pairs by contrasting different parts of a graph, for example nodes versus whole graphs, node‑to‑node pairs, or node‑to‑subgraph pairs. Other methods rely on graph data augmentations (GDAs) that perturb the structure, such as edge perturbation, node dropping, subgraph sampling, or feature masking. A systematic investigation of these augmentations was carried out in GraphCL, which evaluated many combinations of GDAs to identify effective augmentation strategies. Subsequent works such as MVGRL and GCA incorporated domain‑specific knowledge from network science, using centrality measures to guide augmentation. Although these studies demonstrated the importance of augmentation design, they treated augmentations as fixed, manually chosen operations and did not explore learning augmentation policies directly.

The InfoMin principle was recently proposed as a theoretical framework for designing augmentations in contrastive learning. InfoMin posits that optimal augmentations should retain information that is minimally shared between views while preserving task‑relevant features. This principle, however, relies on supervision from downstream tasks to identify the “sweet spot” augmentation that satisfies specific mutual‑information constraints. The requirement of downstream‑task knowledge limits the applicability of InfoMin in settings where such labels are unavailable. Moreover, the original formulation of InfoMin was demonstrated on convolutional architectures for image data and does not address the additional complexities introduced by graph structures, such as irregular topology and the limited expressive power of GNNs. As a result, direct adoption of InfoMin to GCL remains challenging.

In this work, we introduce an adversarially trained graph contrastive learning framework that jointly optimizes a GNN encoder and a learnable graph augmentation module. By framing augmentation as an adversarial player that seeks to reduce redundant information while preserving discriminative features, the proposed method aligns the encoder with the information‑bottleneck objective without requiring downstream‑task supervision. We provide theoretical analysis showing that, under appropriate regularization of the augmentation space, the learned augmentations yield lower bounds on task‑relevant information and upper bounds on redundant information, thereby reconciling InfoMax and information‑bottleneck principles. To instantiate the framework, we design a trainable edge‑dropping augmenter that assigns non‑uniform, graph‑dependent drop probabilities, enabling adaptive, task‑agnostic perturbations. Extensive experiments on 18 benchmark datasets covering molecular property regression, molecular classification, and social‑network classification demonstrate consistent improvements over state‑of‑the‑art GCL methods in unsupervised, transfer, and semi‑supervised settings, with relative gains of up to 14 % in unsupervised learning. Ablation studies further confirm that the adversarially optimized augmentations outperform fixed, manually crafted GDAs across a wide range of perturbation levels, highlighting the effectiveness of learning augmentation policies for robust and transferable graph representations.

[ANSWER]: Our work builds directly on the line of research that establishes fast learning rates for non‑parametric regression under minimal assumptions. In the iid setting, [1] demonstrated that a one‑sided isometry condition suffices for optimal excess‑risk bounds, while [2] extended this insight to linear regression on trajectories generated by linear dynamical systems, showing that mixing only appears as a higher‑order term. We generalize both ideas: by proving a trajectory‑wise hypercontractivity condition for the least‑squares estimator, we obtain mixing‑free leading rates for a broad class of non‑parametric models, thereby unifying and extending the one‑sided isometry framework to dependent data.

A substantial body of literature on learning from dependent data relies on the blocking technique to recover independence at the cost of an effective sample size that is reduced by the mixing time (e.g., [8], [9], [10]). Recent lower bounds [9] show that such degradation is unavoidable in the worst‑case agnostic setting, but that in the realizable linear case the mixing time influences only the burn‑in period. Our contribution is to demonstrate that, under mild ergodicity and hypercontractivity assumptions, the same phenomenon holds for general non‑parametric hypothesis classes. Unlike the algorithm‑specific guarantees of [9], our result is algorithm‑agnostic and applies directly to the empirical risk minimizer, providing a unified excess‑risk bound that matches the iid rate after a finite burn‑in.

The information‑theoretic analysis of the non‑parametric least‑squares estimator in [7] proceeds by establishing two‑sided concentration for both the version space and the noise interaction, which leads to bounds that deteriorate with the mixing time. By contrast, our proof circumvents the need for two‑sided concentration, using a martingale analogue of the offset Rademacher complexity (as introduced in [6]) to capture the stochastic fluctuations of the LSE. This yields a bound where the dependence on the mixing time is relegated to a higher‑order term, improving upon the rates of [7] for processes with slow mixing.

Recent extensions of the one‑sided isometry approach to dependent data, such as [10], retain the powerful handling of heavy‑tailed noise and non‑realizability but ultimately rely on two‑sided concentration arguments, which again cause their rates to suffer from mixing‑time penalties. Our analysis shows that, in the realizable setting, these penalties can be eliminated altogether under the hypercontractivity condition, thereby delivering optimal excess‑risk guarantees without sacrificing robustness to dependence.

The dependence measure employed in our analysis originates from [11] and has been applied to learning problems with non‑sequential covariate structures in [12]. While [12] focuses on settings such as graph‑based data where the risk bounds degrade with the correlation measure, our results exploit the same dependence metric to show that, for time‑series satisfying trajectory hypercontractivity, the excess risk remains essentially unaffected by the correlation strength, highlighting a fundamentally different behavior in the sequential setting.

Finally, a line of work on system identification has established that lack of ergodicity does not necessarily impair learning rates for linear dynamical systems ([2], [13]–[17]) and for models with generalized linear‑model transitions ([18]–[21]). Our main theorem, when instantiated to these settings, recovers the known optimal excess‑risk and parameter‑recovery bounds for linear systems and improves upon the existing guarantees for GLM‑driven dynamics, delivering the sharpest known excess‑risk bound (up to logarithmic factors) for the latter. Thus, our contribution not only unifies the “little mixing’’ phenomenon across a wide range of models but also advances the state‑of‑the‑art in both linear and non‑linear system identification.

[ANSWER]: Learning to achieve arbitrary goals has been studied for decades in both control and reinforcement‑learning (RL) literature.  Early goal‑conditioned approaches treat the problem as a multi‑task, reward‑driven learning problem that assumes access to a goal‑conditioned reward function, while more recent “reward‑free’’ methods rely on hindsight relabeling or goal‑conditioned behavior cloning.  These techniques excel on short‑horizon benchmarks but, without additional assumptions such as deterministic dynamics or dense online data collection, they provide no guarantee of optimality and typically falter on tasks that require long sequences of coordinated actions.  Our work directly addresses this gap by introducing a curriculum‑generation mechanism that automatically produces intermediate waypoints, thereby enabling efficient learning of policies that can reliably reach distant goals without relying on dense rewards or offline demonstrations.

A prominent line of research augments goal‑conditioned RL with graph‑based planning, interpreting learned value functions as dynamical distances and using them to stitch together sub‑goals at test time.  Although such two‑phase pipelines have achieved impressive results on navigation and manipulation, they suffer from two fundamental drawbacks: (i) the planning phase is decoupled from policy learning, so the collected experience cannot be improved by the planner; and (ii) deploying a policy requires costly online graph queries, which limits real‑time applicability.  By performing graph search only during training, our algorithm embeds the planner inside the data‑generation process, producing higher‑quality trajectories that accelerate policy learning.  Consequently, the learned policy can be executed without any test‑time search, reducing deployment latency dramatically.  This training‑time planning distinguishes our method from prior approaches such as RIS, which modify the learning objective but still rely on post‑hoc planning; instead, we use the planner to shape the curriculum, yielding both better data efficiency and lower inference cost.

Effective exploration in goal‑conditioned settings hinges on how goals (or sub‑goals) are sampled.  Existing methods design separate exploration bonuses or curiosity‑driven objectives that are optimized independently of the policy learning objective, often leading to a mismatch between the distribution of sampled goals and the capabilities of the current policy.  In contrast, our approach jointly optimizes the waypoint‑sampling distribution and the goal‑conditioned policy within a unified expectation‑maximization framework.  The E‑step (graph search) proposes waypoints that are reachable given the current policy, while the M‑step (policy update) improves the ability to reach those waypoints.  This tight coupling ensures that exploration is always directed toward states that are both informative and attainable, a property we empirically demonstrate yields superior sample efficiency on long‑horizon navigation and high‑dimensional manipulation benchmarks.

Finally, our method builds on the perspective that RL can be cast as probabilistic inference, where variational objectives correspond to planning operations.  Prior work has shown that, for flat RL problems, variational inference reduces to graph search over state‑space distances.  We extend this inference‑centric view to hierarchical models by treating waypoints as latent variables and using variational EM to jointly infer their optimal placement and the policy that reaches them.  This hierarchical inference formulation not only unifies goal‑conditioned RL and graph search under a single objective but also enables the automatic emergence of curricula that are provably optimal with respect to the variational lower bound.  Through this lens, our contribution is a principled, inference‑driven algorithm that leverages training‑time planning to overcome the long‑horizon limitations of existing goal‑conditioned RL methods.
