[ANSWER]: Researchers of emergent communication investigate methods that enable agents to develop communication protocols in reinforcement‑learning environments, often using reference or Lewis signalling games in which successful task completion depends on effective signaling [1]–[4]. In many “cheap‑talk” settings, agents exchange real‑valued vectors, while other work emphasizes discrete communication among decentralized agents that cannot share weights or gradients during training or execution [5]. Early approaches to discrete signaling frequently employed one‑hot messages; for example, Foerster et al. introduced binary discrete messages, but subsequent studies have largely returned to one‑hot vector representations [6]–[11].

A recurring challenge in emergent communication is the difficulty of producing protocols that are interpretable by humans or by agents trained separately, a problem often framed as zero‑shot learning [12]. Recent investigations have identified environmental properties that promote zero‑shot continuous communication [13], [14]. Efforts to render emergent signals human‑understandable fall into two complementary strands. Bottom‑up approaches align learned tokens with natural language, demonstrating that humans can infer meanings of agent‑generated symbols in zero‑shot contexts [15], [16]; Lazaridou et al. showed, for instance, that a token for “dolphin” could be interpreted as referring to a water scene [8]. Top‑down methods, by contrast, leverage pretrained task‑specific language models to mediate communication in natural language [17].

In the broader natural‑language‑processing literature, early neural models represented words with one‑hot vectors, but contemporary techniques rely on dense embeddings such as Word2Vec, GloVe, and related methods, which capture semantic relationships in continuous spaces and improve both training efficiency and generalization [18]–[21]. These embeddings illustrate how discrete linguistic items can be organized within a semantically meaningful manifold, a perspective that has motivated recent proposals to move beyond one‑hot tokens in emergent communication.

Research on zero‑ and few‑shot classification addresses the ability to assign correct labels to novel classes with little or no training data [22]–[24]. Many approaches incorporate side information—such as labeled data from auxiliary domains or linguistic cues—to boost performance [23], [25]. While primarily focused on classification, this line of work shares the goal of enabling zero‑shot understanding by exploiting auxiliary semantic information.

[ANSWER]: Fairness in machine learning has been framed along two complementary dimensions. The individual‑fairness perspective requires that similar individuals receive similar outcomes, formalized as a Lipschitz‑type condition on the predictor [2]. The group‑fairness perspective, in contrast, focuses on statistical parity across demographic groups, demanding that protected attributes not influence the distribution of predictions [1].

Approaches to achieving fairness are commonly organized into three processing stages. Preprocessing methods transform the training data so that it satisfies a chosen fairness criterion before model learning; early examples include reweighting and relabeling techniques that adjust class distributions or feature representations [3, 4]. Postprocessing methods modify the outputs of a trained model, for instance by applying calibrated thresholds or randomized decision rules to enforce demographic parity or equalized odds [5]. In‑processing methods incorporate fairness considerations directly into the learning algorithm, either by adding regularization terms, imposing constraints during optimization, or altering the model architecture; this category encompasses a wide range of algorithms that aim to reduce unfairness while preserving predictive performance [6‑9].

Within the in‑processing literature, one line of work explicitly projects an initially unfair predictor onto a set of models that satisfy fairness constraints. Such projections often measure distance between models with information‑theoretic divergences; the Kullback–Leibler (KL) divergence has been widely employed to define the closest fair model under various constraint formulations [10‑12]. A complementary line of research draws on optimal transport (OT) theory to quantify the cost of modifying distributions. Several recent studies have used OT barycenters—distributions that minimize the summed transport cost to each group’s empirical measure—to align group‑specific data or predictions with a common reference. These barycentric approaches have been applied to preprocessing [15], classification [11, 16] and regression [17, 18], effectively moving each demographic distribution toward a shared target. While intuitive for a small number of discrete groups, barycentric methods assume that fairness can be expressed as equality among group measures, limiting their flexibility for settings with continuous or multiple sensitive attributes and often requiring the transport cost to be a true metric.

Other works have explored unregularized OT projections onto fairness‑constrained sets, using the resulting transport cost as a diagnostic of group fairness [19]. This perspective treats the OT projection primarily as a test rather than as an integrated component of model training. Overall, the literature reflects a spectrum of strategies—from data‑level adjustments and output‑level corrections to model‑level constraints—each leveraging different notions of distance or divergence to operationalize fairness across individuals and groups.

While these sources are popular, they lack rigorous quality control, including standardized TDM entity representation and complete publication coverage. Some efforts, such as [1] and [2], go further by extracting TDM triples and result values, and normalizing them for leaderboard construction. However, these approaches are constrained by their reliance on a closed domain, requiring extracted triples to match a pre-defined TDM taxonomy. Other studies, such as [3] and [4], only extract TDM triples without result values, thereby forgoing leaderboard construction. Meanwhile, [6] focuses on extracting result values, but only in the context of pre-defined TDM triples, making it similar in limitation to [1]. Consequently, these methods are not adaptable to the dynamic nature of emerging benchmarks, where new tasks, datasets, and metrics are frequently introduced.
In a parallel research thread, scientific leaderboards have been treated as a form of scientific knowledge graphs composed of Task, Dataset, and Metric entities. Information extraction in this context has been widely studied, as seen in works such as [5, 7, 8, 9, 10]. These studies typically focus on linking scientific content to structured entities but often assume that the schema or triples are known in advance.
In contrast to prior work, our approach addresses several critical limitations. We construct our dataset manually from publications to ensure comprehensive TDMR annotations, rather than relying on incomplete or unstandardized third-party sources. We normalize TDMR tuples for accurate leaderboard construction and propose novel experimental settings that reflect realistic, dynamic scenarios, including cold-start conditions without any pre-defined TDM taxonomy. By simultaneously constructing a scientific knowledge graph and linking publications to it in an open-domain setting, our method is inherently adaptable to the evolving research landscape.

ANSWER: Several efforts have been made to automate the extraction and organization of performance results from scientific publications. Early work by [1] introduced a framework, TDMS-IE, for identifying task, dataset, metric, and score tuples from NLP papers, establishing a foundation for automatic leaderboard construction. Similarly, AxCell [2] proposes a robust pipeline that utilizes structural analysis and novel table segmentation techniques to extract results from machine learning papers, demonstrating significant improvements over prior methods. ORKG-Leaderboards [3] offers a systematic approach that integrates leaderboard extraction into a knowledge graph framework, enabling machine-actionable publishing and dynamic visualization of state-of-the-art performance. TELIN [4] focuses on extracting leaderboard-relevant entities from PDFs using a semi-automated approach that reduces human annotation needs through targeted entity refinement.
In addition to these extraction systems, several datasets and benchmarks have been introduced to facilitate the development and evaluation of leaderboard construction tools. LEGOBench [5] provides a large-scale benchmark derived from arXiv and PapersWithCode, and evaluates both language model-based and graph-based approaches. SciERC and its associated framework SciIE [6] support multi-task extraction of entities and relations, enabling construction of scientific knowledge graphs. SciREX [7] extends information extraction to the document level, capturing relationships that span across sections, which is critical for leaderboard generation from full papers.
Other contributions include TDMSci [8], a specialized corpus annotated with task, dataset, and metric entities, which supports the development of more accurate extraction models. SciNLP-KG [9] introduces methods for extracting entity relations from NLP literature to build a knowledge graph, highlighting its potential use in leaderboard automation. Additionally, a diachronic analysis of NLP research trends by [10] shows the evolving influence of tasks, methods, and datasets, providing a broader context for understanding the dynamic nature of scientific benchmarks.

[ANSWER]: Conformal prediction has been extended to settings with distribution shift by leveraging weighted exchangeability, as demonstrated in several recent works. Notably, earlier studies introduced CP frameworks that accommodate covariate shift and applied them to counterfactual inference, providing finite‑sample predictive intervals for treatment and control outcomes. Our approach builds on this line of research but departs from treating the shift as a change in the marginal input distribution; instead, we model it as a shift in the conditional outcome distribution induced by a new target policy. This perspective enables us to construct intervals that are valid under stochastic policies and continuous action spaces—settings that were not addressed by the prior methods—thereby broadening the applicability of CP to a wider class of off‑policy evaluation problems.

A separate strand of work has employed conformal prediction within contextual bandits, primarily to design robust action‑selection rules for discrete‑action problems. Those methods use CP to choose actions rather than to evaluate a fixed target policy, making the problem formulation orthogonal to ours. In contrast, our contribution is an off‑policy evaluation technique that treats the target policy as given and produces adaptive predictive intervals for its outcomes. By integrating out the actions in the conformal weights, our method utilizes the full calibration dataset, which reduces variance and yields tighter intervals compared with approaches that split calibration data by action. This design choice directly addresses the need for efficient use of limited observational data while preserving finite‑sample coverage guarantees.

The majority of uncertainty quantification approaches for contextual bandits focus on estimating the expected policy value and provide asymptotic or finite‑sample guarantees for this scalar metric. While such methods can be extended to risk‑sensitive objectives (e.g., CVaR), they typically lack adaptivity to context‑specific features and do not furnish interval‑level coverage guarantees, often resulting in overly conservative bounds. Our contribution fills this gap by delivering predictive intervals that are (i) adaptive to covariates, (ii) capture the full variability of the outcome distribution, and (iii) enjoy rigorous finite‑sample marginal coverage with additional conditions that guarantee conditional coverage. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first conformal‑based off‑policy evaluation technique that simultaneously satisfies all three properties for stochastic policies and continuous action spaces.

[ANSWER]: Imbalanced classification problems are pervasive in real‑world scenarios and have attracted extensive attention from both academia and industry. The majority of existing studies address class‑imbalanced quantity distributions, where models typically achieve substantially higher inference performance on majority classes than on minority classes. Approaches for mitigating quantity imbalance can be broadly categorized into data‑selection methods and model‑training techniques. Classical data‑selection strategies include active learning and various re‑sampling schemes, which aim to construct a quantity‑balanced training set. In the model‑training phase, re‑weighting methods adjust the contribution of training samples from different classes based on class frequencies, providing a simple yet effective remedy for imbalance.

Directly transferring these techniques to graph‑structured data often neglects graph‑specific topology‑imbalance issues. While traditional re‑weighting operates at the class level, some works propose finer‑grained weighting schemes that assign importance to individual nodes. In the context of data cleaning, methods such as Tomek links, NearMiss, and One‑Sided Selection attempt to exclude samples near class boundaries by measuring feature similarity. However, for graph data the relational information encoded in edges is generally more reliable than raw feature similarity, especially when the number of labeled nodes is limited, making similarity‑based boundary detection less robust.

Graph data appear in diverse domains, including social media, finance, e‑commerce, healthcare, and transportation, and graph node representation learning also suffers from quantity imbalance. Beyond generic quantity‑balancing approaches, several graph‑specific methods have been proposed. DR‑GCN introduces class‑conditioned adversarial training and a latent distribution constraint on unlabeled nodes to address imbalance. RA‑GCN automatically learns class‑wise sample weights within an adversarial training framework. AdaGCN leverages boosting to handle imbalance in node classification tasks. GraphSMOTE synthesizes new nodes and edges to up‑sample minority classes. These methods focus primarily on correcting quantity imbalance while largely overlooking the influence of topology imbalance.

Other studies have explored the positioning of nodes relative to selected anchors by measuring distances in the graph, aiming to infer absolute node locations. Such approaches often treat all anchor nodes uniformly and ignore class‑specific information, which can limit their ability to capture nuanced class boundaries.

[ANSWER]: Robust classification has been extensively studied, and the majority of approaches assume a uniform robustness radius that applies to every input.  Early and influential works on adversarial attacks and defenses—including those by Szegedy et al., Goodfellow et al., Carlini & Wagner, Madry et al., and many subsequent papers—focus on neural‑network models and enforce norm‑ball constraints (typically ℓ₂ or ℓ∞) of a fixed size around each example.  These methods analyze the trade‑off between accuracy and robustness under the assumption that the same perturbation budget is relevant for all regions of the data distribution.

A line of research has explored the possibility of using input‑dependent robustness budgets.  Methods such as those proposed by Zhang et al. and Wang et al. train neural networks with varying radii across examples, trading off robustness and accuracy in a data‑dependent manner.  While these approaches relax the uniform‑radius assumption, they remain centered on neural‑network architectures, retain norm‑ball perturbation sets, and are primarily evaluated empirically rather than through a unified theoretical framework.

Connections between robustness and large‑margin classification have also been investigated.  The support‑vector machine (SVM) and its variants constitute the classic large‑margin linear classifier, seeking to maximize the minimal distance between training points and the decision boundary.  More recent works have extended large‑margin ideas to nonlinear settings, for example by constructing Lipschitz‑constrained classifiers, by optimizing worst‑case margins for deep networks, or by learning metric embeddings that yield large‑margin nearest‑neighbor classifiers.  These studies aim to increase the global margin of a model, whereas the robustness literature often focuses on ensuring a fixed perturbation radius rather than adapting the margin locally.

Robustness of non‑parametric classifiers has been examined both from an attack and a defense perspective.  Several papers have demonstrated that nearest‑neighbor, kernel, and histogram methods are vulnerable to adversarial perturbations.  Defensive strategies include adversarial pruning of the training set to improve 1‑NN robustness (Wang et al.) and the development of the so‑called “max‑astute” classifier, which maximizes accuracy on points that are robust under a prescribed radius (Yang et al.).  These works highlight the challenges of achieving both high accuracy and adversarial robustness with instance‑based methods.

Theoretical analyses of weight‑function‑based estimators have provided conditions under which such non‑parametric methods converge to optimal classifiers in the large‑sample limit.  Stone’s classic results and subsequent extensions establish that, for distributions with a positive separation margin between classes, k‑nearest‑neighbor and kernel estimators satisfy consistency requirements.  More recent studies have shown that, after preprocessing steps such as adversarial pruning to enforce class separation, these estimators can achieve both robustness and accuracy asymptotically.  These analyses differ in the target optimality notion and in the technical assumptions required for convergence.

Concurrent research has introduced a related limiting classifier termed the “margin‑canonical Bayes” classifier.  That line of work focuses on a data‑augmentation scheme designed to guarantee convergence of certain algorithms to this limit, emphasizing algorithmic modifications rather than the intrinsic properties of classical non‑parametric estimators.

In this paper we introduce the neighborhood‑preserving Bayes optimal classifier, a new large‑sample limit that extends the Bayes optimal decision rule beyond the support of the data by labeling each out‑of‑support point with the class of its nearest in‑support neighbor.  This construction yields maximal robustness regions while preserving Bayes‑optimal accuracy, thereby eliminating the traditional robustness‑accuracy trade‑off.  We formulate sufficient conditions under which weight‑function estimators converge to this limit, and we prove that k‑nearest‑neighbor classifiers satisfy these conditions when the number of neighbors grows sub‑linearly with the sample size, and that kernel classifiers converge provided the kernel has faster‑than‑polynomial decay and the bandwidth shrinks sufficiently slowly.  Conversely, we demonstrate that histogram estimators may fail to converge to the neighborhood‑preserving Bayes optimal even when they are consistent for the standard Bayes rule, and we establish a tight lower bound showing that the sub‑linear growth condition on k is both necessary and sufficient for nearest‑neighbor convergence.  Together, these results provide a comprehensive theoretical foundation for achieving locally adaptive robustness with classical non‑parametric methods, and they clarify the relationship between existing robust‑optimal notions and the proposed neighborhood‑preserving formulation.

[ANSWER]: Existing approaches for promoting diversity in neural dialogue generation can be broadly divided into training‑based and decoding‑based methods. Training‑based techniques modify the learning objective or model architecture, for example by introducing alternative loss functions that penalize generic responses, incorporating latent variables to capture diverse utterance intents, or refining the target distribution used in maximum‑likelihood estimation. Auxiliary loss terms have also been employed to directly discourage trivial replies. Decoding‑based methods, on the other hand, apply heuristic strategies during generation such as nucleus sampling, top‑k sampling, or diverse beam search, which are independent of the underlying model parameters and can be combined with any training regime.

The over‑confidence of modern neural networks, wherein models assign excessively high probabilities to their predictions, has been identified as a factor that hampers response diversity. A range of calibration techniques has been proposed, including temperature scaling, confidence‑penalizing regularizers, and loss modifications that encourage smoother output distributions. These methods aim to reduce the certainty of predictions, thereby mitigating the tendency of dialogue systems to produce repetitive or bland utterances.

Knowledge distillation offers another line of related work, wherein a teacher model provides soft target distributions for training a student model, typically by minimizing a Kullback‑Leibler divergence term. In the context of dialogue generation, approaches such as C‑MLM fine‑tune a large pretrained BERT model to serve as the teacher, supplying context‑aware token probabilities that guide the student. While effective, such methods rely on heavyweight teacher architectures and require separate forward passes for each training instance, which can be computationally demanding.

In contrast, the present work introduces an adaptive label‑smoothing technique that dynamically constructs soft target distributions based on the model’s own confidence at each decoding step. By scaling the probability assigned to the ground‑truth token according to the maximum predicted confidence, the method prevents the model from becoming over‑confident while preserving informative gradients for low‑frequency words. A lightweight, single‑layer bi‑directional decoder is employed to generate context‑aware supervision signals for non‑target tokens, and a target‑mask attention mechanism ensures that the target word is excluded from its own prediction, enabling fully parallel training and inference. This approach integrates seamlessly with existing decoding‑based diversity methods, addresses calibration shortcomings highlighted in prior calibration literature, and avoids the computational overhead of large teacher models used in distillation‑based schemes. Empirical evaluation on benchmark dialogue datasets demonstrates that the proposed method substantially improves response diversity without sacrificing fluency or coherence.

[ANSWER]: Adversarial neural networks have been employed to reduce distributional divergence in several studies. Early work introduced an architecture based on gradient reversal and a shared feature extractor, focusing on a binary domain discriminator [1, 2]. Extending this line of research, a multinomial adversarial framework was proposed for domain adaptation, allowing the discriminator to handle multiple classes simultaneously [3].

In the context of weak supervision, adversarial learning has been applied to different aspects of the problem. Virtual adversarial training was used to perturb input representations as a general regularization technique, without being specific to weakly supervised settings [4]. Generative adversarial mechanisms have also been leveraged to select hard negative training instances that are difficult to distinguish from heuristically annotated examples, thereby improving classifier robustness [5].

A substantial body of work has addressed label denoising for weakly supervised learning. Approaches such as those presented in [6–8] aim to correct noisy labels generated by weak supervision sources. Snorkel introduced a probabilistic framework that models the biases and correlations of labeling functions, providing a general solution for aggregating weak supervision signals, though it does not explicitly account for feature- or representation-level biases [9]. More recent methods continue to focus on denoising weakly supervised data, exemplified by a recent approach that integrates denoising directly into the training process [10]. Additionally, the Knodle framework offers a comprehensive benchmark for comparing various techniques that enhance weakly supervised learning, providing standardized datasets and evaluation protocols for the community.

[ANSWER]: The literature on sample‑efficient sequential decision‑making has long emphasized principled information‑acquisition strategies, most notably information‑directed sampling (IDS) and its variants. These works establish the information ratio as a clean objective that balances expected regret against information gain about the optimal action, and they provide elegant theoretical guarantees for algorithms that minimize this ratio. However, all existing formulations measure information solely with respect to the optimal action, implicitly assuming that the designer’s learning target is fixed and optimal. In contrast, our contribution is to extend the IDS framework so that the information ratio quantifies regret and information gain about *any* learning target computed via rate‑distortion theory. By doing so, we preserve the analytical tractability of IDS while enabling designers to specify a spectrum of satisficing targets, thereby directly addressing the gap between efficient information acquisition and flexible target selection.

Parallel strands of research have applied rate‑distortion theory to sequential decision problems, showing that the minimal number of bits required to identify a target can be traded off against a tolerable performance shortfall. Prior work operationalizes the Blahut‑Arimoto algorithm to compute such satisficing targets and embeds them within a Thompson‑sampling policy (the BLASTS algorithm). Although BLASTS demonstrates that agents can automatically adjust their learning target over time, it inherits the sub‑optimal exploration behavior of probability‑matching, which limits sample efficiency. Our work builds on this foundation by replacing the Thompson‑sampling component with an IDS‑based action selection rule, yielding the Blahut‑Arimoto Information‑Directed Sampling (BLAIDS) algorithm. BLAIDS retains the single‑parameter control over the target‑performance trade‑off while achieving strictly better regret‑information trade‑offs than BLASTS, thereby unifying optimal target computation with optimal information acquisition.

A third line of research focuses on the Blahut‑Arimoto algorithm itself, which is celebrated in information theory for solving rate‑distortion problems via alternating optimization. Numerous studies have refined its convergence properties and computational speed, yet these advances have not been leveraged in the context of sequential decision‑making. Our paper adopts the classical Blahut‑Arimoto routine as a black‑box sub‑procedure for generating learning targets, but we deliberately keep the algorithmic core unchanged to isolate the effect of the novel IDS‑based exploration scheme. By doing so, we provide a clear baseline that demonstrates the performance gains attributable solely to the new information‑ratio formulation, while also highlighting that future work could further accelerate BLAIDS by incorporating recent Blahut‑Arimoto enhancements.

Finally, while earlier efforts have either (i) focused exclusively on optimal‑action information (IDS) or (ii) on satisficing‑target computation without optimal exploration (BLASTS), no prior work has presented a unified algorithm that simultaneously optimizes both dimensions. Our contribution fills this exact niche: we introduce a principled, hyperparameter‑controlled method for jointly designing learning targets via rate‑distortion theory and acquiring information about them through a generalized information‑directed sampling rule. Empirically, we show that BLAIDS outperforms both vanilla IDS (which lacks target flexibility) and BLASTS (which lacks optimal exploration), establishing a new state‑of‑the‑art for sample‑efficient, target‑aware sequential decision‑making.

[ANSWER]: Exploration in reinforcement learning has been addressed through a variety of intrinsic‑motivation mechanisms. Classical approaches such as ε‑greedy and count‑based exploration quantify novelty over an agent’s entire lifetime in a fixed environment. Curiosity‑driven methods augment the reward with signals derived from prediction error, density estimation, or random network distillation, and have demonstrated success on hard‑exploration benchmarks like Montezuma’s Revenge and Pitfall. More recent work has extended intrinsic motivation to contextual Markov decision processes, where each episode presents a different environment instance. These CMDP‑focused methods introduce episodic novelty measures, often incorporating count‑based bonuses computed within a single episode, to encourage exploration across a distribution of contexts.

A complementary line of research generates curricula over CMDP variations to facilitate learning. Goal‑conditioned and goal‑free algorithms automatically select or modify environment contexts, effectively shaping a curiosity‑driven exploration process at the level of task distribution. Such curricula assume that the learner can actively configure or sample contexts, and they have been combined with intrinsic‑motivation signals to improve sample efficiency in procedurally generated and embodied‑AI settings.

Elliptical bonuses have a long tradition in the contextual bandit literature, where they provide confidence‑bound style exploration incentives for single‑step decision problems. Building on this foundation, several multi‑step reinforcement‑learning algorithms have incorporated elliptical bonuses. The PC‑PG method leverages linear dynamics and a policy‑cover‑based elliptical term to obtain provable exploration guarantees. Other approaches approximate elliptical bonuses with linear regressors trained on random noise or embed them within learned dynamics models. All of these algorithms target singleton MDPs and construct the bonus over the entire learning horizon rather than on a per‑episode basis.

In contrast, the present work introduces a method that extends count‑based episodic bonuses to continuous state spaces by employing elliptical bonuses at the episode level. An inverse‑dynamics model is used to learn a controllable embedding, enabling the bonus to capture diverse, task‑relevant states within each episode. The resulting approach achieves state‑of‑the‑art performance on a broad suite of MiniHack tasks, matches existing methods on sparse‑reward VizDoom environments, and substantially outperforms prior techniques in reward‑free exploration on Habitat. By combining episodic elliptical incentives with self‑supervised feature learning, the method bridges the gap between singleton‑MDP exploration strategies and CMDP‑specific curricula, offering a scalable solution for high‑dimensional, pixel‑based observations without requiring hand‑crafted inductive biases.

[ANSWER]: Generalization under distribution shift is traditionally tackled by enlarging the training distribution through more data or aggressive augmentations, hoping that the test domain is already covered.  Such “train‑once‑deploy‑anywhere’’ strategies, however, sacrifice performance on any specific shift because the model must spread its capacity across many possible futures.  In contrast, our approach embraces test‑time training: we adapt the model for each incoming sample using a self‑supervised objective, thereby eliminating the need to anticipate every possible shift while preserving high accuracy on the realized one.

Unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA) and transductive learning assume access to a collection of unlabeled target examples, using them to align source and target representations or to fit a local model.  These methods are powerful when a batch of target data is available, yet they remain inapplicable when only a single test image arrives.  We view our method as a one‑sample UDA paradigm: the test image itself provides the self‑supervision signal, so no pre‑collected target set is required and the model can “overfit’’ safely to the sole instance that will be evaluated.

Earlier vision works that continue training at deployment—such as face‑detection bootstrapping, personalized generative‑model fine‑tuning, per‑image super‑resolution, and online teacher‑student video segmentation—rely on multiple test samples or on task‑specific cues to drive adaptation.  Our algorithm differs fundamentally by requiring only the current test image and a generic reconstruction loss, enabling instantaneous adaptation without any external data or task‑specific engineering.

Test‑time training was first popularized with rotation prediction as the auxiliary task.  While rotation is simple to implement, it is either too easy (e.g., outdoor scenes where the horizon reveals orientation) or too hard (e.g., top‑down views where all rotations appear plausible), limiting its usefulness across diverse domains.  We replace rotation with masked autoencoding, a self‑supervised task that leverages the ubiquitous spatial redundancy of natural images.  Empirically, this substitution yields consistent gains on four object‑recognition benchmarks, demonstrating that masked autoencoders provide the right level of difficulty and universality for test‑time adaptation.

Subsequent extensions of test‑time training, such as TTT++ and TENT, presuppose access to a batch or stream of test samples drawn from a common shift, and they aggregate gradients across the batch to stabilize adaptation.  Our method deliberately avoids any batch assumption, performing adaptation independently for each sample.  This design choice simplifies deployment, respects privacy constraints, and matches the theoretical setting of one‑sample learning that we analyze.

Finally, masked autoencoders have become a cornerstone of visual representation learning, with variants that pre‑train large ViT encoders and fine‑tune or linearly probe them for downstream tasks.  We repurpose this paradigm for test‑time training: the encoder is updated on‑the‑fly using the MAE reconstruction loss, while the classification head remains unchanged.  Our theoretical contribution characterizes how this per‑sample encoder update improves the bias‑variance trade‑off in linear models, providing a principled explanation for the observed empirical benefits.  Together, these elements establish masked autoencoding as an effective and theoretically grounded self‑supervised task for test‑time adaptation.

[ANSWER]: The data‑programming paradigm introduced a way to label data programmatically by combining multiple noisy labeling functions (LFs) and treating the true label as a latent variable in a generative probabilistic graphical model (PGM).  Early work focused on learning the parameters of this generative model, and a series of methods have been proposed to mitigate the computational burden of inference, including approximate sampling, variational approximations, and matrix‑based estimators.  A recurring limitation of many of these approaches is their reliance on strong independence assumptions among the LFs; popular libraries and several research studies adopt a conditionally independent model, which can lead to substantial performance degradation when the true dependencies and correlations among the sources are misspecified.  To address this, a line of work has explored learning the intra‑LF dependency structure, either by augmenting the PGM with latent dependency variables or by employing structure‑learning techniques that infer correlation patterns from the unlabeled data.  Separately, co‑training methods share the spirit of leveraging multiple views of the data but typically assume the availability of some labeled examples; recent extensions combine a small labeled set with multiple weak supervision sources, often relying on large pre‑trained language models to achieve strong results, though their applicability to non‑text domains or settings without such models remains uncertain.  

Aggregating noisy labels has also been extensively studied in the crowdsourcing literature, where the goal is to infer the unknown ground‑truth label while modeling worker reliability.  Classical approaches employ expectation‑maximization (EM) or related iterative algorithms to jointly estimate worker accuracies and the latent label, usually under the assumption that worker errors are independent and randomly distributed across instances.  Unlike weak‑supervision settings, crowdsourcing frameworks often consider dynamic task assignment and may optimize which workers label which items.  A number of end‑to‑end methods have been introduced for crowdsourced annotation, particularly for image labeling tasks, that integrate EM‑like worker models directly into the training of discriminative predictors.  These methods typically tailor their assumptions to the crowdsourcing scenario—such as fixed numbers of workers and specific error models—and therefore may not generalize to broader weak‑supervision contexts where the sources are heterogeneous rules, heuristics, or distant‑supervision signals.  

In contrast to the prior approaches described above, the present work proposes a fully end‑to‑end framework for learning downstream models directly from multiple weak‑supervision sources without any labeled data.  By re‑parameterizing the posterior distributions of traditional PGM‑based label models with a neural encoder that produces attention scores for each source, the method jointly optimizes the encoder and the downstream predictor using a shared loss that treats the other component’s predictions as constant targets.  This design eliminates the need for separate generative‑model learning, avoids costly sampling or matrix inversions, and does not rely on conditional independence or correctly specified dependency structures among the LFs.  The approach is also agnostic to the nature of the weak sources, handling both binary heuristics and probabilistic signals, and it remains robust when the sources are highly correlated or adversarial.  Empirical evaluations demonstrate consistent improvements over state‑of‑the‑art latent‑label modeling techniques across several benchmark datasets, including a crowdsourcing task where the method outperforms specialized EM‑based baselines.  An open‑source implementation compatible with arbitrary PyTorch models is released to facilitate adoption in diverse application domains.

ORKG-Leaderboards [3] offers a systematic approach that integrates leaderboard extraction into a knowledge graph framework, enabling machine-actionable publishing and dynamic visualization of state-of-the-art performance. TELIN [4] focuses on extracting leaderboard-relevant entities from PDFs using a semi-automated approach that reduces human annotation needs through targeted entity refinement.
In addition to these extraction systems, several datasets and benchmarks have been introduced to facilitate the development and evaluation of leaderboard construction tools. LEGOBench [5] provides a large-scale benchmark derived from arXiv and PapersWithCode, and evaluates both language model-based and graph-based approaches. SciERC and its associated framework SciIE [6] support multi-task extraction of entities and relations, enabling construction of scientific knowledge graphs. SciREX [7] extends information extraction to the document level, capturing relationships that span across sections, which is critical for leaderboard generation from full papers.
Other contributions include TDMSci [8], a specialized corpus annotated with task, dataset, and metric entities, which supports the development of more accurate extraction models. SciNLP-KG [9] introduces methods for extracting entity relations from NLP literature to build a knowledge graph, highlighting its potential use in leaderboard automation. Additionally, a diachronic analysis of NLP research trends by [10] shows the evolving influence of tasks, methods, and datasets, providing a broader context for understanding the dynamic nature of scientific benchmarks.

[ANSWER]: The issue of fairness in rankings first emerged from independent audits of recommender systems and search engines, which revealed that results could systematically disadvantage relevant social groups [1]–[5]. Subsequent research has focused on ranking algorithms that promote fair exposure for individual items or for sensitive groups of items [6]–[11]. These approaches often aim to mitigate winner‑take‑all dynamics, address popularity bias [12], or support smaller producers [13], [14]. Most of the work in this line adopts a notion of fairness that is oriented solely toward items.

Efforts toward two‑sided fairness have explored user‑side fairness by employing concave functions of user utilities [15] and by imposing equality constraints on user outcomes [16], [17]. Such methods typically build on the item‑side fairness definitions described in earlier studies. Envy‑free rankings for users, combined with guarantees of a fair minimum share for items, have also been proposed [18]; however, this line of work does not readily support controllable trade‑offs between user and item utilities.

In the reciprocal recommendation setting, fewer studies have examined ranking fairness. One line of work seeks to equalize user utility across groups, though it encounters challenges identified in later analyses [19]. Another approach generates rankings using a welfare‑function perspective but optimizes only the utility of users being recommended [20]. Post‑processing techniques have been introduced to correct inconsistencies between estimated and declared user preferences [21]. Related research in ridesharing applications addresses fairness in matching rather than ranking [22]–[24].

A growing body of literature connects fairness in machine learning with concepts from social choice theory [25]–[32] and, more specifically, with welfare economics [33]–[37]. Within this context, parity‑penalty methods for classification have been linked to fairness assessments that incorporate Pareto‑efficiency [34]. The use of concave welfare functions and Lorenz dominance has been advocated as a means to capture two‑sided fairness considerations in recommendation scenarios.

[ANSWER]: GFlowNets applied to tree‑structured directed acyclic graphs (i.e., autoregressive generation) can be viewed as reinforcement‑learning (RL) methods equipped with suitable entropy regularization, soft Q‑learning, or control‑as‑inference formulations [1–3]. Empirical investigations have highlighted that these approaches may break down when extended to more general DAGs, a setting for which GFlowNets were explicitly designed [4]. The mechanism by which trajectory‑balance propagates training signals across sequences of actions bears resemblance to loss constructions in RL that operate on sub‑trajectories [5].

Connections between GFlowNets and Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) techniques have also been explored, particularly in the context of sampling from unnormalized densities. Prior work has sought to accelerate or partially amortize such sampling in discrete spaces where exact sampling is infeasible [6,7]; some of these efforts are tailored to specific domains or problems [8]. In contrast, GFlowNets address the compositional structure of data as a learning problem rather than embedding it as a bias within the sampler, shifting the computational burden to the learning phase instead of to search at generation time.

[ANSWER]: The method most closely related to the approach of learning perceptual grouping in an unsupervised fashion is Tagger [1], which employs multiple copies of a neural network that cooperate to reconstruct different parts of the input. In Tagger, these copies also learn to output the grouping, providing direct control over segmentation and enabling application to complex texture segmentation tasks. A recurrent extension of Tagger, called RTagger [2], adds recurrent connections to support sequential data.  

The binding problem was first examined in neuroscience [3], [4] and motivated early work on oscillatory neural networks that use synchronization as a grouping mechanism [5]–[7]. Subsequent research replaced explicit oscillation simulation with complex‑valued activations [8], [9]. Although any recurrent neural network can in principle learn a suitable grouping mechanism, practical implementations often require additional structures such as competition [10], fast weights [11], or perceptual grouping strategies.  

Unsupervised segmentation has been investigated across a range of domains, from random vectors [13] and texture segmentation [14] to natural images [15], [16]. Early unsupervised video segmentation methods used generalized Expectation‑Maximization (EM) to infer how to split frames of moving sprites [17]. More recent approaches leverage optical flow to train convolutional networks for figure‑ground segmentation [18], [19]. Multi‑causal modelling formalizes perceptual grouping as inference in a generative compositional model of images [20], and Masked Restricted Boltzmann Machines extend RBMs with a latent mask inferred via Block‑Gibbs sampling [21].  

Gradient backpropagation through inference updates has been explored in the context of sparse coding with (Fast) Iterative Shrinkage/Thresholding Algorithms ((F)ISTA) [22]–[24]. In these works, the unrolled graph of a fixed number of ISTA iterations is replaced by a recurrent neural network that parametrizes gradient computations and is trained to predict sparse codes directly [25]. Similar derivations from neural EM frameworks have yielded trainable procedures that embed the structure of iterative pursuit into the architecture while retaining tunable degrees of freedom for enhanced modeling capabilities [26]. An alternative strategy of untying weights across iterations has been investigated to further empower such networks [27].

[ANSWER]: Several studies have highlighted shortcomings in the evaluation of graph neural network (GNN) methods on static graphs. Dwivedi et al. demonstrated that inconsistent experimental settings lead to unreliable comparative results, while Shchur et al. showed that the widespread reuse of identical train‑test splits can cause overfitting and that alternative splits may substantially alter method rankings. The Open Graph Benchmark (OGB) responded to these concerns by providing a diversified collection of datasets together with unified protocols, metrics, and data splits to promote reproducibility and fair comparison.  

Research on dynamic graphs has emphasized the distinction between static and temporal link prediction. Early work by Junuthula et al. introduced the notion of predicting edge insertions and deletions over time, and subsequently explored how friendship information from social interaction networks can be leveraged for future link prediction. Haghani and Keyvanpour offered a comprehensive review of link‑prediction techniques for social networks, categorizing the task into missing‑link and future‑link prediction. These contributions collectively underscore the unique challenges posed by temporal edge dynamics.  

The importance of negative‑edge sampling has been recognized across graph representation learning. Yang et al. argued that negative sampling (NS) is as critical as positive sampling for learning robust embeddings. In static link prediction, common strategies include uniform random sampling, degree‑aware sampling, and distance‑based sampling. Kotnis and Nastase provided an empirical analysis of how different NS schemes affect the quality of learned representations in knowledge‑graph settings.  

Dynamic graph representation learning has experienced rapid growth, with surveys by Kazemi et al. and Skardinga et al. cataloguing recent advances in temporal network modeling. Both surveys note that continuous‑time formulations afford finer temporal granularity and have motivated a series of dynamic graph neural networks, such as JODIE, DyRep, TGAT, TGN, and CAWN. These models have reported high accuracy on existing dynamic link‑prediction benchmarks, yet their near‑perfect scores raise questions about the discriminative power of current evaluation practices.  

In light of these observations, the present work introduces a suite of contributions aimed at strengthening the empirical study of dynamic link prediction. First, it proposes two novel negative‑sampling strategies—historical and inductive—that generate harder negative edges based on the recurrence patterns of observed links, thereby offering a more rigorous test of model generalization. Second, it presents EdgeBank, a non‑parameterized memorization baseline that stores previously seen edges and predicts them at test time, establishing a strong reference point that requires no learning or hyper‑parameter tuning. Third, it expands the benchmark landscape by curating six dynamic graph datasets from diverse domains—including politics, economics, and transportation—addressing the limited domain diversity of prior collections. Fourth, it introduces visualization tools that expose temporal edge‑recurrence patterns, revealing that many edges reappear across time steps while the specific recurrence dynamics vary by domain. Together, these contributions provide more robust evaluation protocols, richer baselines, and broader datasets, enabling clearer differentiation of future dynamic graph representation methods.

[ANSWER]: Generalization under distribution shift remains a central challenge in supervised learning. When the training and test distributions differ, models trained on a single source often fail to transfer, and robustness obtained by adapting to one type of shift (e.g., Gaussian noise) does not reliably extend to other shifts (e.g., salt‑and‑pepper noise) even when the visual appearance is similar. A common strategy to mitigate this issue is to broaden the training distribution through larger datasets or extensive data augmentation, with the hope that the unseen test distribution is covered implicitly.

A related, but more restrictive, line of work assumes access to unlabeled data drawn from the target distribution in addition to the labeled source data. By leveraging these unlabeled target samples, methods can align source and target representations or fine‑tune models to the specific shift. While this approach reduces the gap between source and target, it requires prior knowledge of the test distribution and limits applicability to scenarios where such data can be collected in advance.

The concept of learning from unlabeled test data dates back to transductive learning, where the goal is to predict the specific test instances at hand rather than a generic decision rule. Early transductive methods, especially those built on support‑vector machines, incorporated test examples as constraints on the margin of the classifier. Local learning approaches extended this idea by constructing a dedicated model for each test input based on its nearest neighbors, effectively performing instance‑wise adaptation.

In computer‑vision practice, several works have demonstrated the benefits of continuing training during deployment. Examples include leveraging easy faces within an image to improve detection of harder faces, fine‑tuning a generative model on a few images of a specific individual, training super‑resolution networks from scratch on each test image, and employing online student‑teacher learning for video segmentation. These studies illustrate a broader trend toward adaptive inference in visual tasks.

Test‑time training (TTT) formalized the notion of instance‑wise adaptation through self‑supervision. The original TTT framework generates a distinct model for every test sample by optimizing a self‑supervised objective on that sample before performing the primary prediction. This paradigm has been extended to diverse domains, such as vision‑based reinforcement learning, legged locomotion, video object tracking, natural‑language question answering, and medical imaging, each time employing a domain‑specific self‑supervised task.

The self‑supervised pretext task originally used in TTT is rotation prediction, where an image is rotated by multiples of 90° and the model must classify the rotation angle. Although simple to implement, rotation prediction can be either too trivial (e.g., detecting horizon orientation in outdoor scenes) or too ambiguous (e.g., for top‑down views where all orientations appear similar), limiting its generality across varied visual domains.

Viewing TTT as a form of one‑sample unsupervised domain adaptation highlights its distinction from conventional UDA, which typically relies on a batch of unlabeled target samples. By operating on a single test instance, TTT removes the need for prior collection of target data and reframes adaptation as an extreme form of over‑fitting that is permissible because the adapted model is evaluated on the same sample used for adaptation. Subsequent works have explored related settings that assume access to a batch or entire dataset of test inputs, such as TTT++ and TENT, but these methods retain the requirement of multiple target samples during inference.

Autoencoders have long been employed for unsupervised representation learning, with early approaches like context encoders predicting missing image regions from surrounding context. Recent advances integrate spatial autoencoding with Vision Transformers, culminating in masked autoencoders (MAE). MAE randomly masks a large proportion of image patches and trains a model to reconstruct the missing content, typically using a large encoder and a lightweight decoder. The encoder can then serve as a feature extractor for downstream tasks, either via fine‑tuning of the whole network or linear probing of a frozen encoder followed by training a task‑specific head.

In this work we introduce a test‑time training approach that leverages masked autoencoders as the self‑supervised objective for one‑sample adaptation. By employing a reconstruction‑based pretext task that balances difficulty across diverse visual domains, the method achieves consistent improvements on multiple object‑recognition benchmarks under distribution shift. We provide a bias‑variance analysis that characterizes the theoretical benefit of the proposed adaptation scheme, and we demonstrate empirically that the approach outperforms prior TTT variants, including rotation‑based TTT, TTT++, and batch‑oriented methods such as TENT, while requiring only a single test instance and no additional target data.

[ANSWER]: Diffusion probabilistic models (DPMs) [1] can be interpreted as a class of variational autoencoders (VAEs) [2, 3] whose architecture and loss formulation enable efficient training of arbitrarily deep networks. The recent resurgence of interest in diffusion models stems from their strong performance on image‑generation benchmarks [4, 5].

[4] introduced several architectural and algorithmic innovations to the original DPM framework, achieving notable improvements on standard image‑generation quality metrics. They showed that, for a diffusion model with discrete time and diffusion variances shared across input dimensions, the variational lower‑bound (VLB) objective is equivalent to multi‑scale denoising score matching up to specific weightings per noise scale. Subsequent work by [6] built on these ideas and obtained higher log‑likelihood scores through further refinements.

The concept of learning generative models via a multi‑scale denoising score‑matching objective was first proposed by [7], with methodological enhancements presented in [5]. This line of research was later extended to continuous‑time diffusion processes, accompanied by novel sampling algorithms that reverse the diffusion dynamics [8].

Several studies have derived variational lower bounds for the data likelihood under continuous‑time diffusion models. In particular, [9] and [11] provided derivations based on stochastic differential equations, while [10] examined both stochastic‑differential‑equation and VAE perspectives and discussed their connections. Earlier diffusion‑probabilistic approaches typically fixed the diffusion process and parameterized the individual diffusion steps [⋯]; later work explored direct parameterization of the mean and variance of the marginal distributions [⋯]. Architectural variations have also been explored, including the incorporation of Fourier features to improve model expressiveness and likelihood performance [⋯].

[ANSWER]: Convex neural networks were first introduced as an alternative to the traditional non‑convex training of feed‑forward models, showing that certain architectures admit an exact convex reformulation of the learning problem. Early works established convex formulations for two‑layer ReLU networks by lifting the parameters into a higher‑dimensional space, thereby preserving the expressive power while enabling global optimization. Subsequent studies extended these ideas to broader classes of networks, including deeper architectures and variants with structured regularization. A subset of the literature focused specifically on scalar‑output networks, providing convex duality frameworks for fully‑connected two‑layer ReLU models and for convolutional networks that incorporate average pooling. These approaches demonstrate that the non‑convex objective can be expressed as a convex program, but they are limited to settings where the network produces a single scalar or a low‑dimensional output. Other contributions explored architectural modifications that make inference convex, yet they still rely on non‑convex training procedures to obtain the network parameters.

In parallel, deep learning has become a dominant tool for solving inverse problems, where the goal is to recover an unknown signal from indirect or noisy measurements. Two main paradigms have emerged: (i) learning expressive priors that are embedded within iterative reconstruction algorithms, and (ii) directly learning a mapping from measurements to reconstructions using feed‑forward networks. The first paradigm often employs latent codes or deep decoder networks that are optimized jointly with the forward model, and several works have attempted to analyze the theoretical underpinnings of such approaches. The second paradigm, which is especially attractive for real‑time applications such as magnetic resonance imaging, trains convolutional neural networks to de‑alias or denoise an initial linear estimate obtained from the forward acquisition model. Numerous studies have demonstrated that these feed‑forward models can substantially outperform classical sparsity‑inducing regularizers such as total variation or wavelet penalties in terms of image quality and computational speed. Building on this success, a line of research has proposed unrolled architectures that mimic the steps of convex optimization algorithms, aiming to combine the interpretability of iterative methods with the efficiency of learned models. Recent investigations have also examined the stability of neural reconstruction pipelines, providing robustness guarantees for certain iterative designs.

While the convex reformulations for scalar‑output networks and the convex‑inference architectures that still require non‑convex training have broadened our understanding of neural optimization, they do not directly address the challenges posed by fully convolutional denoising networks whose output dimension matches the number of image pixels and where all pixels share a common hidden representation. Likewise, most deep‑learning‑based inverse‑problem methods focus on empirical performance or on unrolled iterative schemes, leaving the interpretability of supervised, feed‑forward denoisers largely unexplored. Moreover, existing stability analyses concentrate on iterative or unrolled designs rather than on end‑to‑end trained convolutional networks.

In this work we bridge these gaps by presenting a convex‑duality formulation for a two‑layer fully‑convolutional ReLU denoising network that operates on image‑wide outputs. The resulting convex program has polynomial complexity in the number of training samples and hidden units, and it is provably equivalent to the original non‑convex network, exhibiting a zero duality gap. This equivalence enables the use of off‑the‑shelf convex solvers to obtain globally optimal parameters, providing robustness guarantees for reconstruction. Furthermore, the convex dual reveals that weight‑decay regularization in the original network corresponds to an implicit path‑sparsity regularizer, offering a principled capacity control mechanism. At inference time, the dual representation can be interpreted as first clustering image patches according to their latent codes and then applying a linear filter within each cluster, thereby furnishing a transparent, piecewise‑linear description of the denoising operation. Extensive experiments on MNIST and fastMRI datasets confirm that the convex solution matches the performance of the trained non‑convex network and illustrate the interpretability of the learned dual model, positioning this approach as a tractable and explainable alternative to existing deep learning methods for inverse problems.

[ANSWER]: Addressing heterophily in graph neural networks has motivated a variety of architectural and methodological advances. MixHop [1] tackles weak homophily by extracting features from multi‑hop neighborhoods, thereby enriching node representations with broader context. To guide GNNs on heterophilic graphs, measurements based on feature smoothness and label smoothness are proposed in [2], offering diagnostic tools for assessing graph heterophily. Geom‑GCN [3] precomputes unsupervised node embeddings and constructs a geometric graph in the embedding space, enabling a bi‑level aggregation process that better captures non‑homophilous relationships. H‑GCN [4] integrates three key designs: separation of ego and neighbor embeddings, incorporation of higher‑order neighborhoods, and combination of intermediate representations, all aimed at improving performance on heterophilic data. CPGNN [5] introduces a compatibility matrix to model label correlations, propagating prior belief estimations through the network and proving beneficial for graphs with dissimilar neighboring labels. FAGCN [6] extends the attention mechanism of GAT [7] by learning edge‑level aggregation weights that can be negative, allowing the model to capture high‑frequency components of graph signals. GPRGNN [8] similarly employs learnable propagation weights of both signs, adapting to heterophilic structures and handling both high‑ and low‑frequency signal parts. BernNet [9] designs a framework for learning arbitrary spectral filters using Bernstein polynomials, explicitly targeting heterophily through flexible frequency response. Finally, [10] argues that homophily is not a prerequisite for effective GNNs and characterizes conditions under which GNNs can succeed on heterophilic graphs, providing a theoretical perspective on the limits of traditional homophily‑based assumptions.

[ANSWER]: Multilingual sequence‑to‑sequence models frequently encode the target language with a dedicated token appended to the input sequence, thereby conditioning the decoder on a language identifier while sharing the bulk of the network parameters across languages. Early work on neural machine translation introduced this technique to enable a single model to produce output in multiple languages by prepending a language symbol to the source (e.g., Johnson et al., 2017). Subsequent studies extended the idea to accommodate code‑switching and mixed‑language inputs, allowing the decoder to emit tokens from any of the supported vocabularies rather than imposing a hard constraint on the output language (e.g., Bapna & Firat, 2019). These approaches have shown that flexible language flags can improve modeling of multilingual data, particularly in settings where speakers naturally interleave languages.

A substantial body of research has explored shared‑parameter architectures for cross‑lingual representation learning. Multitask learning frameworks that train on multilingual document‑classification tasks have been employed to induce bilingual word embeddings, with linear vector‑analogy operations subsequently revealing accurate lexical translations (e.g., Conneau et al., 2017; Artetxe & Schwenk, 2019). Many of these methods rely on parallel corpora or explicit translation prediction as auxiliary objectives. In contrast, recent work has demonstrated that grounding language in non‑linguistic modalities—such as images, video, or sensor data—can yield cross‑lingual semantic alignments without parallel text (e.g., Lazaridou et al., 2015; Kiela et al., 2020). By leveraging shared visual or perceptual contexts, models are able to discover correspondences between languages through the structure of the external world.

The use of language‑control symbols has also been incorporated into a variety of multimodal generation tasks beyond translation. Neural models for image captioning, speech recognition, and syntactic parsing have adopted a single multitask architecture in which a language flag determines the output modality (e.g., Aharoni & Goldberg, 2020). These systems illustrate the practicality of unified models that can switch between tasks and languages via a minimal conditioning signal. More broadly, grounding language generation in perceptual inputs—ranging from static images to interactive simulated environments—has been proposed as a way to reduce dependence on large parallel corpora, especially for low‑resource languages (e.g., Anderson et al., 2018). Researchers have advocated for evaluating multilingual and multitask models on tasks that require reference to the immediate context, arguing that such benchmarks better capture the interplay between semantic understanding and pragmatic language use.

In this work we construct a bilingual speaker model for a grounded color‑reference game by augmenting the encoder input with a language‑identifying token while sharing hidden representations across English and Mandarin Chinese. The model is trained on parallel data in the two languages but is allowed to emit tokens from either vocabulary at any decoding step, enabling naturalistic code‑switching behavior that is not constrained by conventional multitask setups. Unlike prior approaches that depend on parallel text for cross‑lingual alignment, our model derives bilingual semantic relationships solely from the shared perceptual context of the color game, allowing it to infer synonymy between English and Chinese color terms via vector analogies in the output layer. We evaluate the system using a pragmatic informativeness metric that measures how effectively generated utterances guide a listener to the target color, and we compare its performance to monolingual baselines. Analyses reveal that the bilingual training improves pragmatic adaptation to contextual difficulty, preserves language‑specific color term systems, and introduces only modest cross‑lingual influences on lexical semantics. Together, these findings demonstrate that multilingual grounding can enhance both the pragmatic and semantic aspects of language generation without relying on explicit translation supervision.

[ANSWER]: Confidence estimation has attracted extensive attention because it enables reliable failure prediction and out‑of‑distribution detection in deployed systems. Early work on calibration focused on classification models, where techniques such as temperature scaling and Dirichlet‑based methods have become standard. In the context of neural machine translation, only a handful of studies have examined calibration. Prior analyses reported that NMT models appear well‑calibrated during training but become severely miscalibrated at inference time, especially for decisions such as predicting sentence termination. These works, however, stop at diagnosing the problem and do not provide a practical solution that can be integrated into modern Transformer‑based translators. In contrast, our approach introduces a learned confidence estimator that is trained jointly with the translation model, requires no additional supervision, and can be incorporated into any standard NMT architecture without degrading translation quality. By interpreting confidence as the number of “hints” the model requests, we obtain a direct, interpretable signal that is tightly coupled to the model’s own decision process.

Monte‑Carlo dropout and other Bayesian approximations have been proposed to capture predictive uncertainty in NMT. While effective at revealing model variance, these methods incur substantial computational overhead because they require multiple forward passes at test time and are sensitive to the choice of dropout rate. Our method sidesteps these limitations by learning a single‑shot confidence score from the decoder’s hidden states. This score can be computed with the same cost as a standard forward pass, making it suitable for large‑scale or latency‑sensitive translation services. Empirically, the confidence estimates produced by our model outperform dropout‑based uncertainty measures on both sentence‑level and word‑level quality estimation benchmarks.

Supervised quality estimation (QE) models aim to predict translation quality without references, but they typically rely on large amounts of manually annotated quality scores, which are expensive to collect and often unavailable for low‑resource language pairs. Consequently, supervised QE remains impractical for many real‑world deployments. Our work contributes an unsupervised alternative that leverages internal signals of the NMT system itself. By using the learned confidence estimator as an intrinsic quality proxy, we achieve higher correlation with human judgments than existing unsupervised QE metrics, while avoiding the need for any external annotations or repeated stochastic sampling.

Beyond providing a reliable confidence metric, we exploit the estimated confidence to guide instance‑specific label smoothing during training. Existing label‑smoothing techniques apply a uniform smoothing factor across all training examples, ignoring the varying difficulty of individual sentences. By scaling the smoothing mass according to the model’s confidence, our confidence‑based label smoothing adapts to the intrinsic uncertainty of each instance and consistently yields better translation performance than the standard uniform approach across multiple benchmark datasets (e.g., WMT14 En‑De, NIST Zh‑En, WMT16 Ro‑En, IWSLT14 De‑En). This demonstrates that our confidence estimator not only serves as a diagnostic tool but also enhances the core training objective of NMT systems.

[QUERY]: Your task is to write related work section that contributions and/or positioning of the paper is provided in the final paragraph as a summary.


[CRITERIA]: Each paragraph (except final paragraph) should focus on summarizing and comparing prior research etc., without mentioning the current paper’s contributions or its position in the literature. The final paragraph should address aspects mentioned in previous paragraphs. Scoring rubric is as follows:
0: The answer provide contributions in each paragraph or final paragraph fails to address the points in previous paragraphs or do not mention contributions-positioning at all.
1: Only the final paragraph emphasizes the contributions-positioning by addressing the points in previous paragraphs.

<START OF EXAMPLE 1>

ANSWER: Previous studies on TDMR (Task-Dataset-Metric-Result) extraction primarily rely on data sources such as NLP-progress or PapersWithCode. While these sources are popular, they lack rigorous quality control, including standardized TDM entity representation and complete publication coverage. Some efforts, such as [1] and [2], go further by extracting TDM triples and result values, and normalizing them for leaderboard construction. However, these approaches are constrained by their reliance on a closed domain, requiring extracted triples to match a pre-defined TDM taxonomy. Other studies, such as [3] and [4], only extract TDM triples without result values, thereby forgoing leaderboard construction. Meanwhile, [6] focuses on extracting result values, but only in the context of pre-defined TDM triples, making it similar in limitation to [1]. Consequently, these methods are not adaptable to the dynamic nature of emerging benchmarks, where new tasks, datasets, and metrics are frequently introduced.
In a parallel research thread, scientific leaderboards have been treated as a form of scientific knowledge graphs composed of Task, Dataset, and Metric entities. Information extraction in this context has been widely studied, as seen in works such as [5, 7, 8, 9, 10]. These studies typically focus on linking scientific content to structured entities but often assume that the schema or triples are known in advance.
In contrast to prior work, our approach addresses several critical limitations. We construct our dataset manually from publications to ensure comprehensive TDMR annotations, rather than relying on incomplete or unstandardized third-party sources. We normalize TDMR tuples for accurate leaderboard construction and propose novel experimental settings that reflect realistic, dynamic scenarios, including cold-start conditions without any pre-defined TDM taxonomy. By simultaneously constructing a scientific knowledge graph and linking publications to it in an open-domain setting, our method is inherently adaptable to the evolving research landscape.

EVALUATION:

The contributions are clearly summarized and detailed only in the final paragraph. Therefore, the evaluation score should be 1.</reasoning>

ANSWER: Several efforts have been made to automate the extraction and organization of performance results from scientific publications. Early work by [1] introduced a framework, TDMS-IE, for identifying task, dataset, metric, and score tuples from NLP papers, establishing a foundation for automatic leaderboard construction. Similarly, AxCell [2] proposes a robust pipeline that utilizes structural analysis and novel table segmentation techniques to extract results from machine learning papers, demonstrating significant improvements over prior methods. ORKG-Leaderboards [3] offers a systematic approach that integrates leaderboard extraction into a knowledge graph framework, enabling machine-actionable publishing and dynamic visualization of state-of-the-art performance. TELIN [4] focuses on extracting leaderboard-relevant entities from PDFs using a semi-automated approach that reduces human annotation needs through targeted entity refinement.
In addition to these extraction systems, several datasets and benchmarks have been introduced to facilitate the development and evaluation of leaderboard construction tools. LEGOBench [5] provides a large-scale benchmark derived from arXiv and PapersWithCode, and evaluates both language model-based and graph-based approaches. SciERC and its associated framework SciIE [6] support multi-task extraction of entities and relations, enabling construction of scientific knowledge graphs. SciREX [7] extends information extraction to the document level, capturing relationships that span across sections, which is critical for leaderboard generation from full papers.
Other contributions include TDMSci [8], a specialized corpus annotated with task, dataset, and metric entities, which supports the development of more accurate extraction models. SciNLP-KG [9] introduces methods for extracting entity relations from NLP literature to build a knowledge graph, highlighting its potential use in leaderboard automation. Additionally, a diachronic analysis of NLP research trends by [10] shows the evolving influence of tasks, methods, and datasets, providing a broader context for understanding the dynamic nature of scientific benchmarks.
